Chapter IX THE PURPOSE OF THE STATE
§1. Our Reformed Confession. From the Calvinist side, there was no doubt in the days of the Reformation about the purpose for which the State was instituted. Our Confession of Faith also clearly expressed this in Article XXXVII: “We believe that our good God, out of care for our unruly behavior, has appointed kings, princes, and magistrates; willing that the world should be governed by laws and policies; that the unbridled passions of men might be restrained; and all things may be conducted in good order among human beings.” Where then a further explanation is added: “Moreover, everyone, of whatever status, condition or rank he may be, is bound to subject himself to the governing authorities, to pay tribute, and to obey them in all things that are not in conflict with God’s Word; to pray for them in their prayers, that the Lord may be their support and strengthen them in all their ways, and that we may lead a quiet and peaceful life in all godliness and propriety.” On this standpoint, which in a strict sense is to be called Scriptural, the definition of “living in the State” presented no difficulty. The power of the authorities was on this standpoint a corrective that could not be missed, as soon as it became apparent that the genealogical coherence of our race was no longer able to give expression to this coherence in a patriarchal way. For the unity of our race, the multiplicity of peoples took the place, and in order to ensure this unity in each people by establishing a firm bond, the authorities were summoned, and as long as the unity of the people was firmly riveted in the authority of the government, through the form of the State. The State lay entirely outside the original order of creation. Even if the Fall had not occurred, no State would ever have been formed, and no authorities would ever have appeared. Authorities and State are abnormal phenomena, which with a surgical and medical purpose tear out of its context

—- Page 307 —-

TWO PERSPECTIVES
The two parts of our human race, as they appeared as peoples, were necessarily constituted in this way. But just as clear as this connection between sin and the fall with government and the state, it is equally easy to understand that one who does not reckon with these givens in his state’s purpose must be perplexed when the question of the purpose of the state and thus also of the government arises. For then it is not to be seen why our race had to be divided into peoples, nor also why a power was to be set over these peoples that could maintain good order, when after all, without sin, the natural order would have been sufficient by itself.

§2. Two Perspectives
Yet one could not escape answering the question of the purpose of the state, and so it was natural that two different opinions soon stood opposed to each other on this point. One had to deal with a people, that is, with a group of people, and in each of those people with a separate person. The state could therefore be conceived either as an institution to serve the separate individuals as private persons, or to bind the collective persons as a people. The latter began, for it was all about preventing the scattering of the whole and moving the individuals to make themselves and their households subservient to the preservation and well-being of the state. In fact, this happened through the overwhelming power of the rulers, who by force subjected the interests of the individuals to the interest of the common good (and with this was often meant the personal interest of the ruler). In the further development, on the other hand, as this took place especially in the Greek world, one was intent on replacing the rule of force with the rule of the idea, so that not the despot, but the philosopher, and especially Aristotle, so quickly put the state idea to the fore, that the individuals let themselves be treated as subordinate order quite voluntarily. The state thus imposed itself on the popular consciousness as the proper matter, the only valid purpose for which the citizens received their existence. To sacrifice oneself for the state was the honor of men. Even the recognition ultimately emerged that the state had existed first, and that the citizens had only appeared to give content to the state.

—- Page 308 —-

292 THE PURPOSE OF THE STATE
Now that the largest part of the population of a city like Athens consisted of slaves, so that not only what we call the working class, but also the entire craft and shop class stood outside the real citizenry, and these therefore reduced themselves to a relatively small number of persons who alone had the right to have a say, it is understandable that the full citizens considered themselves to be lords and masters, and at the same time invested with power over the great multitude, felt no need to stand up for their personal freedom against the authority. The state, that was them, and the rule of the state was built on their own greatness. It is therefore perfectly understandable how all monarchical lust for power, all absolutism and tyranny have always appealed to this ancient state law that arose in the school of Greek philosophers, and that an entirely different world of thought had to master the spirits, if the solemn anchor of freedom was to be lowered into the waves. However, one unfortunately fell into a troublesome exaggeration, and especially after 1789, and as a fruit of the spirit of the time that arose from the Revolution of that year, an equally untenable notion has now arisen, as if the individual person, the individual, was the main thing, and as if the purpose of the state could not be developed more richly than by seeking it in the promotion of the happiness of the individual citizen. The public matter had to serve the private interest. Already Bacon of Verulam gave the first impetus to this line of thought, and whenever one turned in England, under the leadership of Edmund Burke, initially against the Paris turmoil, yet this view so much suited the English character, which in “my home is my castle” places the individual decisively in the foreground, that not only the school of Smith and Cobden in the economic field, but even a man like Macaulay in the political field, emphasized this seeking of the purpose of the state in the promotion of the private interests of the individual citizens far too strongly.

—- Page 309 —-

GERMAN PHILOSOPHY
§3. The German Philosophy. First, German philosophy has sought to eliminate the antithesis between the general and the particular interest by bringing the idea of law to the forefront. This already came from Kant, who in his Rechtslehre §47 ff. advocated the position that the goal of the state was not so much the well-being and happiness of the citizens, but rather the realization of a condition in which complete agreement was achieved between the constitution and the abstract principles of law, “Not the well-being of the citizens and their happiness, but the state of agreement of the constitution with the principles of law is the well-being of the state.” Kant found support for this in Fichte, and even Eötvös, in his Modern Ideas II p. 91, knew no other state purpose than the secure legal condition of the individual citizens. “The purpose of the state,” as he expressed it, “is the security of the individuals.” The police state had offended and oppressed the citizens, now the state had to become a state of law, and as such set itself the goal of bringing into being a condition that corresponded to the highest demands of law, and as such guaranteed the highest right to the citizens. The state became a state of law. Yet there could be no question that in this formula: “agreement of the rules of political life with the philosophically developed ideas of law,” the action of the government would be absorbed. Consistently, this view would have led to the abolition of all our departments of general administration and the retention of only those of Foreign Affairs, Justice and Finance. The affairs of state cannot be concentrated in legal formation, legal proceedings and law enforcement. Alongside the administration of justice, a very extensive task is assigned to every government in what concerns the soil and traffic, in what concerns the education and formation of citizens, in what concerns trade and industry, and no less in what concerns agriculture, insofar as all these interests can only find their required care under the cooperation of many and under fixed regulation. Woe to the state that wants to draw all these interests under its monopoly, but conversely, social life must fade and wither away if the government, where the help and guidance of political power is necessary, deliberately and on the basis of a false theory, withdraws this from the life of the citizenry. Even science and art cannot always do without this support and help. Thorbecke may have decreed as the personification of the state of law that art is not a state affair, yet the younger artistic life among us shows how only after the fall of Thorbecke’s verdict did the artistic bloom unfold, and how greatly his statement had sinned through exaggeration and one-sidedness. What we now regard among us as culture must also not be neglected by the government. Here too there are very certain limits that the government must scrupulously respect. Sphere Sovereignty also asserts its strict demands here, but whoever has to watch over what touches on everything—and so it is with the government—can never say of any domain that it is indifferent to him, and therefore his only task is to see in absolute abstinence. There are personal, there are corporate, but there are also common and economic interests, cultural interests and ideal interests, and although it does not belong to the state, and thus also to the government, to intrude here, yet where its help is invoked, that help may not be refused by the government either. What can thrive and bloom of itself, the government must not disturb in its independent development, but what would perish without its help and support, it must help to continue to exist and to bloom again where it has withered. Necessity may demand this to the most intimate degree. From religion, the government keeps its coercive hand as far as it can, but even in the religious domain, situations may arise that never demand guidance, but then still demand support from the government, and a government that in case of need would fall short in fulfilling this always extremely delicate task, would prove to be unequal to the burden of its calling.

—- Page 310 —-

§4. The state of law is insufficient. Hegel had already seen that with the formal idea of law of Kant and Fichte one could not get out, and therefore gave preference to seeking the purpose of the state in what he calls “the morality,”

—- Page 311 —-

THE STATE IS INSUFFICIENT. 295 By this he means that the people will be elevated to a higher ethical level through the state organization, and that both the state and the people will become the embodiment of the ethical principle. As one wishes, it returns to the Platonic representation. Schelling renewed the confession that the state must also be a means to the self-glorification of a personal God, a fundamental thought that also inspired Stahl. Robert von Mohl, in his: Encyclopaedia of Political Science, was the first to go back to the innate nature of man in general and of each people in particular, in order to derive from this the tasks and thus also the purpose of the state, that it was up to the state to create the best possible opportunity for this general and particular endowment to come to full development. He even declares on p. 74: “It must be recognized that everything that develops consistently from human nature is also justified,” while with regard to foreign countries on p. 75 he testifies: “In this respect, all the purposes of the people and thus of the state are permitted, which do not immediately prejudice the existence, security and pursuit of the own life purposes of other states.” Dr. H. Ahrens in his Organic Theory of the State, Vienna 1850, awards the palm of honor to Krause’s philosophy on p. 92 and tries to shed further light on his conception of the state in an organic sense, but he himself recognizes that he has not yet reached it and then says: “We expect the recognition of the true purpose of the state from the time when the one-sided, dissolving and disintegrating or retrogressive doctrines are eliminated by the higher educational instinct of the life of the people and the state,” a testimonium paupertatis that shows how close Ahrens has come to the only correct standpoint, but without having reached it. Dr. Jul. Hatschek, General State Law I p. 20, starts from the “general state law based on legal comparison,” so that the purpose for the particular is indicated in the general as a matter of course. Conrad Bornhak in his General Theory of the State, Berlin 1909, p. 26, starts from the contrast between the absolute state of the Greeks and the individualistic state of Kant and von Humboldt, and comes to the realization that “just as man is the highest purpose on earth, so the state also exists for the sake of man.” The state, he says, is not “an end in itself, but serves man.”

—- Page 312 —-

THE STATE’S PURPOSE The Ancients. Bluntschli, in his “Allgemeine Staatslehre” 6th edition, Stuttgart, 1886, was primarily concerned with specifying the purpose of the State. He distinguishes (p. 363) “World Powers” from “Great Powers” and then again “Middle Powers” from “Small States,” but finds the purpose of all States to be (see p. 367) ultimately in what is the life-task of the individual person, i.e., in the unfolding of individuality, in the development of one’s talents, in the manifestation of each one’s peculiarities, and in the harmony of life’s purpose for one’s own family, nation, and humanity. Just as the individual stands over against the individual, so too, according to him, does the nation stand over against the nation. In fact, with Bluntschli too, it all comes down to the fullest possible unfolding of the nation’s powers in accordance with the nation’s character. And now, where the individual can be exposed to “unlawful attacks,” there it can only be the task of the State “to protect the private freedom of the individual,” while conversely the State never has the right to disturb or suppress the talents of the individual. Fundamentally different from this view is what Stahl, in his “Staatslehre,” Berlin 1910, p. 28, attests: “The purpose of the State does not consist merely in making human conditions more perfect, but above all in the maintenance of God’s commandments. Not in the free pursuit of advantages or privileges, but in submitting to inviolable ordinances. Just as in the concept of the State, the Authority forms the highest moment, so too in the concept of Authority, this maintenance of God’s commandments is the highest moment.” One senses how, even in this definition of the State’s purpose, the “formal-legal” is still conceived of too one-sidedly, while the Tenth Commandment: “Thou shalt not covet” already shows in itself how questionable it is to take the Ten Commandments as the guiding principle here. In Stahl, the one-sided formal jurist who is blind to the practical demands of life no longer speaks, but even his view still hung too much in the philosophical and abstract. Stahl too did not fully do justice to the connection between the State and the fall in paradise. It always appears that the writers on general State theory were partly governed by the prevailing ideas of their time. Did Bluntschli (see p. 364) not point out, in discussing the Small States, that in our time they have at best a doubtful right to exist, and that they...

—- Page 313 —-

Can they only secure a future for themselves by placing themselves under the protection of a great power? Something that almost sounds like an invitation directed at the Netherlands as well, to seek shelter in the German Empire as a federation.

§5. The City of God. However, one does not arrive at clarity with all these proposals. When Pölitz in his Handbook of Philosophy, second ed., II, says that the purpose of the State lies in “the freest possible approximation of all its citizens to the ultimate purpose of humanity under the unconditional rule of law,” (see Ahrens, Organic Theory of the State, p. 104) then the ultimate purpose of our human race is subordinated to the purpose of the State, and the law becomes little more than an appendage, since from this standpoint the law itself has also become a very elastic and pliable magnitude. One must, however, take refuge in such definitions as soon as one no longer takes the State as a corrective, but as the highest good in our human community. In general, however, one does not get any further with such analyses than that one passes from the State to humanity, in order to indicate in general that in human nature and in our human race the germ has been sown from the beginning, which, once developed, would give an ever richer picture of what was intended in organic connection with the creation of humanity; and the only further definition is then borrowed from the rule of law, something which will then always aim at the restoration and maintenance of the law against encroached or threatened injustice, and thus always comes down to a struggle between the evil and the good. The Platonic attempt, as Augustine called it, and as F. Schlegel, de Maistre and Stahl each interpreted it in their own way, to admire in the earthly arrangement of the State the faint shadow of the Civitas Dei, or of the Kingdom of God, could also not permanently captivate the world of thought, if one has the right conception of the Parousia of Christ, the Last Judgment and the rise of the new earth under the new heaven. In fact, little else has emerged from all that has been said about the purpose of the State than that...

—- Page 314 —-

298 THE STATE’S PURPOSE.
The conclusion to be drawn is that the State too was called to cooperate in helping to develop the rich endowment that was implanted in our race, both physically and psychologically. And this was to be done by regulating the proper relationship between the individual and the people to which he belonged, under the sovereignty of the purest right. This is to be understood in such a way that the individual did not stand in the way of the flourishing of the whole, but rather contributed much to it, and conversely, that the whole did not oppress the individual, but rather upheld and assisted in the unfolding of his powers. The Law here rendered a twofold service; first, in that it indicated the proper relationships, and secondly, by as much as possible restoring these proper relationships whenever they were disturbed, whether by egoism or by evil will. However well this representation may sound, one nevertheless never arrives at the State with this reasoning. All this can be said just as well of humanity as a whole, or even of all kinds of other groupings of people, whether in official or private associations. The State can never serve as an organ in the great organic whole of our human race. The organs of a body each have their own origin, their own mode of operation, and their own purpose. But it is not so with the States. They are all alike to one another, differing only in form and expression, not in essence. Just as our body has two eyes and two ears, two arms and two feet, where the one eye or the one hand may differ somewhat from the other, or even have something peculiar to itself, but as organs they all serve the same function—a body with only one eye or one arm would be unthinkable. And so it is here. However much the States may differ in extent, in population, in location, in wealth, and in military strength, they are nevertheless all States, and in their operation they are not identical, but still of the same kind. If you see the States as organs of living humanity, then you get a body of humanity with only one kind of organ, namely, just States, and this is unthinkable. Even among the lowest forms of life, such a simple system of organs is found, but in the highest standard of life that prevails in a people, such a thing is non-existent.

—- Page 315 —-

THE PSYCHE GOVERNS THE RELATIONSHIP - THE CONSUMMATION. 299
To say that the endowment that was implanted in us is to be developed, that the parts and the whole must stand in proper relationship to each other, and that where this proper relationship is lacking or disturbed, the law has the task of restoring the right proportions and relationships, points to a purpose that you can recognize to a certain extent in all expressions of life, but does not specifically indicate wherein lies the distinctive character of the State.

§6. The Psyche Governs the Relationship. Even stronger must it be spoken. Even if one grants, as we did above, that the physical also counts, yet it requires no argument that the development purpose and the endowment that our human race received at creation are primarily governed by the psychic life, even if dependent also on the physical. This brings about the fact that the Sittlichkeit, as Hegel called it, and naturally in connection with religion, art, and science, govern the future; and even though the State can and must act upon these psychic givens, yet it requires no further proof that these psychic moments are spiritual and not material in nature, and therefore can never derive their origin or vital force from the State. Every attempt to derive the development of the endowment that is given in humanity, in a people, or in the individual, from the State, must therefore fail. The State can only render auxiliary service in all this. It can be the nurse, or even the wet-nurse, but never the mother, in the birth and upbringing of peoples and of humanity. §7. The Consummation. Yet even this is not enough said. For any representation, as if in the State were to be sought the institution that will gradually lead humanity, by slow development, to its ultimate goal, collides with the definite declaration of Holy Scripture, that the state of affairs under which we now live on this earth...

—- Page 316 —-

The State’s Purpose. It will not achieve its fulfillment through a gradual and ever-continuing unfolding, but rather the existing order will, on the contrary, be violently shattered. Faced with this apocalyptic vision of Scripture, every writer must take a definite position on the purpose of the State. He must know whether he believes or rejects this solemn testimony of Holy Scripture. The teacher who will enlighten us about the State cannot say, “As a Christian, I confess what Scripture prophesies, but in my scholarship I do not reckon with it.” Whoever does not submit to Christ and does not ascribe authority to Holy Scripture can ignore Scripture both in their scholarship and in their personal life. But what does not concern them, and is irreconcilable with their honesty, is the fact that so-called believing writers, insofar as they move on the scientific terrain, abandon their faith and act as if Holy Scripture were for them little more than a bundle of fables. While we are constantly progressing in humbly submitting ourselves to the authority of God’s Word, we cannot but insert the consequences that the Scriptural doctrine of the Parousia and the end of the world must have for the doctrine of the State. In 2 Peter 3:10, we are told: “But the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night; in the which the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat, the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up.” And then follows in verses 12 and 13: “Looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God, wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat! Nevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.” Now if this categorical announcement stood by itself, one could still object that the authenticity of the Second Epistle of Peter is often very differently judged, but this is not the case. Christ Himself has spoken just as decisively about His Parousia, see Matthew 24:29 ff. “Immediately after the tribulation of those days shall the sun be darkened, and the moon shall not give her light, and the stars shall fall from heaven, and the powers of the heavens shall be shaken: And then shall appear the sign of the Son of man in heaven: and then shall all the tribes of the earth mourn, and they shall see the Son of man coming in the clouds of heaven with power and great glory.”

—- Page 317 —-

THE CONSUMMATION. All the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven, with great power and glory... The heavens and the earth will pass away. Watch therefore, for you do not know in what hour your Lord is coming. And further in Matthew 25:31 ff.: “When the Son of Man comes in his glory... then he will sit on his glorious throne... and before him will be gathered all the nations, and he will separate them one from another as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats.” Already in Isaiah 65:17 it was prophesied: “Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth, and the former things shall not be remembered or come into mind.” Precisely as the fulfillment is depicted for us in Revelation 21:1, where it says: “Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away, and the sea was no more.” Based on this, one therefore comes to the conclusion that it is not to be determined how long the present process on this earth will still continue; that no one can give any certainty in this regard; but that two things are certain, namely 1° that the ongoing world process will suddenly be interrupted; and 2° that when it is interrupted, all that now exists or will then exist will be annihilated and replaced by an entirely new state, here described as “a new earth under a new heaven.” With this, what the learned want us to believe—that the State possesses the mystical power to bring about the consummation and to bring our human race to the complete unfolding of the powers implanted in it—is absolutely incompatible. According to the prophets of the Old Covenant, according to the express statements of Jesus, and according to the apocalyptic visions in Revelation, the enmity against God and his Christ, that is, unrighteousness, will continue to the end, there will be no question of a higher perfection of our human race, and an end will come to the existing evils, not because virtue here overcomes evil, but because God puts an end to this entire dispensation and brings into being an entirely new dispensation by a mechanical intervention. In the end of all things, the State will not prove to have been the Redeemer of humanity, but rather in its powerlessness.

—- Page 318 —-

The goal of the state has been displayed, and when Christ, the Redeemer of our race, appears, He will call forth a new and sinless state of affairs in life, and will recreate all things. For the state, it will end in complete fiasco, the triumph will remain with Christ alone. The state will prove powerless to restore to us the lost paradise. To give us back our paradise, and that too in an exalted state of glory, can only be the work of our King, who is now seated at the right hand of God. A promise of the future, in connection with which it may be observed, how far geologists and astronomers agree with this prophecy, as has been set forth at length in our articles on the Consummation in the weekly De Heraut, that even some practitioners of these sciences expect a sudden break and destruction of the existing order, and in any case are unanimously of the opinion that no process in infinitum of our human life on our planet is to be thought of. They may postpone this outcome to a very distant future and thereby weaken the impression of it on their readers, but in fact their expectation of the outcome is the same, and thus to be summarized: Not an uninterrupted process, but rather a sudden break, a destruction of what has existed, in order to await the realization of the ideal thereafter. This now fits excellently with the conception of the state as a temporary corrective and surgical aid, but can never be reconciled with the now so prevalent notion, as if the state had given us the natural and highest unfolding of the nature of “man’s life in society.” And it remains inexplicable to us how more than one scholar of the state, who yet professed his Christian faith, nevertheless slid into this perverse conception of the state.
§8. The fall into sin and misery. It stands quite differently, however, when we have to do with a creature of God that has fallen from his first love. There is then a fall into sin and misery, and thus a disruption of external life-happiness and of inner perfection. We then have to do with a human race that, under the curse of sin, has lost the original state of blessedness and has fallen into a state of misery.

—- Page 319 —-

THE FALL, SIN, AND MISERY. The demonic influence stands and undergoes the consequences of the curse. One of the most striking consequences of this is that the genealogical coherence of the entire race is broken, and the whole shatters into families, households, and individuals. Had this gone on unimpeded, all higher development of our race would have been cut off, and baseness, greed, rapacity, and murderous impulses would have gained the upper hand—which is to say, our entire human development would have ended in complete bankruptcy. This was then prevented by the twofold benevolence of God, which through particular grace prepared the Kingdom of Christ, and through Common Grace made our human life on earth possible and cleared the terrain on which particular grace could flourish. Now among the cardinal elements of this Common Grace belong both the rise of groups of people in the organic bond of nations and peoples, and the appearance of the Government, to hold these peoples together and help them mature. And so the State now arose as a corrective and aid, as a medical bond applied to the peoples, in order to make possible, through cooperation, the physical and psychical unfolding of our life. In Christ, particular grace has broken through to dominion, and one day the whole will triumph in an even more exalted paradisiacal form, under His kingship, the triumph of God’s power over the demonic powers of disruption. But in the meantime, in the folk groups under appointed Governments, a firmly knit communal life has developed that has increasingly taken on the form of the State, as the bond now laid down to heal the sickened and ailing body. The purpose of the State, so understood and conceived, can therefore be none other than to provide crutches for the unfortunate who have broken a leg, to apply a surgical bandage to those who could no longer walk on their own; if you will, to deploy those who could not get along without being struck down, into a wonderfully agile and quickly maneuverable hand-propelled wheelchair, so that while the leg remains paralyzed, the action is almost entirely restored, as if it had never been injured. Yet this does not heal the cripple. And this continues until the complete healing finally comes, which lifts the sufferer out of his mechanical vehicle and restores the broken leg to its original strength.

—- Page 320 —-

The State’s Purpose. And now, in an elevated manner, it allows him to enjoy the life that has been entrusted to him—no longer under demonic inspiration, but now under the intonation of angels. But this leads to the Parousia.

§9. The Surgical Bandage. Understood in this way, the State thus has no other purpose than to make it possible for humanity, which could no longer progress, not indeed to run by itself, but at least to move again. It has no other purpose. Just as a cripple, who hobbles along on his crutches and is thus enabled to go to his office, earn a living, and provide for his family, it is not the crutches that work in the office, receive wages, and care for the offspring. All this is an expression of the man’s will, of his powers, of his talents, but the crutches perform a completely different service here, to make possible what he otherwise could not do, and what is this other than the “controlled” movement of his will. And so it is here as well. That a people develops, that a people progresses, that it gradually becomes more capable in its entirety and in its individual members, is the outflow of inclinations and drives, talent, genius, willpower, and opportunity. The people owe this in the least to the State or to the Government. The State and the Government only work, and can only work, to ensure that the development of all these powers and gifts, which would otherwise lag behind and even remain absent, now enters and enhances the positions of both the people and the individuals within this people. The State appears, with a Government that has no higher authority over it on earth, as soon as it takes the reins in hand and actually guides the group of people that it considers subject to it, but then the purpose of such a State is also quite different, namely to stem the fragmentation of the mass and its disintegration into group-wise divisions. The unity of our entire race is no longer to be restored, only the possibility of a group-wise cohesion has remained. This cohesion does not now proceed arbitrarily, but follows as a rule the division of our entire race into peoples. Even the division into races, since it led to large masses, could not be followed. On the other hand, what went by itself with a separate, mutually related group here and there

—- Page 321 —-

THE SURGICAL BANDAGE. 305 Also for an aggregate of only partially related groups and even merely aggregatorically imitated. But under whatever motive it may have come into being, the purpose of the intervention of the Government, and thereby of the intervention of a broader group in the state association, was never anything else and could never be anything else than to hold together what would otherwise fly apart, and to regulate this bond in its association, and to keep it under the constraint of the established rule, so that it could again reach the development of a normal human life. The Government and the State formed under its authority confront the partial further growth of the enclosed corruption. They intervene in a human life where sin physically and the curse psychically threaten the free, pure, normal unfolding of the life created by God and placed under regulation. But never in the sense that it is either from the Government or from the State founded by it that new life would go forth. However weak and half-withered the plant may be, whoever wants to revive it does not pour new life into the decaying branches themselves, but removes obstacles and brings in elements that must enrich or strengthen the inherent life in the plant. The high final purposes of the creation of human life never arise from the Government or from the State form, but are given in the creation of our race, and in the creation of the earth belonging to our race. Here too, it is the gardener who weeds out the weeds, removes pests and insects, binds up what has drooped, manures and waters what has withered, but the life itself never comes from the gardener, and no single vital force can the gardener imprint as a new foundation in the languishing plant. The growth, the development, the unfolding of the powers of the human race does not come from the State, nor from the Government, but from our human nature as God created it, and in connection with soil, climate and cloud pressure causes to germinate. Without a curse hindering this, and then the Government and the State founded by it to stem injustice in the moral and material sphere of nature corruption, and at least to make the unfolding of normal life possible to some extent, but still so that neither from the Government nor from the State could anything else ever go forth than the promotion and guidance of the development of life.

—- Page 322 —-

THE STATE’S PURPOSE. Not the unfolding, but never the life itself. In restraining corruption and strengthening what still remains of our human fellowship in its weakened state, therein lies the sole purpose given to the State. We might add that the Government, through the state form of the group or people over which it acts, defends against neighboring peoples who refuse to respect the boundaries between themselves and other peoples, but this is already inherent in restraining corruption. In an attack of people upon people, it is selfishness, ambition, or greed that speaks, and defense against its outbreak is the restraint of the poisonous element that stirs. Imagine sin and curse entirely gone, imagine a condition in which sin and curse would have remained unknown powers, then there would never have been a Government, nor would a State ever have been formed. This also explains why we do not reckon with the Fall, just as we are unable to indicate the purpose and action of Government and State, as one who deems sickness in human nature unthinkable, could explain the purpose of existence of a pharmacy, a hospital, an instrument cabinet, or a laboratory. In this predicament, one then grasps at something of a different kind, and assigns as the purpose of the State what is the purpose, not of the State, but of the Creation on this earth of the human race.

§10. The Help of the Law. This comes out most strongly in the high significance that has always, but especially in the previous century, been attributed to the Law in the life of the State, as the highest expression of that life. Although this may be exaggerated, yet no one will deny that in our sinful condition, that is, in a condition that threatens to break or falsify all relationships, the knowledge of the Law, the regulation of the Law, and the maintenance of the Law come to the forefront. Outside of sin, this would not be so, but it cannot be otherwise, now that hardly anything stands upright by itself as it ought to. In such a state of affairs, complaint after complaint arises, from one against another, and from many together against the general state of affairs. This leads to the question: Are the relationships as they ought to be? and as a direct consequence to this second one: How ought the relationships then to be? And if agreement has been reached on this, even if only to a certain extent, one naturally comes to the third question, how, what is crooked, shall be set right again, and maintained in a rightful state. All this now touches on legal knowledge, jurisprudence, legal regulation, legal enforcement, in short, the administration of the law in its most extensive sense, and it is therefore not at all incomprehensible that the sense of the law has for a time completely claimed the State idea for itself in the identification of the State with the Rule of Law. In the palace of a tubercular sick prince, hardly anything else is spoken of than hygiene and medications, how the lungs must be, what has crept into the lungs, and who can write the best prescription for that. One does not do that in the palace of a prince who enjoys robust health. It is the sickness that brings about this concentration of all attention on tuberculosis and its cure. And so it is here. The Law would hardly come to the fore in a normal Paradise state. It is the violation of the Law in the life of the people that draws all attention to the Law. And in nothing so much as precisely in this one-sided placing in the foreground of the Law, one tangibly feels how corrupt and unjust spontaneous action in the life of the people is. This is understood by one who takes the Fall as the starting point, but for one who, outside of sin everywhere, tries to explain our human life as a normal phenomenon, this coming to the forefront of the Law is completely inexplicable.

—- Page 323 —-

§11. The Operation of the Curse. Why do we point to the curse again and again here? Simply for the reason that the curse physically caused the same disturbance as sin did psychologically. The whole nature around us ceased to be normal. Hence, Government and State do not merely have as their purpose to stem crime and uphold the Law, but also something else, to counteract the destruction brought upon nature, hygienically in man himself, and in an ordering way in streams, shores, and forests. The monopoly of the Rule of Law State

—- Page 324 —-

308 THE PURPOSE OF THE STATE.
It follows from this that, when the necessities of life pressed themselves upon us, and the technical inventions also called for a connection with world commerce to ward off physical evil, the life has yet another side than the purely juridical. Man is soul and body. There are treasures that only the spirit grasps, but also treasures of health or gold that cling to the physical element; and, against all vain cries of the Rechtsstaat, this physical side of life has always asserted itself in all lands, so that the neglect of duty by the Government was a blot on the State’s equilibrium, if one judged that this whole side of life could be left to private initiative. Our human existence and life is, after all, two-sided. We are not angels, but men. We exist both psychically and physically. And it is a misunderstanding of human nature if one excludes the broad physical domain from the idea of the State and the Government. Even the absolute indispensability in the construction of roads, river improvements, fire brigades, lighthouses on the coast, and so much more, provides proof that the equilibrium of the State cannot rest on the juridical alone. Only the corruption of sin explains why the maintenance of the law over against the ever-surging unrighteousness has come more and more to the fore, however strange it may be that those who went furthest in this did not reckon with sin or curse.

§12. Soli Deo Gloria. If one now asks not what the purpose of the State, but what the purpose of human society is, which the Government ought to recognize in its guidance of the State’s affairs, here too the Soli Deo Gloria comes to the fore. You cannot go back to the Creation without it being established that God called our world and our human race into being in order to glorify Himself in it. You cannot make God’s Almighty power subservient to a purpose lying outside of Himself. In the Redeemer, who was Himself God, the divine love has indeed assumed the human form of self-sacrifice, but in God as God, love can be nothing other than the taking up into Himself and into His own sphere.

—- Page 325 —-

Soli Deo Gloria. Not absorption, but a living out, through and unto itself. Richer than what the man of Tarsus has said, it cannot be expressed. The purpose of our human existence lies not in ourselves, nor in any other creature, but solely and exclusively in God. The “tending towards Him” (ad Deum) is decisive here. It is the Creator who wishes to glorify Himself in His creature. In the artist, we find a shadow of this. In fact, a Rubens and a Rembrandt have also glorified themselves in their production. Yet even for the greatest artist, if he stands rightly before God, the end is that he kneels down and ascribes the honor to God. But above God, there is no one. God cannot kneel. God cannot give thanks. Everything comes from Him, is through Him, and culminates in Him. The life of creation, and thus the life of our human race, has no other purpose than to bring to manifestation the rich treasure of life that lay hidden in seed form in all things, and in that manifestation to display the Almighty, the Wisdom, the Holiness, and the Beauty of the Divine. Strength, talents, and gifts are laid down and hidden in the creature, and now it is the glory of God that reveals itself, as soon as all that God had enclosed and hidden in it comes forth, and comes forth in such a way that all rational creatures behold it with wonder and give thanks, since they see the Majesty of God shining in it. The horror of the fall, first in the angelic world, and then under Satanic influence in Paradise, was precisely that this self-glorification of Almighty God was disturbed and even permanently cut off according to Satan’s intentions. Immediately thereafter, however, the power of Grace manifests itself, of which Satan had not dreamed, and now it is this grace that gives such a direction to the life of the fallen world that nevertheless the honor of God in His work continues, and that in the end the human world will ascribe all honor to God, which He had enclosed within it. After this has been emphasized almost exclusively from the Calvinistic side, this Grace now divides itself, on the one hand into a Grace that is entirely general in character, but then also only has a restraining power, and on the other hand into a perfecting Grace that only comes to the benefit of a core of our race, but then also fully manifests the Majesty of God. The former Grace is usually called Common Grace, the latter Particular Grace. It is now in the dispensation of Common Grace that government has been assigned to us, and that the life of the State...

—- Page 326 —-

310 THE STATE’S PURPOSE.
It has arisen; and first, as the consummation of all things draws near, the Kingdom of Heaven, or if you will, the Kingdom of God, will break through as the fruit of particular Grace, which is already being prepared, but in the unseen, as Jesus Himself has testified, that this Kingdom is indeed within us, but does not come with outward appearance, (Luke XVII:20).

§13. The Church. The divine plan thus comes down to this, that the world, and on this world our human race, after the fall, is not destroyed, but remains in existence. If everything had been destroyed immediately after the fall, then the treasure of Majesty, which God had hidden in the germs and kernels of all things, would never have come to light, and the glorification of God in His work of creation would have failed. This could not be, and therefore even after the fall this earth is spared, our human race is spared, and the possibility is opened to our race to bring to manifestation all the powers, gifts, talents and treasures that were hidden in the creature. This went slowly, since it always went in friction with sin and curse. Hence the long duration. But in the course of the ages, the treasure of nature and the treasure hidden in human life has nevertheless continually come to light in ever fuller and richer measure. Even the previous century, and now already the 20th, has produced a development in all areas that has even aroused the expectation in not a few that we were approaching perfection, and thus the consummation. Even science could not entirely free itself from all enthusiasm. Yet this led astray. The development of all the treasures hidden in nature and in our human race will continue to the end. God will be perfectly glorified in His creatures, but the canker that lurks in our life will never be overcome, it will continue until the Parousia, in which everything will once and for all pass away, so that only then this dying world will rise again in new glory as a “new earth under a new heaven.” But in the meantime more happens. The dispensation of particular Grace continues

—- Page 327 —-

THE CHURCH. 311 and the appearance of Government and State also aims to give a place, as the Psalmist calls it, to the lair of its foot. For only where the Government creates an ordered State life, is it possible for the Church of Christ to appear. There remains a floating uncertainty, for the elect alone form the Body of Christ’s Church and on this earth we lack the fixed sign to distinguish precisely from person to person what belongs to this holy group or stands outside it. But even with this uncertainty, that Church is still a holy appearance, which carries a future in itself, in which the rest of the world will have no part. And now it appears again and again how the ordered human life, which is only possible under the guidance of Government in the State community, has brought about and confirmed the possibility of existence for this Church. Collision between these two heterogeneous phenomena cannot be avoided. Martyrdom had to come. But even so, it may be established that the perpetuation of our human race, as its life has unfolded in the course of the ages under the dispensation of Common Grace, has already realized to no small extent the twofold purpose, on the one hand to bring to ever richer manifestation what God has placed in the creature, and on the other hand, to prepare in the believers the Kingdom of Heaven, which will break forth after the Parousia. And it is now to the attainment of these two purposes that the Government in its State creation, by stemming sin corruption and curse and by re-creating life from its natural germination, has cooperated for all the ages since the Flood and continues to cooperate. In these two lies not the particular purpose of the State, but the purpose of the life of our human race on this earth, and it is the Government that in its State function serves this high twofold purpose. In these two, let it be repeated, lies not the direct purpose of the State. It is and remains in its duality the purpose of the continued existence of our human race, but the State offers for the attainment of this purpose in all human life a support that cannot be sufficiently appreciated, which man did not devise and assemble himself, but which God has ordained for us. Why the Consummation of History will not result in a complete

—- Page 328 —-

The question of whether the state aims at the happiness of all people, and even after the Parousia not at the salvation of all human creatures, is a theological issue that does not require discussion in the present context. The only thing that matters here is to make it clear how inconsistent and illogical it is for Christian statesmen to adopt the views of unbelievers regarding the purpose of the state, while completely setting aside what is so firmly and decisively revealed to us in Holy Scripture. Even the living humanity is subject to its own law, and an incoming element like the government, or even an abnormal form of life like that of the state, can never take the place of life itself. If the normal life had not been disturbed and corrupted, there would have been no government and no state, and therefore the purpose of both can only lie in the containment and binding of the breach that sin, corruption, and curse have inflicted on our human life, physically and psychologically. Chapter X THE STATES IN RELATION TO ONE ANOTHER

§1. Mutual Intercourse. However strictly the boundaries of the separate states may be guarded, a wall that would hermetically seal them off from one another cannot be erected on those boundaries. There always exists a certain mutual bond between country and country, especially where their borders overlap. This bond can be of various kinds and have a more general or more particular character. In a general sense, reference is made here to international law, both in its concrete and in its ideal form, and in a particular sense to various alliances, to special treaties, to the protectorate, and also to colonial possessions. States, as we have tried to show in the preceding paragraphs, are not of a natural, but always of a more or less artificial formation.

—- Page 329 —-

MUTUAL INTERCOURSE. 313
If the state appears as the outcome of the consequences of sin and curse, it does not at all follow from this that every government finds no other consequence of sin and curse to counteract within its own territory than what stands for its own country. Sin and curse are demonic powers that do not respect state borders. If, with regard to the curse, a plague epidemic breaks out, it does not halt at the border between the country to the right and the country to the left, but necessitates measures that the governments of both countries must cooperate to take. The same applies to serious livestock diseases. Also, a stream that comes from the mountains and seeks the sea does not respect any land border, but flows on, destroying what is in its way; and this too can compel the governments in the different countries to consult and cooperate on the outcome of that consultation. Even apart from the curse, the ordinary needs of life can already make such consultation and such cooperation unavoidable. The traffic on roads and railways, and likewise the communication of thoughts by post, telegraph, and telephone, require this. The natural desire to visit foreign countries, the frequent occurrence of marriages between persons from two different countries, the regulation of inheritances, and much more, cannot remain without international order. And to this is added, on a broad scale, trade in all its ramifications, which aims at the exchange of livestock and goods. All this already requires, in addition to the practical and material care of interests, the constant regulation of mutual legal relations. Crime does not hide within the borders either. The inhabitant of one country can commit his crime in the country across the border, or also try to bring his robbery to safety across the border, or finally try to escape the justice of his own country by fleeing to the other country. All this sets certain demands of mutual understanding to the states in their mutual relations, which are indeed of a very general nature, but nevertheless regulate themselves in an increasingly specific way according to the spirit of the times. Formerly there was often too much of a certain tendency

—- Page 330 —-

314 MUTUAL RELATIONS BETWEEN STATES.
They scarcely concerned themselves with what was going on or happening in neighboring countries, and did not always even lack the noble desire to make life difficult for one another. Even now there are still issues of this kind that have not yet been brought to a sufficiently satisfactory solution, especially with regard to the extradition of political offenders. But thanks to the vigorous initiative of the late Mr. Asser, it may now be said that at least in the civilized countries, the will exists to put in order, through mutual understanding, what we have summarized above. And this not in the sense that we thereby confer a favor on one another, but much more as a wholly natural obedience to the demands placed by the general community. Insofar, all measures flowing from this endeavor also have an international legal character, for all international law ultimately rests on the notion that there are duties resting on all States in their mutual relations, which are not the result of an agreement or of a burden imposed by a higher authority, but follow spontaneously from the divine order, and are therefore to be accepted entirely of their own accord.

§2. Sovereignty and International Obligation.
More emphasis must be placed on this spontaneous character of mutual international obligations, and thus also of the rights to which compliance is to be given, because the authority that regulates these rights and obligations is no other than God. If one clings strictly to the notio necessaria of sovereignty, consisting in the fact that whoever is sovereign on earth recognizes no other authority above himself, it follows directly that sovereign States cannot be compelled by any power on earth to recognize any right or any obligation whatsoever. There is no such authority and no such power on earth, and it can only rest in God. The great riddle for international law has therefore always been how one could explain the sovereignty of States in their own sphere, and yet concede that on earth they have no higher authority outside or above themselves; and yet still subject these sovereign States to certain obligations in international law.

—- Page 331 —-

SOVEREIGNTY AND INTERNATIONAL OBLIGATION
Burdens and obligations can only be imposed on those who have voluntarily submitted to them. Logically, the one excludes the other. Yet, the attempt is made to evade this by claiming that sovereign States are only subject to Common Law, while in every other domain it is constantly asserted and upheld as doctrine that law, to be valid, must be defined and enforceable. But in the domain of international law, we find that both of these are entirely lacking—both the authority that establishes the law by statute, and the authority that maintains the violated law. This compels submission to it. If the entire human race were one, living under a single government, and if this universal human government had not only proclaimed the rights and duties of all states in a general legal code, but had also established a judiciary to punish transgressions against it, and had appointed an executive power to enforce the verdict, then everything would fall into place naturally. But such a general world government does not exist. The law of nations, which is not based on contract, and to which people appeal, thus hangs entirely in the air. One cannot point to the source from which it springs. There is no lex scripta that defines it. Every government can make of it whatever seems most suitable to its own interests. The opinions of scholars, as well as public opinion, certainly provide a verdict pro and con. But anyone who reflects on how even within other domains the very concept of law is often still vague, wavering, changeable, and uncertain, can hardly suppress the question of why all these doubts, which have reduced the significance of law when left to itself, should on the terrain of international law suddenly lose all meaning, so that one dares to elevate an entirely unwritten law, lacking any lawbook, judge, and executive power, to the status of the ruling power over all lands, peoples, and states. That this way of thinking can be maintained if one honors this law as resting in God, requires no further argument. Even then, uncertainty may remain in the details, but for one who believes in God’s Almighty decree, the main lines of law can still be so unshakable that the deeper legal consciousness cannot be subject to any error. Those, on the other hand, who

—- Page 332 —-

Here too, they refuse to reckon with God’s Almighty power, and then want us to believe that the ultimate secure anchorage for international law lies in human nature itself—either by binding us to historical traditions that would then be inviolable, or by finally wanting to make us believe in the sovereignty of the legal ideal as such; and this without their ever having succeeded, nor ever succeeding, in providing proof for the correctness of their legal conception, unless they—and here I lay all the emphasis—unless they themselves acknowledge that it is either in human nature, or in the course of history, or in the Idea of Law itself, that the authority of a Higher Power, which must then be God, reveals itself. From the Christian side, this can never be emphasized enough. As long as we move within the boundaries of our own country, one can still accept the earthly legislator as the source of law, the law as the definition of law, and the judge as the upholder of the law. We do not say this is entirely correct, but one can at least conceive of it in this way. But as soon as one crosses the borders of the State to enter the domain of international law, that is the higher realm that lies above all States, then the human legislator, the human law, and the human judge fall away, and one is no longer under the protection of God’s authority. A thick bundle of treaties certainly provides a firm hold here, but what flows from this binds only those States between whom a treaty has been concluded in itself. And insofar as one can appeal to nothing else with regard to other States than the spirit that speaks consistently from these treaties, one has nothing but precedents before one, the validity and correctness of which one must first prove.
§3. International Law. International law cannot maintain its majesty unless it bears a universal and sacred character. There must speak from it an ordering of God, which, apart from all treaties, with power to exercise even destructive criticism of those treaties, impresses itself upon the conscience of the peoples as summoning all to obedience.

—- Page 333 —-

THE LAW OF NATIONS. 317 And for this, it is all the more imperative that this reverence for divine authority be absolute, because in no other domain is the temptation to break the law out of lust for dominance or greed as strong as when it comes to the relations between peoples. This temptation already pricks where a dispute is being settled by diplomats. No statesman feels a richer emotion than the awareness that, for the good of his land and people, he has checkmated the government of another country. But not so the people themselves, in their broader circles, when involved in such diplomatic wrangling—then a national passion arises, with which no other passion in our human nature can even be compared. What this passion, even between cultured peoples, can drive to on the battlefield, the Franco-German war of 1870 had already taught us, but it was only in the recent World War of 1914 that it came to clear expression. Nothing so much as national passion brutalizes man. History therefore provides almost irrefutable proof in every age that nowhere less than in the international domain is one inclined to yield to the law. The most slippery language is devised to present, in the garb of sacred justice, what is repugnantly unjust. The salus rei publicae suprema lex esto has always dominated the legal conception in the international domain. To acknowledge inequality before his lands and peoples in the face of an advancing army is the hardest thing that can befall a Prince or Statesman, and however severely Machiavelli may have condemned it, every thoughtful reader of history will ultimately concede that when a mighty international conflict looms, reverence for the law is often reduced to a minimum. The cruelty and bloodthirstiness that have so often wrought havoc in Europe in the past, and are now repeatedly manifested, especially in Asia and Africa, and as was also evident not long ago in Mexico, and is also not lacking in the war of 1914, cries to heaven. That international law has nevertheless had such a blessed and effective influence would not be explicable if that law had found its support only in the idea of law. Rather, this blessing can only be understood if one takes into account the power of righteousness, as it goes forth from God as the Judge of heaven and earth. The entire Pacifist movement is based on this serious awareness

—- Page 334 —-

The Pacifism. This Pacifism now aims at two goals. On the one hand, it feels and confesses that governments have far too often drawn the sword too lightly, and therefore—and this very rightly—it strives to curb such reckless and dangerous presumption for the future. On the other hand, it has gone very rashly to dream of a future unity of the whole human race and dreams ever more of a gradually approaching Paradise, where the sword will be forged into a sickle. No more army, no more navy, and thus a realization even now of “Peace on earth.” These two movements can now support each other to a certain extent, but they must not be confused with each other. For both actions proceed from completely different expectations for the future, and here too the acceptance of God’s Word or the rejection of the authority of Holy Scripture makes the principled distinction. Whoever derives the image of life from his own nature, from history, from what he still observes around him today, and illuminates it all through Scripture, sees and knows that there is a disruptive force at work both in our nature and in our society, which will always cause the struggle to flare up, not only between individuals, but also between nations. There can be no talk of a radical excision of this cancer. This evil may continually take on other forms, but in principle it remains what it was from the beginning and does not at all diminish in the deadly effect of its cancerous poison. He who stands for the abolition of armies and navies, for eternal peace, for having done once and for all with all strife and war, cannot believe this of himself. He knows that only with the Parousia of Christ will the curse be lifted, and therefore he rejects false utopias. He knows it, struggle remains imposed on us here on earth. But although this stands irrevocably before him, this in no way relieves him of the high...

—- Page 335 —-

PACIFISM. It is a serious obligation not to leave undone anything that is possible, in order to help turn away the evil that can be averted. Hence, the official action in this field has always had the heartfelt sympathy of the Christian, and he can thank God for every threat of artillery that is averted by Pacifism. He does indeed thirst for the eternal peace, but he cannot expect that except with the return of Christ. The advocates of the other view, on the other hand, do not think they can limit themselves to this. They set aside the Holy Scriptures, and refuse to take into account even a Consummation of this world by the Parousia of Christ. They believe only in the steady progress of our human life through Culture. In connection with this, they cling firmly to the Peace Conferences and the Court of Arbitration, even though they recognize that with this only the first spadeful in the ground has been made, and that the whole digging up of the field is still to come. The goal they are aiming at is that an accord be reached between all nations, without a single one excepted, so as to forestall as much as possible all diplomatic disputes, and, if it should still break out, to bring it before the Court of Arbitration for settlement. From all nations there must then go forth a mandate to a formed college, or to a designated government, to exercise control over all peoples, to call to account the nation that offends, to settle disputes of whatever nature between peoples, and to compel by force, if need be, the condemned nation that will not listen willingly, to submit. All armies, it is thought, will then be abolished, all fleets sold for scrap. Nothing will remain but an international police force, in which the governing college of the entire world-empire will possess the means of power to compel obedience. All higher, intermediate and lower education must imprint deep in the youthful heart the conviction that this is as possible as it must be. All literature will be made to breathe the same spirit and strive to work on the minds. Finally, all peoples in all peoples, and every individual person, will want nothing but peace. Whoever still babbles of war, his wages will be scorn and mockery, and ever louder will resound the prophecy of a complete happiness of all peoples.

—- Page 336 —-

320 BETWEEN THE STATES. This comes, and under the enjoyment of this no one will any longer be able to explain to himself how there could ever have been a time when fleets and armies devoured the treasure of all peoples. Combating this second striving outside of Scripture, the Pacifists consider absolutely unnecessary. Utopias always fade into hallucinations, and the evil is only that war after war continually comes to disturb these hallucinations again. There is simply no solution in harmony between these two, thirsting for peace as the prophets. For on both sides one holds fast to his ideal, and the difference is only that the one believes in God, while the other has nothing but his faith in man. Fortunately, this difference in starting point does not prevent us from joining in the high song of peace. The military bravado never again finds an echo with us. And in part the action of these ever-excited Utopists does support our own purpose.

§5. Court of Arbitration. The Arbitration or Court of Arbitration has also increasingly become our serious slogan, even though there is still something enigmatic in the fact that the action for the introduction of this Court of Arbitration has just come from the Czar of Russia, while on the other hand it may be called symbolic that not in one of the gigantic capitals of Europe or America, but in the rural country of The Hague, the Palace in which the idea of the Court of Arbitration found its confirmation, was established. Czar Peter had already in the 17th century laid a bond between Russia and the Netherlands, and if one were to look for a people small in extent, but ambitious through its past, then the choice that the Emperor of Russia made was undoubtedly justified, both historically and geographically. Now, however, whoever soberly grasps reality, promises neither from the Peace Conferences nor from the Court of Arbitration golden mountains. To the accord on which one hoped when signing the treaty of July 29, 1899, the great powers have not even given a hair’s breadth of follow-up, and insofar as it was accepted, this was done with such strongly bound reservations, for what “vital interests, independence or honor” of the contracting Parties might touch, that the

—- Page 337 —-

COURT OF ARBITRATION.
The treaty concluded on February 12, 1904 between the Netherlands and Denmark in Copenhagen, and from which both parties omitted any such reservation, still remains unique of its kind. This was to be expected. Already the Argentine guide on international law, Carlos Calvo, declared in his “Le Droit International Théorique et Pratique” 4th Ed. Paris 1887, p. 790: “Arbitration can address any kind of disagreement or international dispute, except those in which the national honor or dignity are directly at stake and which relate to an intimate, so to speak personal, sentiment of which a third state could not make itself the judge.” The no less authoritative Geffcke expressed himself thus: “A question which concerns the position of power and honor, a state will not easily submit to arbitration” (see Heffter, European International Law, p. 231). Renault likewise said in his Preface to the Recueil des Arbitrages Internationaux of Lapradelle and Politis, that the arbitration treaty is “a delicate instrument, which does not suit all affairs, which must be handled by expert and prudent hands where its use is justified” p. XI. And Bonfils, in his Manual of Public International Law, also testifies on p. 225 that there was not a single arbitration treaty concluded that excluded disputes over “the sovereignty or independence of a State.” And although it must be acknowledged that treaties were concluded between some South American Republics that were almost all-encompassing, yet one should note that this has by no means prevented the outbreak of war between two of those states shortly thereafter. One is therefore wrong to imagine that such an arbitration agreement could ever guarantee peace, if only because the preservation of the political balance of the Great Powers from within compels them to intervene. Renault also comes forward in his aforementioned preface: “I admit that in practice I do not attach very great importance to these prior commitments, because ultimately, in a given case, the organization and foundation of the arbitration always depend on the good will of the governments involved in the dispute” (p. VII). The absolute arbitration treaty that came into being in 1904 between the Netherlands and Denmark has therefore every chance of remaining a unique case, and this is partly explained by the improbability that a conflict of a serious nature would ever arise between these two States, and

—- Page 338 —-

322 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. This was partly due to the precarious position in which Denmark had been placed by its war with Germany and Austria, and no less by the digging of the North-East Sea Canal. Only for Russia did the free passage through the Sound remain a vital interest in the well-known Russian authority on international law, the prematurely deceased Dr. Martens, who ultimately insisted so strongly on the self-neutralization of both Denmark and the Netherlands, may be considered to have contributed in Copenhagen to the acceptance of the Dutch proposals for the conclusion of an absolute Arbitration Pact. In the commemorative issue of Peace through Law, which appeared when the King of Denmark visited our Court in May 1914, I have explained this and other matters in more detail.

§6. The Arbitration Idea. Yet it would be a mistake to infer from this that the advance of the arbitration idea is to be underestimated. Impressed as I am with the results of the First Peace Conference, I have not hesitated to express my dissatisfaction with them in the Second Chamber. The exclusion of the delegates from the South African Republic, and the treatment accorded to the Armenian complainants, aroused my deep indignation in themselves; and when shortly afterwards the war in South Africa broke out, leading in 1902 to the incorporation of the Transvaal and the Orange Free State into the Colonial Empire of Great Britain, my disappointment surpassed itself. Especially those who remember how the Czar had convened the First Peace Conference, not at all solely to give currency to the arbitration idea, but very expressly also to promote partial disarmament, feel the bitter contrast when not only war after war, but also over the whole of Europe, such an increase in armaments followed as had not even been considered possible in 1899. That nevertheless the Cabinet which I had the honor to form in 1901 intervened so earnestly for arbitration, as appears from the treaty with Denmark, provides proof that it is by no means only the Utopians who are preparing to serve the cause of peace. Four of the then ministers

—- Page 339 —-

THE ARBITRATION IDEA. The gentlemen Melvil van Lynden, Ellis, Loeff, and Van Weede have recently advocated for this idea—and it is noteworthy that Mr. Ellis, who was a Vice-Admiral and thus a military man, vigorously defended the treaty in our House of Representatives. The Court of Arbitration has already rendered excellent services in the past ten years, not least in the case of the Russian fleet when it sailed from the North Sea to Japan and committed the unforgivable blunder of firing on English fishing boats, which would have immediately provoked a declaration of war, especially since England was Japan’s ally. It was precisely in that precarious moment that the Court of Arbitration in Paris reaffirmed the already loosened peace for Europe, and the prospect is by no means excluded that the idea of arbitration may bring us even more beautiful surprises. Deep in the 19th century, though never forgotten, diplomatic art all too often sought its honor in having a bundle of grievances ready in each Department of Foreign Affairs, which were then temporarily allowed to rest, but kept at the ready so that, should war prove desirable, they could seriously provoke tensions. At the given moment, the sharpest grievance was brought forward, and thus one’s opponent was kept in one’s power. This indefensible practice from a higher point of view has now been ended to a considerable extent. When England began to incline towards indicating its antithesis to France and preparing a more friendly posture, there was even a deliberate clearing away of old grievances between these two mighty Empires, and the old tactic of hoarding potentially usable grievances has at least come to an end between these two States. The idea of arbitration has gained ground, and not without reason has Bonfils in his Manual of Public International Law, p. 542 ff., sought to collect together what had already been spoken and done in various Parliaments in favor of the Court of Arbitration. No diplomat now dares, as before, to brutally seek a cherished occasion in every knot that disfigures the cord in order to disturb the peace. And now it is quite true that hitherto for the Court of Arbitration there have been nothing but disputes of a very subordinate nature to settle.

—- Page 340 —-

It has been brought about, and it is to be feared that this will remain so for the time being, but those who complain about this forget that in the past, occasions for breaking the peace were all too often sought precisely in such insignificant matters, and that behind what seemed to be insignificant trifles, such deeply-felt sensitivities of an entire people can be stirred up that what appeared small later proves to have harbored the stuff for a truly serious conflict. And although the time has passed when one would send a declaration of war to one’s neighbors at the drop of a hat, and although it must be admitted that no great war is now conceivable for Europe, except where it concerns the balance of power between states or groups of states, it cannot be denied that precisely where the balance of power necessitates war, one will prefer not to come out so openly, whereby the danger remains that one will once again draw some insignificant incident or dispute from Pandora’s box in order to seemingly justify one’s aggression. In the removal of all such dangerous and easily inflammable material, therefore, there is gain, and although it stands firm for us that the “eternal peace” cannot be ushered in before the return of Christ, and although it cannot be denied that whoever believes that is indulging in utopias, our love for peace must never be silenced by military inclinations. Our sympathies remain pledged to peace, bloodshed remains an abomination to us, which does not exclude, however, that the dreadful moment may also come for the Netherlands when we would have to tell our own sons that it was their duty before God to risk their own lives for the fatherland. Who is there who did not feel this in August 1914?
§7. Arbitration is an agreement. Arbitration is an agreement pure and simple. It is not a legal act of the highest court. Hence, there is no higher authority to compel the parties to comply with the decision imposed on them. Professor van Vollenhoven, a utopian in this regard like few others, has felt this, and for this reason he has distanced himself from the proposal...

—- Page 341 —-

It is a daring proposal to designate a specific country’s government as the one that would have the right and duty to ensure the enforcement of any given arbitration decision, if necessary by the use of armed force, and that would therefore need to have at its disposal a secure international police force for that purpose. But precisely what he proposed provides conclusive proof of how little such a plan is likely to be realized. Naturally, his plan completely excludes the whole world. Just consider how, time and again in the past, revolutionary popular elements have succeeded in creating and deploying an armed force that simply swept away the army of the State and disarmed it, and then ask yourself what would become of all of Asia, America, and Europe, not to mention Africa and Australia, if the government did not have at its disposal a sufficient military force to counter and suppress all such revolutionary outbreaks everywhere. Taken together from all countries, such a police force, even if all armies were abolished, would have to represent such a formidable, tremendous power that there could hardly be talk of an unarmed peace.
