What was England, comparatively speaking, not yet so significant in the middle of the 16th century, how little did our small Holland then weigh against Great Britain, and how rapidly has England’s rise in social and political importance deprived us of our supremacy at sea? Therefore, it is not to be assumed that from now on and for centuries to come, the map of the world would remain as it is now. This simply cannot be. The ebbing and flowing of the life forces in the various peoples and the changes that this brings about in their mutual and reciprocal relationships cut off the possibility of this irrevocably. The saying of the Stoics, “All changes and nothing remains as it was,” applies here completely. What lives, changes constantly. See it in the plant world, see it in the animal world, see it in family life, and therefore it cannot be otherwise with the life of the peoples. In the bedding of the peoples there are no stagnant waters that become poisoned in the swamp, but there is a constant stream, and this current keeps the freshness alive. Now, recognizing this, would one dare to assert that this constant change could also be regulated in full peace by treaties? One feels how the whole nature of human nature rises up against this. Every attempt by the

—- Page 410 —-

394 THE STATES AMONG THEMSELVES. The States, in order to diminish the significance of the State and push it back within ever-narrower boundaries, would provoke the threatened State to offer a last, extreme, heroic resistance; and it would end up in war. Of course, the picture that the world presents at this moment does not have an absolute character. The accidental nature of it will be denied by no one. And how then would one want the accidental result of the recent past to remain the rule for all ages? It would be the same as if one were to let the garment tailored for a particular age be the fixed model until his death, although everyone knows how constantly we grow in bodily form and how he who makes our clothes must continually reckon with this growth. A federation of unchangeable States would therefore be absolutely unthinkable for the entire community of nations. In the very idea of such a federation for the whole world lies a Conservatism to which the living nations do not conform.

§40. COMMUNITY OF ORIGIN. But even with this, the critique of the tempting but unworkable idea of a single federation for the whole world is not yet complete. It is very serious that the question must still be considered, what then moved the originally independent States, which dissolved into it, to unite so closely. And then the main thing is to point to two motives, namely, to community of origin and to common danger. The dissolution of England and Scotland into Great Britain always went much more smoothly than the union as far as Ireland was concerned. Even quite recently, the union of England with Ireland has not been sealed without difficulty. And what else can explain this, than the fact that England and Scotland are both populated by Germanic tribes, while Ireland is of Celtic origin. That religious difference was added here, worked in, but just this religious difference was again connected with the difference in tribe. What was Celtic always found its religious satisfaction in what Rome offered. What comes from divergent origins does not usually fit together harmoniously.

—- Page 411 —-

COMMUNITY OF ORIGIN. 395 The Flemings and Walloons in neighboring Belgium still feel that the wall of separation between them stands erected. Austria, which has been brought together from population groups of four different origins, still lacks its national unity. In Italy, on the other hand, the entire people have cried out for centuries for their Irredentism, and Germany knows no way to overcome its former fragmentation than by boasting of its common origin. Czechs and Germans in Bohemia, that remains the cause of endless strife. And wherever one looks at the map, one always perceives that the close union of any population groups is hopeless where the difference in origin is too great, and on the other hand has sometimes succeeded very vigorously where the origin was one. Seen in Russia and Finland, what resistance the fusion runs into, and conversely in the Balkan States, where the bond of origin could still draw for centuries after separation. But this binding force of unity of origin now completely gives way in a world federation, for this unity of origin steps between Negro and Swede, Chinese and Spaniard, Arab and Swiss, or if you will, Dane and Copt, a difference of existence that always cut off all deeper connection. And where one points to Switzerland and the American Union to show how very different groups can still come to a very close connection, this does not prove in the least the possibility that it could go the same way in the entire world community. Switzerland is too small and geographically in too isolated a position to be able to set laws, and in the Union of North America it was undoubtedly the Anglo-Saxon element that absorbed the other immigrant groups into itself, although not without the Anglo-Saxon type itself undergoing modification; something to which it may be added that the newer States of the Union have never been independent States, and, born in the Union, have never known any other life than that of the Union. Language decides this above all. All of America in the Union speaks English, though with an American accent.

—- Page 412 —-

396 THE MUTUAL RELATIONS OF THE STATES.
§41. The Danger They Share in Common. No less important is the second fact to be considered here, namely, that the alliances which have thus come into being owe their cohesion almost solely to a common danger. The alliances, in the narrower sense, take the lead in this. A larger union, such as that contemplated at the Congress of Vienna after the fall of Napoleon, could not but bear a special character. Napoleon had set his sights on a world empire, in which the supremacy of France over all the peoples of the earth was to be assured, and against this design the whole of the rest of Europe had then united. But this created a relationship that could not be maintained. When France’s supremacy declined and sank, the allies soon had disputes among themselves, and in these emerging disputes they moved in very divergent, and at times even sharply conflicting, directions—thus leading to the alliance first of the three Emperors of Eastern and Central Europe, and then, after Russia’s swing, to the Central European trio of Germany, Austria, and Italy, arrayed against France, Russia, and England. These alliances, however, are not durable as a rule. They are for a time, and regulate themselves according to the events and relationships that govern the situation for a while. What has penetrated much deeper, and in the end also governed the alliances, is the kinship that of itself divides the states into certain groups, or even forces them into competition with each other by too strong a unity in blood. The peoples of Asia are moving in a different direction than the peoples of Europe, and in Europe the peoples of the three great stocks—Romance, Germanic, and Slavic—are each moving in their own direction. Between these diverging groups, tension arises of itself. The question is always recurring whether Asia, or Europe, or even America, and also whether in Europe the Slavs or the Germanic and Romance peoples, will play the leading role, just as you now see tension arising between China and Japan. Thus each race and each stock is constantly in danger of being overpowered by another race or another stock, and herein lies “that silent power” which in the end...

—- Page 413 —-

COMMON DANGER. The fact that peoples of the same kind always draw closer together and live in peaceful harmony is due to a common danger. Interludes occur here as well. The relationship between Russia and England is now completely reversed from what it was in the 19th century. But on the whole, the primordial struggle between the races and main tribes, which is always of a twofold cause, first in that it can never lead to a world union, and secondly out of fear of the competing races, so that peoples of the same race are ultimately always ready to offer each other mutual support. This is also why the seductive idea of a world league of states must inevitably stumble; and all appeals to the union of earlier miniature states that later merged into one kingdom lack all power of proof against this. What merged and coalesced was of the same ancestral stock, or feared a common danger. Now, since racial and tribal differences divide the world, and the competition of races and tribes drives them into mutual hostility rather than hand-in-hand cooperation, it is clear that the idea of a league of states, as soon as it aims to encompass all the peoples of the entire earth in one bond, is nothing but an illusion, which is cruelly exposed as vain by overwhelming events time and again. If our human race could have unfolded itself in a natural way as one organic whole, there would never have arisen any division, it would have blossomed forth as one mighty organism created by God, and there would not even have been any need for a mechanical connection in the form of states. But since the sin of the Fall broke our unity, the variations of life resulted in antithesis, and a dividing, dominating element arose from the human heart, the organic bond lay shattered, and now in the states a mechanical bond has come to the fore, which, though working as a valuable surgical aid, yet inevitably brought with it the loss of the psycho-organic unity, so that a pax aeterna between the peoples and thus also between the states is no longer conceivable. Not as a product of human invention, but as the fruit of divine restoration, what we with all the Pacifists pray for will one day come through Christ’s Parousia, but what the Pacifist conjures up with his slogans and incitements is nothing but a mirage.

—- Page 414 —-

The peoples do not live under a fixed Law. International law cannot change its nature, and therefore, however sacred its dominion may be, it will always lag behind in the administration of foreign law among the various States. Our conclusion therefore returns to our starting point. That there must also be a definite Law between peoples is beyond doubt. There is a relationship among men, and so also between peoples. That relationship must be as God has ordained it and as He wills it to be in each particular case. Thus there is an international law in an absolute sense, and the fault is ours if the legal relations here do not strictly correspond to it in every respect. Just as God alone is the One who has ordained and determined how the relationship between man and man shall be in the family, in society, and in a nation, so too God alone can determine how the relationship between peoples shall be. Now that Law ordained by God is International Law. This absolute Law comes to expression among peoples through consultation, through a sense of justice, and through agreement, but the organ to administer it in God’s Name, that is, to prescribe and govern it, is lacking. Such an organ does exist for the Law of God in an ordered State, but not for the peoples among themselves, inasmuch as there is no Authority set over all peoples. This is the case within the boundaries of each nation taken separately. The domestic Authority, if we may so express it, acts in the Name of God, commissioned by Him as His organ. That the Authority cannot be understood in any other way than as the organ of God’s all-encompassing sovereignty

—- Page 415 —-

THE LIFE OF PEOPLES UNDER A FIXED LAW. 399 The power to dispose is evident from the fact that the Government does and can do, and in part must do, what never belongs to any person, but only to God. God alone has the right to take away someone’s possession, to deprive them of their freedom, to imprison them and ultimately to kill them by punishment, or through the cruelty of war. These three—that the Government takes your money in the form of taxes and even forces you to sacrifice it, that the Government can imprison you, and that the Government can dispose of your life—are powers that by nature belong only to God, which no human being can appropriate by agreement with their fellow human beings, and which therefore constitute a usurpation by the Government officials appointed by God to exercise these all-encompassing powers that belong solely to God. However, the powers of the Government do not extend beyond the boundaries of the State or the Kingdom. What goes beyond those boundaries can only be determined by treaty or agreement. This determination by agreement may often find support in tradition and in the sense of justice. Insofar as the Special Revelation of Holy Scripture sheds light on this, it should also be taken into account by Christian Governments. To a certain extent, treaties and agreements will then be a reflection of the right ordained by God. But there is still a lack of certainty here. Time and again, treaties have been concluded that contained injustice towards one of the two parties or injustice of both parties to the detriment of a third party. In the replacement of such a retaliatory treaty by a new one, the recognition of the injustice often lay in that change. Just as in the private life of people it frequently happens that one is formally bound by one’s agreement, but nevertheless comes to the insight that one has conceded things that one ought not to have conceded, so that the casuistry of the law of promises and the state of necessity comes into play, so also in the agreements and treaties between peoples. That is precisely why it is so desirable that, while conflicts are constantly arising in international law and all legislation and administration of justice from above is lacking, the Court of Arbitration should increasingly gain ground and the Peace Conferences should also establish the laws of war. But however much one may strive for this and not cease the attempt to make the public opinion of all peoples ever more determined against war, it must nevertheless be clearly seen that there can never be a real administration of justice between the peoples of the earth. Even if in the end all the peoples of the earth, without a single exception, were to bind themselves by treaty, the sun of this new international life would not shine for long. For already now it has been amply shown how absolutely impossible it is to persuade all States to sign. And what is even more depressing, repeatedly dashing the fairest expectations: if there is even one who breaks what was agreed, then everything is immediately back on loose screws, and there is no power to punish the violator of what was agreed, according to the law. The world police, too, has been discussed above. It is logically conceived, but it is either not to be established, or, even where it has been established, it would make such a fiasco that it would mock all the ideals of peace. That the representation of the mutual relationship between the States given in this chapter deviates from the common doctrine is to be explained by the fact that the definition of what is right in an absolute and objective sense cannot come to sufficient clarity when one tries to leave the divine origin of all Right out of account. Whoever does this must subjectivize the concept of law and cannot start from the objective.

—- Page 416 —-

400 THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLICS AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE. Chapter XI THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLICS AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.

§1. The Rise of the Republic. Let us now follow, as we already indicated in §33 of the previous chapter, a brief account of the sad events at the Cape.

—- Page 417 —-

THE ENGLISH AT THE CAPE. The first contact with the colony that would later be called the Cape took place in 1486, when Bartholomew Dias landed at Lisbon, and after him a second adventurer established trade relations in 1497. Yet it was not until the 16th century that European settlement in this vast Kaffir land came about. The first attempt was made in 1630 by two English ship captains, but without leaving any occupation behind, so that the Dutch East India Company found the terrain free when they took possession of Table Bay in 1652.

§2. The English at the Cape. Many Dutch colonists gradually migrated there after that occupation. Even from the Amsterdam orphanage, adult girls were sent there, as they sought to prevent them from marrying Kaffir girls from the outset. Also many refugees who had meanwhile sought refuge in the Netherlands went there as emigrants. And soon the Cape was the most prosperous of the Dutch colonies. This initially rising state of affairs, however, lasted only until 1795, when the English took possession of the Cape. And although it had been determined at the Peace of Amiens that the Cape should revert to the Netherlands, when the war between England and France broke out again, England considered itself free to conquer the colonies of a country that was so united with France. The final occupation then took place in 1806, and since then the Cape has remained English, for at Vienna in 1815 England managed to secure this acquisition of colonial territory, with the acquiescence of the other powers. This occupation was followed in 1820 by an invasion of English settlers, who treated the old Dutch colonists very haughtily, so that any growth of the two types of inhabitants for the rural population soon proved impossible. Especially the treatment of the natives soon gave rise to very serious divisions, and since the English governors always took the part of their countrymen, this soon brought about such a tense situation that there was no longer any thought of peaceful coexistence.

—- Page 418 —-

The South African Republics and the Orange Free State. The colonial settlers decided to move northward, cross the Vaal, and take possession of the land above the Vaal. Transvaal also came to take an independent position, after the Orange Free State. At first, this gave an irregular relationship with England, but Commandant-General A.W.J. Pretorius was able to initiate negotiations in 1848 that already led to the conclusion of a treaty on January 16, 1852, by which treaty, called the “Convention of Sand River,” Great Britain recognized the independence and sovereignty of the “Boer territory beyond the Vaal River.” This Sand River treaty made no the slightest reservation for any English sovereignty. For Article 1 declared: “Her Majesty’s Commissioners warrant in the fullest manner on behalf of the British Government, to the Emigrant Boers beyond the Vaal River, the right to manage their own affairs, and to govern themselves according to their own laws, without any interference on the part of the British Government, and that no encroachment shall be made by the said Government on the territory beyond to the north of the Vaal River.” Furthermore, in Article 2 the system of arbitration was accepted for any possible disputes that might arise, while in Articles 3–9 certain provisions were established regarding slavery, trade and commerce, the acquisition of ammunition, land tenure. It was even stipulated that no trade in ammunition with the natives would be allowed from the English side. Over this Convention, England soon came to regret. When it became clear how powerfully the two Boer republics were developing, they immediately tried to retract the step taken, and already on April 12, 1871, the Governor of the Cape, Sir Theophile Shepstone, did not hesitate, by completely setting aside the Convention, to declare the two Republics to be British territory and to annex them for England. The flag of the Republic was lowered in Pretoria on his orders by his soldiers, and the British flag hoisted in its place. A deputation that went to London to protest against this act of violence returned without having achieved anything. It became clear that the English government had set its mind on the destruction of the Transvaal. Hence, already on December 10, 1879 the storm signal went up in Heidelberg and the war broke out. England tried to suppress this resistance, but the courage of the Boers was not to be broken, and when on February 27, 1881 General...

—- Page 419 —-

When Gladstone, with his blue-coated regiments, overwhelmed the English forces on the Spitskop or Majuba, it was felt in England itself that a new state of affairs had come into being. Thereupon, on the 8th of August, a new Convention was concluded at Pretoria. This, however, could no longer satisfy the Boers. For Article 1 of this Convention stipulated that the inhabitants of the Transvaal territory would indeed be granted “complete self-government,” but, as it literally stated, “subject to the suzerainty of Her Majesty”; and furthermore, “subject to the following limitations.” In particular, Article 2 provided that the British Government would maintain a Resident in the Transvaal, and furthermore, “that the control of the external relations of the said State, including the conclusion of treaties and the regulation of diplomatic intercourse with foreign powers,” would “be vested” in Her Majesty’s diplomatic and consular agents abroad. Kruger, Joubert, and Pretorius signed this Convention on behalf of the “Transvaal Burghers,” not on behalf of the country, nor by authority of the Volksraad, and ultimately, it cannot be judged otherwise than that, especially with Majuba in mind, the Boers came away from this Convention with the short end of the stick. Yet at the time there was no prospect of obtaining better terms. People therefore resigned themselves and awaited a better future, as soon as the spirit of the Cabinet in London would have penetrated to the Cape.

§3. Gladstone. In 1880, Gladstone then assumed his fourth ministry, and this raised hopes in the Transvaal. This new phase was kept regularly informed by Secretary Du Toit, and thus one could closely follow the entire course of events that led to the dispatch of the Deputation Krüger, Du Toit, and General Smit to London. In November 1883, this Deputation took up residence at the Albemarle Hotel in London, where I, together with the late Mr. Beelaerts van Blokland, joined them at Krüger’s request. It was this Deputation that then succeeded in not allowing the Pretoria Convention to stand, and in having it replaced by a new Convention of February 27, 1884. In this new Convention, there was no longer any mention of suzerainty, and the name of the Transvaal was now officially changed to the “South African Republic.” No Resident was appointed anymore, but it was merely stated in Art. 3: “If a British officer is appointed to reside in Pretoria, or elsewhere in the South African Republic, to discharge functions analogous to those of a consular officer, he will receive the protection and assistance of the Republic.” And further, it was only stipulated in Art. 4: “The South African Republic will conclude no treaty or engagement with any State or nation other than the Orange Free State... before it has been approved by Her Majesty the Queen.” And as if to soften even this provision, it was added that “if within six months after the notification of any such treaty or engagement to Her Majesty’s Government, no objection has been made thereto, the treaty or engagement shall be regarded as approved.” Of course, it would have been very desirable if even this provision had not been inserted, as it still fell short of the full sovereignty of the Republic, but since the right remained to appoint envoys and consuls abroad, Krüger, Du Toit, and Smit apparently saw no overriding objection, to the extent that they even sent a request from the Albemarle Hotel on February 27 to Lord Derby, to immediately apply this new provision to the negotiations of the South African Republic with Portugal. As is well known, the Portuguese port of Lourenço Marques was of great importance to the Transvaal, and Krüger therefore insisted that the closer relationship with Portugal be regulated without delay. §4. The gold mines. So a better future seemed to be dawning, when unexpectedly the discovery of the gold mines in the vicinity of Johannesburg came to disturb the peace. Immediately this gave rise to an invasion of English fortune-seekers, who settled in such large numbers in Johannesburg and even in Pretoria, that their number was soon estimated by the English Commission at 26,000, even exceeding the number of the Boers themselves.

—- Page 420 —-

Page 421—-
THE GOLD MINES. 405 Secondly, the gold mines weakened the moral and serious character of the Boers. They suddenly found themselves showered with riches, of which they had never dreamed before. No longer was it the case that the South African Republic, with a population of not even half a million, had an annual budget of around 50 million guilders. Advances were being handed out in all directions. Almost everyone was enriching themselves. And the pious spirit that had characterized the first years after the crossing of the Vaal was weakening. The British Government, now under Chamberlain, who was excellently informed about this, then opened negotiations in Bloemfontein, which, as everyone now sees in hindsight, were aimed at provoking a conflict. The British Government demanded the right to vote for the many Britons who had settled in the Transvaal, a demand that Krüger could not accept, as this would have transformed the Volksraad itself into an English Parliament. So one gradually felt all over Africa that it was heading for an eruption. That came. With a courage and bravery that enthused all of Europe, the Transvaal tried to fend off the attack. America could have averted the blow, and when I was in Washington in 1898 for an audience, I succeeded in discussing this point with Mackingly, but Chamberlain had just gone to America in the autumn of ‘98 to avert any danger from that side. The dispute had to be fought out. The Boers shot magnificently. The English infantry was defeated on all points against them. In the end, an army had to be sent to the Cape, four times larger than the entire armed force of the Boers. For a year it even seemed as if England would have to withdraw. But in the end, danger came from the Kaffir. The Messrs. Fisher and Wolmarans, who had come to The Hague to provoke a suspension of the war, openly admitted that their wives and children in the Kaffir country could no longer be protected. They pressed me in every way, in my then capacity as prime minister, to make an attempt to intervene with the English Government. The Cabinet decided to do so in January 1902 on my advice. Lord Lansdowne had previously let me know that he could say nothing until they had understood each other, but that he would gladly see this proposal for submission. This led to the necessary steps being taken on our side.

—- Page 422 —-

406 THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE. At the conference in Vereeniging in the Transvaal, the English Government then received the two delegates. At the time, one had to yield in the Transvaal. The struggle could no longer be sustained. And so in 1902 not only the South African Republic, but also the Orange Free State, was driven off the political stage, something that was even more shameful for the Orange Free State, as this State had possessed full sovereignty and had only come to the aid of the South African Republic out of brotherly love.

§5. Bitter end. One of the most beautiful illusions I had ever cherished went under here. Unscrupulous politicians had triumphed with armed superiority. Also a lesson for our own country, never to imagine that we would be safe under the shield of international law. A law, like international law, without written law, without a court or executor, and thus reckoning with God, not yielding except to violence. General Den Beer Poortugaal rightly pointed out in The Forum, an American monthly, how bitterly sad the 20th century had begun with what was happening in South Africa. The scene that South Africa offers, he writes in the September issue of 1901, p. 15, “is a shame for Christianity. A cruel war is being waged, in which two small, peaceful, religious Protestant Republics are being destroyed by fire and sword by mighty Protestant England, who has always thought herself at the summit of civilization.” From the English side itself, a brand was placed on Chamberlain’s attitude. Almost everyone, says Mr. Den Beer Poortugaal, recognized that England was in the wrong. Proud in his “Diary” admitted that “the seizure of the Diamond Fields by the English was a great wrong in which the British Government was shamefully imposed upon. Yet Great Britain still continues to choose the path of injustice.” My old teacher Robert Fruin went even further. He proved in his “A word from Holland on the Transvaal question,” already in 1881, that Shepstone, when he proceeded to the annexation of the Transvaal, had acted in violation of international law.

—- Page 423 —-

THE BITTER END.407 had violated his letter of commission. With regard to the Sand River Treaty, Shepstone’s mandate is stated on p. 5: “That no order of annexation should be issued by you with respect to any district, territory or state, unless you be satisfied, that the inhabitants thereof desire to become our subjects.” As the matter now stands, the annexation of the Transvaal in 1877, which Fruin says was also nothing other than “an act of perfidy and injustice.” The German Emperor felt this when he sent Krüger his well-known telegram, which, however daring, still pleads for his noble heart. I myself gave vent to the bitterness that also filled my heart in the Revue des deux mondes, February 1900, and I ended, according to Fletcher’s translation, by writing: “So long as the Lioness of the Transvaal, surrounded by her young lions, shall roar against England from the heights of Drakenburg, never will the Boers be definitively subdued.” A.E. Fletcher translated my piece into English, this translation reached its 16th edition. Mr. Elout gave a Dutch translation of it. A separate French edition was published. A Swedish translation saw the light in Stockholm. In short, it became clear that something was reverberating abroad of what had provoked me to protest. A horror has been committed, which casts an especially dark shadow over the Law of Nations, because the Transvaal war coincided precisely with the first Peace Conference. Rightly, Bornhak (p. 234) shows that even in the 1881 treaty there could have been no question of suzerainty. With suzerainty, he observes, sovereignty is divided into two parts. Here, on the other hand, there were two completely independent States that by treaty agreed that the foreign relations of one of the two States would run through the other. Even now there are still quite a few noble figures in South Africa who refuse to settle with Botha in the new state of affairs. They feel aggrieved. They are deeply convinced that the Law of Nations itself has here suffered a defeat to be deeply regretted. And just for that reason, the act of the Dutch Government in not inviting the delegates of the South African Republic and the Orange Free State to the Peace Conference is still something against which all that is in me revolts.

—- Page 424 —-

THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE
§6. Peace Conference. It is indeed very well understood how one can formally defend this action. One can say that participation in such a Conference is an act of foreign policy. Transvaal’s foreign policy was placed under England’s hands by Article IV of the Convention of February 27, 1889. Our Government could thus have invited the two States, but they could never have complied with this invitation without bringing the correspondence on this matter to the knowledge of the English Government, and naturally, England, especially at that moment, would never have allowed that communication to pass without a definite refusal. Something to which the question is immediately attached, whether our Government did not then do better, not to expose the South African Republics to such painful treatment. It is even possible that the English Government, foreseeing the difficulty in which it could become entangled, had already given our Government to understand beforehand that it would never be able to grant its consent. But even if this can be heard very well formally, it does not seem to me to defend this attitude. This already appears immediately, if one pays attention to the decisive distinction between the Orange Free State and the South African Republic. For between these two, the cases were by no means equal. The limiting provision of the Convention of 1887 did not apply to the Orange Free State. The Orange Free State had already for years had its Consul-General in The Hague, Mr. Mulder, who dealt freely with us. If, therefore, our Government had at least summoned the Orange Free State and only left the South African Republic at home, there would still have been something to say for its attitude, at least formally, but this has not happened. The Orange Free State has also been left at home and neglected, and neglected at a critical moment when all of Europe was already beginning to foresee what evil plans of violence England was harboring against the Republic. But even if we admit that there was a certain formal distinction between these two Republics, it must still be contradicted if it is claimed that Article IV of the Convention of 1884 made it impossible for our Government to send an envoy

—- Page 425 —-

THE PEACE CONFERENCE. 409 To admit Krüger to the first Peace Conference. The article does not say in so many words that it was unlawful for the South African Republic to enter into negotiations with any foreign Power, unless Great Britain had previously granted its consent. Article IV only speaks of the “conclusion” of a treaty or alliance, not of negotiations and discussions, and negotiations and discussions aimed at the conclusion of such a treaty or alliance are certainly not precluded. According to the clear letter of the article, the South African Republic was entirely free to enter into consultation and discussion with any foreign power, even with a view to the conclusion of a treaty or alliance. It was even entirely free to allow such discussions to lead to the drafting of such a treaty. One could even continue the negotiations on this until an agreement was reached. And only when it had come so far, and one was about to definitively conclude the mutual obligations, then the approval of the British Government had to be obtained, and otherwise the ratification would not be permitted. The request for approval simply took place by communicating or discussing the draft act, which had been fully prepared in advance and, as already noted above, the sensitivity of the Republic had also been taken into account in this, in that the declaration of approval was not even demanded as a requirement, but the approval would be tacitly granted if no protest followed within 6 months of the submission of the draft. It follows, therefore, that the South African Republic, by virtue of the treaty of 1884, was just as free as any other power: 1° to establish legations and consulates with whichever power it wished, as Mr. M. Beelaerts van Blokland had long acted as the Republic’s envoy to various governments; 2° to enter into negotiations on any subject whatsoever with all foreign governments, on any subject that the President, in agreement with the Volksraad, considered suitable for this; 3° that these negotiations might also aim at the conclusion of a treaty or alliance; 4° that the Republic, entirely apart from England, was free to do so.

—- Page 426 —-

410THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.
So far, the process could advance to the point that ultimately the treaty or alliance, as provisionally accepted, was entirely ready in draft form and required only ratification; and 5° that it must first, and only then through a simple communication, be brought to the knowledge of the English Government, and finally that they could only then show resistance, if they could not consent to its establishment. The purpose of this provision in Article IV was also clearly no other than to give the English Government the opportunity to remain informed of what was going on, in order to prevent any treaty or alliance from being concluded that could harm England’s position or England’s interests. This must be emphasized. Had the intention of this Article IV been to subject the South African Republic as of inferior order to the suzerainty of Great Britain, then naturally a form would have had to be chosen, like the Convention of 1881, which stipulated that all foreign relations of the Transvaal would run through the British Government. By virtue of what was agreed upon in 1881, there was no separate republic called the Transvaal for the foreign powers. They then had to deal with England alone. In 1884, on the other hand, the qualification of suzerainty was entirely dropped, no Resident was appointed, the right of legation and consulate was granted to the Republic, freedom of negotiation with all powers was granted, and only notification of the draft of the alliance was required. Just as in an alliance between two great powers, it can be stipulated that the one will not enter into a more specific alliance of a certain nature without informing the other. The only difference then comes down to the fact that England ultimately retained the right of veto.

§7. Even stronger. To top it off, this reservation was even excluded with respect to any treaty or alliance with the Orange Free State; something that shows two things, namely, 1° that the Orange Free State, which our Government did not even invite in 1899, was not bound by this very lax reservation either, and 2° that the South African Republic and the Orange Free State were regarded as fully sovereign states, with whom England had to deal on an equal footing.

—- Page 427 —-

EVEN STRONGER was the bond that was formed; and 2° that the reserves created were intended for no other purpose than to ward off dangers to England from the European side. One must remember, however, that already in the last years before the end of the 19th century, the tension between England and Germany had risen very high, and there had already been more than one rumor of possible German expeditions to the Cape. The entente with France had not yet been established at that time either. Hence, England had repeatedly harbored fears of European interference in Cape affairs. With this in mind, it was now imperative for England to leave no means untried to stay abreast of what was going on. One had to remain on guard against unwanted surprises. Since there could now be no talk of a Suzerainty right in Art. IV, and the wording of this article was as unobtrusive as possible, the provision in that Article IV can only be interpreted as a safeguard for England’s own security, which it deemed it could not allow to slip away. This interpretation of the article is all the more correct when one recalls what inspired the Gladstone Cabinet, and Lord Derby in particular, in 1884. The overrunning of Spitskop had left a tremendous impression in England, and in general it can be said that the liberal Cabinet condemned all that had happened in 1877 and thereafter. The negotiations at the time, which were conducted in London from November 1883 to February 1884 and which I was able to follow as a participant in all their details, had not only a benevolent, but even an almost apologetic, friendly character. The struggle was over Swaziland and the West-North border rights, but all discussions about the independence of the Republic almost came to a close without interruption. Both in the English Blue Book and in the complete edition of the diplomatic documents of the South African Republic, one finds proof of this on page after page. The liberal Cabinet refused to move along the line of the previous administration. Gladstone and Derby broke with this evil past, and when Krüger left London in February 1887, he took a truly heartfelt farewell from the English Cabinet, in the full conviction that the Republic no longer had a rival in England, but a friend.

—- Page 428 —-

THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.
And their ally. As I have already pointed out above, even before Krüger left London, he arranged his excursion to Lisbon with Lord Derby in order to avoid getting into difficulties over Lorenzo Marques. Now, since the Portuguese government at the time was little more than a vassal of England, this obligingness towards Krüger sufficiently proves how clearly he foresaw the future when he left England. That he went too far in this, and that the generous acceptance of Art. IV, as well as the immediate invocation of England’s aid under that article, cannot be entirely exculpated from imprudence with a view to the future, I have already remarked above. But in this the gentlemen of the South African Republic acted in too good faith. In any case, it is evident that the Majuba had done its work, that the liberal Cabinet had bidden farewell to Shepstone’s tactics, and that now complete confidence had set in. And this being so, what more valid reason could there be than that our Government, apparently under completely perverse inspiration, decided neither to invite the Orange Free State nor the South African Republic to the first Peace Conference?

§8. The Non-Admission to the Conference. As has been shown in an irrefutable manner, there was at least no objection to inviting the Orange Free State. As for the omission with respect to the Orange Free State, the attempt at exculpation has never even been tried. But also with regard to the South African Republic, it is undeniable that there is not a syllable or a letter in Art. IV of the treaty of 1884 that would have precluded the appearance of its delegates at the Peace Conference. The invitation to the Peace Conference, however, involved nothing more than a request to come and deliberate. No prior treaty or covenant had been entered into by whom it came. It was a Conference to converse, to negotiate, and to consult together. To what extent these discussions would lead to a treaty or covenant was not to be determined beforehand. Insofar as even in 1899 some draft treaty was established at the Conference,

—- Page 429 —-

THE NON-ADMISSION TO THE CONFERENCE.
This had so little binding character that the admission or non-admission to the accord was left to the discretion of the powers, and this with the result that there are still some who did not do so. But even if we were to stretch the point in question so far as to say that Art. IV of the Convention of 1884 even prevented the free accession to the treaty of 1899, what then? Then the South African Republic could have expressed its assent, in expectation of the cooperation that England would have to grant for this. If now England itself went along with the Treaty of 1899, what would it have mattered then to make the same acceptance impossible for the South African Republic? And suppose that Chamberlain, with an eye to his tyrannical plans, would have been very capable of this, what other consequence would this have had than that the delegates of the South African Republic, sitting at the Conference, would have abstained from participating in the vote on this point. However far one may stretch the meaning and intention of Art. IV, it can never and never be deduced from it that the South African Republic was not, entirely apart from England’s approval, freely and internationally authorized to participate in the discussions on the Peace among the peoples at the Conference, by also letting its delegates express their opinion, and, without awaiting England’s subsequent disapproval, to take a preliminary part in the decisions of the Conference. From whichever side one looks at the case, it remains therefore completely inexplicable on what grounds our Government at the time could have decided to leave the two Republics of South Africa, as if they did not belong there, at home. That our Government was bound up to a certain extent with the insights of the Tsar can be admitted, the Conference originated from him and the Government of the Netherlands merely lent itself to the further elaboration of his generous plan. Suppose then that Russia of its own accord had protested against the invitation of the two Republics, then at least something would have been understood that one did not immediately strike hard, although even then it could have been pointed out the close ethnological kinship of the Netherlands with the Boers and the difficulty for our Government, in view of the strong sympathy that this kinship had already aroused at the time in our country, to ignore the participation of the Boers in the Conference.

—- Page 430 —-

414 THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.
to defy the national sentiment. However, this has never been asserted in public, and it also remains my conviction that no protest has gone out from the Russian side. The position taken by our Government at the time is also difficult to explain from any other motive than that they were threatened from the English side, and indeed by giving to understand that England would not appear at the Conference if they were forced to sit in council with the delegates of the Republics.

§9. The Sentiment in England.
In England, almost everything had already been prepared for the destruction of the Republics. The two debates over Swaziland and the voting rights of the more than 20,000 English had already kept Pretoria in constant tension. This was known in England. It was disapproved of from the liberal side in England itself. When I returned from America via London in December 1898, I discussed the already pending matter, among others, with John Morley, and even this otherwise so cautious and prudent statesman gave me the assurance that Chamberlain would not dare to carry out his evil plans in any case. He had even discussed it with Chamberlain that very morning, when I spoke to him in the evening. But supposing that our Government at the time had stood before the dilemma of either leaving the South African Republic alone, or driving out England and thus causing the Conference to fail, then it still remains a very serious question whether the choice made was a good one. Why, in this critical moment, was it not rather agreed to postpone the Conference until this point of contention had also been resolved? Not yielding on this point could have led to the unmasking of England’s plans. And so, even before the war broke out, a common opinion against England could have formed, from which even Chamberlain would have shrunk back. As it now went, a kind of free pass was issued to Great Britain, as it were, to carry out its domineering plans in South Africa. To what extent the gold and diamond magnates of the Rand had a hand in this evil game cannot be determined, but the fact remains that the Dutch Government, by acting as it did, has greatly weakened the at the time so strongly threatened position of both Republics, and under English threat has allowed the idea to gain ground that Europe would ultimately acquiesce in everything, and even the Netherlands would consent. Something that must be emphasized all the more, since it was also very well known in The Hague that the Cape had, as it were, been taken from us as a reward for our proven services, that England’s Governor had forced our Boers to retreat behind the Vaal, that the Sand River Treaty had recognized Transvaal as completely free and independent, that this treaty had been violated and nullified in 1877 in an unforgivable manner, and that the treaty of 1884 could not provide the justification for our attitude, which was so encouraging of misery. At the Conference, no power appeared with a view to its own profit. There was no question of any concessions being granted to any country, in distinction from other countries. Nor was there even for a moment any question of the Orange Free State or the South African Republic being able to enter into any treaty or covenant with any country whatsoever in order to secure the promised special rights for themselves. At the Conference, nothing would come up for discussion other than what could serve to avert the danger of war, to promote friendly understanding, and to ensure the establishment of an international peace. And while every diplomat and statesman at that moment knew that the first war that threatened was about to break out in South Africa, and thus the vigilance of the Conference had to be directed very particularly against this war, the South African Republics were declared unworthy to even participate in the great plans for peace. The result, which is just as offensive to the Christian spirit as to every humane feeling, has been no other than that scarcely had one returned home from the Conference, or England threw itself upon its victim, and before three years had passed, neither of the two Republics existed any longer.

§10. Personal Relationship.
I could not leave this painful incident unmentioned here. From 1882

—- Page 431 —-

PERSONAL RELATIONSHIP. 415
onwards, I have been in personal contact with the leaders of the South African Republics, and I have always been struck by the fact that they were men of a high moral character, who were deeply imbued with the spirit of their people and who, in their political actions, were guided by a sincere desire to maintain the independence of their country. I have never met with any indication that they were driven by a lust for power or by a desire for aggrandizement. On the contrary, they have always impressed me as men who, in the face of the great difficulties that surrounded them, were animated by a noble patriotism and who, in the defense of their country’s rights, showed a self-sacrificing spirit that commands respect. And when I now see how these men, who were so highly esteemed by all who knew them, have been overwhelmed by the superior power of England, and how their republics, which had been recognized as fully sovereign and independent, have been destroyed, then I cannot but feel the deepest sympathy for their fate and the most sincere indignation at the way in which they have been treated. For the South African Republics were not only the bulwark of the Dutch element in South Africa, but also the last stronghold of a political system that was based on the principle of Sphere Sovereignty, and that stood in sharp contrast to the centralizing tendencies of modern states. Their destruction is therefore not only a blow to the Dutch population of South Africa, but also a serious setback for the cause of political decentralization and the recognition of the rights of smaller nationalities. And this is why I believe that the fate of the South African Republics is a matter of concern not only for the Dutch, but for all who value political freedom and the right of smaller peoples to maintain their independence.

—- Page 432 —-

THE SOUTH AFRICAN REPUBLIC AND THE ORANGE FREE STATE.
I have always been personally involved in this mighty struggle. I know it through and through, and am therefore entitled to pass judgment on it. And although it does not occur to me to suspect the statesmen who sat in our Cabinet during the first Peace Conference of not feeling anything for Transvaal, or to think that they would not have welcomed the delegates of the two Republics to the Conference as gladly as I myself, yet the sad outcome of this painful history weighs heavily on the question of whether the decision then taken by them was not lacking in governing power, and whether the downfall of the Dutch element at the Cape was not hastened. When I came into the Cabinet in July 1901, I managed to persuade my colleague from the Foreign Office to do what was still possible at the time. What was then done by the Cabinet was entirely in line with what Wolmarans, Fisher and De Wet desired. And if, according to their expectation, the treaty that came about as a result of the effort made by our Cabinet with the British Cabinet at least prevented the extermination of the Boer race, and in the future the more vigorous re-emergence of that race is not entirely excluded, I will thank God that at least something has been achieved. And now that the resistance of many against Botha’s guilty complicity in the great war of 1914 already shows that a twig is still shooting from the cut-off trunk, the hope for a not entirely severed future is still alive. For since I wrote the above in the spring of 1914, the great war broke out, and the Cape was also involved in it, as a result of which the Government in London expressed its desire that the German East African colony might be conquered from the Cape. Botha then lent himself as head of the Cape Government to this, but while the necessary for this campaign was being prepared, resistance broke out in Transvaal and the Orange Free State, and the Law placed itself at the head of it, apparently guided by the never extinguished desire to revive the old Republic. Maritz played his own game, but under De la Rey, De Wet and Fourie, even not entirely without the support of the Duke, a small uprising broke out, with the sad result that Botha marched against the Law, defeated and imprisoned him.

—- Page 433 —-

TWO DIFFERENT FUNDAMENTAL VIEWS.
The painful regret that this course of events has also awakened in the Netherlands. Already when in 1902 Botha, De Wet and Fischer discussed the interests of South Africa in my room, it was already noticeable how Botha stood much more sharply against General De Wet. Since then, Botha has gone even further from his past, married an English woman, changed his sympathies, and is now ultimately the man who may have extinguished the last spark of hope for the revival of South Africa. That it would come this far with him, I myself would never have dared to think in 1902. Yet he lost. Not he, but De Wet remains the hero of history. Smuts has been Botha’s evil genius. Fortunately, our whole country has felt what disgrace has been inflicted on the best memories of Transvaal and the Orange Free State by Botha in serving as a vassal for England in a war that, according to Asquith’s assertion, was undertaken to protect the independence of the “small nations” from destruction. Chapter XII STATE AND CHURCH
§1. Two different fundamental views.
The no less important question of State and Church is directly connected to the conclusion to which the tenth chapter brought us. This cannot be fathomed by anyone who sees the Church only as a spiritually intended outward manifestation of the faith in Jesus Christ as the leader of our spiritual life. For then the Church stands entirely apart from the practical community life, just as it requires regulations and care in society and the State. There are then two entirely different and easily distinguishable domains, on the one side the State and on the other the Church.

—- Page 434 —-

The Church can only move within the limits set by the State. The Church is there only for the mysticism of the heart, and everything else in our life that calls for order and rule falls under the scepter of the State. This is the reason why the Church, insofar as it appears in practical social life, has to submit itself to the system of the State. Just as the logic of Freemasonry or a Spiritualist association or an artistic guild can pursue a goal that is subjective in character, and can therefore act in complete freedom, nevertheless, as soon as they come to expression in social life, they are bound by the law of the State, so also the Church, although it may claim full freedom within its own sphere as the mere embodiment of what moves the mysticism of the heart, yet as soon as it manifests or reveals itself through procession, bell-ringing, church building, collection, marriage blessing, or anything else, on the civil terrain, then it also falls under the general rules and owes an equally unlimited obedience to the authorities. Against this view, however, stands in principle everyone who in the Church sees an institution, not of men, but of Christ Himself, who confesses Christ as the King of the Kingdom of God, and who from the Holy Scriptures believes with all his heart that the Kingdom of God, which awaits us under Christ as our King, will embrace not only spiritual elements, but, on the ground of Holy Scripture, will just as decidedly have a physical as a psychical character, and will therefore not only include the spiritual, but just as certainly also the social life, and what we call the life of the State. The Church, as it now sojourns on earth, is a prefiguration of that coming world-empire; it stands in direct connection with it. Hence, there is an undeniable connection between the Church, as the prefiguration of the coming world-empire, and the State, which now forms the bond for the external social life of men. The Church does not appear in a human society that is by nature under the control of the State, but carries within itself the germ of the all-embracing world-empire that will one day replace all State, and take over its task. It is therefore completely incorrect if one sees in the State the palace in which the Church has no more than a side-room.

—- Page 435 —-

TWO FUNDAMENTAL PERSPECTIVES.
The State is little more than the scaffolding, erected on the construction site where the Church is busy laying the foundation for the palace in which Christ the King will one day establish His throne. The State is a surgical aid to help human society in its bound condition, and the Church provides the power for the restoration of the original life-connection that was once lost, but will one day return. Hence, as the Consummation approaches, the present State will completely vanish, just as a cripple discards his crutches once both his legs are fully healed; and conversely, the Church, when the new age dawns, will emerge more glorious than ever in her everlasting dominion. The State passes away when the struggle is over, forever and for all time; and not the State, but the Church, is the source from which the dawn will rise for the eternal existence of the peoples. Because many scholars of the State have not elevated themselves to this revelation of Holy Scripture, but have rather seen in this entire Scriptural representation little more than the dream of an enthusiast, it could not be otherwise than that the relationship between Church and State had to be completely distorted by them. The State is the garment that is cast aside, while in the Church lies the germ of the new body that is in the process of being formed in the womb of life. Whoever, then, mistakes that garment for the body, can naturally not allow the germ of the new life to come to its right. Hence the struggle over the significance of the Church of Christ between the aforementioned scholars of the State and the believing thinkers can be nothing other than a principled one. And though one may generally succeed in finding a modus vivendi that at least makes possible the progress of life for both State and Church, the false conception of the State to which one has become accustomed can do nothing other than degrade the Church to a private association for the satisfaction of certain mystical needs, to which one then grants a certain freedom of movement, but always bound by certain rules established by the State; while conversely the scholar of the State, who sees the realization of the Kingship of Christ as the ultimate goal, can and may do nothing other than place the Church in his esteem far above the State, even with respect to that part of human society that is currently entrusted to the care of the State.

—- Page 436 —-

420 CHURCH OF THE STATE.
§2. The Resurrection. The resurrection fundamentally refutes this plea. If our personal death is a complete and final separation from life in the visible and tangible, so that only the soul continues to exist after our body and all that was connected with the body has ceased to be for us, then there is no resurrection from the dead, and the mighty Passover applies not only to Christ, but above all to what is called human. Those who have died have then no more to do with life on this earth. Their existence on this earth has then definitively ended. Their continued existence has therefore no more interest for this visible world, and this earth has no more interest in them. Those who die go away from us and never return to this world. Moreover, just as they died away, in the end this world itself also dies away. This may last for as many centuries as one may guess, but the end will come when this earth too is consumed and destroyed by the world-fire. Then nothing remains, unless, what one then does not deny, but also dares not affirm, the souls of those who have died, if at least these souls have continued to exist and as spirits have continued their existence in a spiritual sphere. Many do not even accept this. But even if we grant that our spiritual existence will have a continued existence, in any case this will be a mystery in the invisible. We know nothing about it. With our existence, as it is now, it will have nothing whatsoever to do. The State, which regulates our existence here on this earth, can therefore in no way concern itself with it. If there is an eternal spirit-realm, then this whole matter lies completely outside the State. The State has nothing to do with it. It cannot have anything to do with it, for it lies entirely beyond its reach. It can help the believers here by, as far as can be observed, allowing them to attach themselves to a Church to satisfy their spiritual needs, and even offer assistance for this; but the sovereignty, the setting of the rule for life, and the maintenance of the law against everyone, remains its own. The Church too makes no exception in this respect. Rather, there is every reason to be doubly vigilant and cautious towards the Church in this regard.

—- Page 437 —-

THE RESURRECTION.421 It is clear that more than one scholar of the state has taken the vague position of statesman or wielder of state power, in order to judge the Church in this way, and not otherwise, since one cannot recognize it at the same time in its eternal and in its cosmic character. But then, as we remarked, neither has the Passover been celebrated, nor the denial of the resurrection of the dead been taken as a starting point. The separation between the spiritual and the material, between the physical and the psychical, between the temporal and the eternal, is then taken as absolute. With the future of the spiritual, the psychical and the eternal, the Church may concern itself, but the State does not and cannot do this, for there is no resurrection from the dead, and there is therefore also no new value that will shine under the new heaven. All these are mere fantasies. Partly full of high poetry, but a poetry with which the practical statesman has nothing to do. The believers die just like the unbelievers. Their fantasies too go down into the grave with them and perish; or they also evaporate in the crematorium with their corpse into ashes. So there is absolutely nothing to be reckoned with after death. Reality possesses only what is perceptible to us here. The practical statesman does not occupy himself with poetic images. Whether sooner or later this earth perishes in water or even in fire, well, then it is also over. The Church will be just as powerless in the face of this as the State. All self-conceit of the Church, as if it would find an overflowing existence in a tangible future, or could already in this dispensation raise itself on earth to a higher form of being, is the pretension of spiritual self-delusion. Cinematically, we may be able to conjure up such a future before our eyes, but the actual realization of it is a complete absurdity. The dead do not rise, at least not in bodily form. No one who has died receives a new body. Nor is there any talk of a renewal of this earth. In the entire existence of the Church there is not a single element that could have any connection with such a product of fantasy. If the Church finds it good to occupy itself with such fantasies, one can let it go its way. The State cannot forbid any poet from indulging in poetry and dreams, but it dares not, whatever Church it may be, to usurp the grandiose idea that lies in the resurrection.

—- Page 438 —-

Not only to express, but also to want to apply in fact, the sovereignty of the dead, then a higher sense of duty demands that the State resist such excesses with its unchanging majority. There are no dead standing up. With death, it is over. There comes no new value. How then could the Church ever bear a germ from which a new order of things in the visible could arise sooner or later. That the government still tolerates the celebration of the Easter feast and even grants its servants free time on the second day of Easter, also has a completely different significance. This only means that a certain immortality of the soul can still be counted on; that even after death, a purely spiritual continued existence of what is called the soul is not considered absolutely unthinkable; and that the triad of Kant: “God, Virtue and Immortality” is not yet explicitly denied. There is no longer any certainty. Even the agnostic can join in the jubilation, as long as it is only in hope. But, and we emphasize this fully, the public celebration of Easter by the State means in no way that one believes in Christ’s resurrection on the third day; much less still, that one believes in the future resurrection of the dead in a new bodily appearance. One therefore sees clearly that the modern theory on the relationship between State and Church has also arisen from the outright denial of the future resurrection of the dead; that this denial of our resurrection at the same time includes a denial of Jesus’ resurrection; and that it is thus the Divine Person and the Divine Mission of the Christ that is here assaulted for the exaltation of the State. For it is the same earth, it is the same human race on this earth, and it is the same life of our race, which is now placed under the rule of State and Government, and which will one day present itself in its completed form as the Kingdom of Glory, of which the spiritual germ is now already hidden by God Himself in the Church on earth.
§3. Anti-Clericalism. The struggle of the centuries also provides the complete proof that this whole theory, which denies all significance to the Church for the common life, is nothing but a new form of the old anti-clerical struggle, which has always been waged against the Church.

—- Page 439 —-

ANTI-CLERICALISM. The modern State theory cannot stand on a footing of peace with reality if it denies the life of the Church in the State. Is it not true that the very advocates of the modern State theory feel called upon to take up the political struggle against what they call Clericalism, time and again on the terrain of the State? Yet this name of Clericalism is certainly not accurate. Derived from Clergy, it refers to the priestly class, as it took the lead in the Roman Catholic and to some extent also in the Greek Orthodox Church. For the Churches of the Reformation, and especially for the Reformed Churches whose formation proceeded from Calvin, such a Clergy is completely excluded. These Churches rather combat the appearance of a Clergy on principle. Yet in our country too, since the last quarter of this century, one increasingly follows the method of branding the statesmen and electors of Calvinistic origin as “Clericals”; something which shows that the struggle is waged not at all only against a Clergy as such, but against every attempt to assign to the Church a position that grants her a rightful influence on the life of the State. This anti-Clericalism dominates the political struggle in the Netherlands, and no less so in neighboring Belgium and Germany. Now if this crude expression of anti-Clericalism were concerned only with the rejection of certain ecclesiastical claims regarding salaries, widows’ pensions, or the like, one could still argue that its anti-Clericalism meant nothing more than rejecting these claims as unreasonable or excessive. But history also shows us otherwise. The struggle between the so-called Clericals and Anti-Clericals increasingly dominates the entire political sphere. The voters split into two camps that stand opposed to each other across the entire political line. Even where the rights or pretensions of the Church are not at all in question, the Clericals and Anti-Clericals continually confront each other in the great political contest. Entire schools have arisen in the domain of constitutional law, in order to defend or combat these two opposing views, not only at the ballot box, but equally on the scientific field, with all permissible weapons. Whatever presents itself to us as Clerical or Anti-Clerical politics, it pleads its case by no means solely for the realization or rejection of ecclesiastical interests, but covers the entire field of politics, as for what concerns...

—- Page 440 —-

THE CHURCH AS STATE. As for the principles, and as for the measures derived from them. And the advocates of Liberalism feel so extremely well that here it is not about the special rights or material interests of the Church, but how, quite generally, one system is coming to stand against another, which they still constantly set as their main goal, to put the voters, the statesmen and the men of science who move on the Christian standpoint in a state of non-activity, by taking the leadership of affairs out of their hands.

§4. Guilt on both sides. The world empire. It may not be the case that the Church and the State are to be set against each other as two powers of entirely heterogeneous tendency, yet history shows how extremely difficult it is to place both in the right relationship. The cause of this lies in guilt on both sides. It is by no means only the heroes of the State who have restricted the rightful position of the Church, but just as much the persistent attempts from the ecclesiastical side to extend their power beyond the legitimate boundaries. The ancient struggle between Pope and Emperor continued, though in a modified form, even after the Reformation. From the Roman Empire the idea of the world empire had passed down through the Middle Ages as the dominant thought of history. Pacifism now revives this idea in a modified form, but since the 16th century it had fallen more and more into discredit. True, the “Holy Roman Empire” still continues its existence, but so very much in appearance only, that no guidance whatever proceeds from it. Otto Gierke in his Deutsches Privatrecht, second edition, 1902, p. 235, did not express himself too strongly when he wrote: “The Reichspublizisten drag the imperium mundi along as a lifeless phantom.” In the 19th century the inspiration that once emanated from the idea of the world empire had also completely died out. One reckoned with independent States, but the political thought did not any longer penetrate into the unity of our human race. Only now has the study of Pacifism again made us see how absolutely necessary it is to keep the eye fixed on that world empire which is one day to embrace our whole race. Only in so far as the tendency of Natural Law had infused new life into the idea of the world empire, in so far as international law was understood as a law that would hold good in the “Societas gentium,” thus leaving the sovereignty of the several peoples unimpaired, and yet bringing the unity of our race under a tribunal, though a very weak and yet a venerable one.

—- Page 441 —-

GUILT ON BOTH SIDES. THE WORLD EMPIRE. - THE UNIVERSAL CHURCH. 425 If we go back to the latest times of ancient history, we feel how the State and the Church then stood over against each other in a quite different framework than now appears before us. In the political domain the idea of a realm embracing the whole world and thus the whole of our human race then reigned with uncommon power. It is true that outside the Roman Empire there still existed a very large part of the world, both in Asia and in Africa, but this counted for nothing. From those as yet unsubdued fragments no dominant influence went out, and even when the Migration of Peoples thrust whole groups of these peoples upon Europe, they fitted themselves of their own accord into the existing unity, and did not think of introducing a “Sonderbündlerei” of States. The image of the world empire, which in Holy Scripture so constantly comes to the fore with a view to the future, and in the apocalyptic visions even gives the keynote, now seems to a large extent something that can only be conceived in fantasy. In those days, on the contrary, this was the leading thought, not only in poetic representation, but just as much in real politics. One always started from the idea of the World Empire, and one could not well conceive of the binding together of the peoples in any other way.

§5. The universal Church. So it stood in the political domain, and when for the Church of Christ a conception of its essence as absolutely universal was maintained, and in every way it was asserted that even the antithesis between Abrahamites and Pagans had disappeared, so that now there could no longer be Greek or Jew, Scythian or Barbarian, but all must be one in Christ and had actually become so, the idea of the World Empire felt itself even surpassed. The universal imperial idea then at least still recognized certain boundaries in the Barbarians and especially in the East, but the universal Church idea knew of no boundaries at all.

—- Page 442 —-

The Parthians and Scythians may have been outside the Church of Christ, but the Church of Christ even went beyond those outermost boundaries, and as far as this earth is concerned, embraced the entire Cosmos. It is therefore remarkable how broad a place the world-concept occupies in the holy Revelation. Continually in psalms and prophecies, “all the inhabitants of the world” are summoned to serve Jehovah. “The world is mine,” says Jehovah, “and the fullness thereof.” He will not judge only a single people, but “the whole world in righteousness.” Christ Himself testified that His Gospel would be preached to the whole world. He called Himself the light of the world, and charged His disciples to preach His Gospel to the entire world. Dying on the Cross, He would give His life for the whole world. Satan, in his struggle with Christ, is designated as the Ruler of the world. The Mediator brings His offering for the sin of the whole world. God has reconciled the world with Himself in Christ. In 1 John 3:14 it even says that Christ was sent as “the Savior of the world.” And in Revelation 11:15 we hear the language of victory, that now “the kingdoms of the world have become the kingdoms of our God.” Always the same, universal thought, which was attested to us in John 3:16, namely, that God “so loved the world, that He gave His only begotten Son, that whosoever believes in Him should not perish, but have everlasting life.” At that time, therefore, the world-idea, both historically and in the expectation of the peoples, and also in the Revelation, especially in the Mission of Christ, stood in the foreground. And although Mysticism for years, and now also Pacifism, have taken up that world-thought, it cannot be denied that it had fallen all too much in value among the Protestant peoples. With Napoleon, the last world-conqueror seemed to have gone under. Since 1814 one had to deal only with peoples, and in no respect anymore with the idea of empire. And as for the Church, although Réveil and Irenic may have caused our thoughts about the walls of the narrow church to glance beyond, yet as a member of a world-church, almost no one of us felt that way anymore. At the Synod of Dort in 1618, one may have still honored the thought of a world-church, through envoys and delegates from foreign lands,

—- Page 443 —-

THE UNIVERSAL CHURCH. 427 The churches have been called upon, and since then this rich idea has become increasingly impoverished. It was the so-called People’s Church, the national Church, the Church of a particular country, that came to the fore, and with this the lofty ideal that had previously been so powerfully inspiring was abandoned. In this way, an increasingly troublesome contradiction arose between the life environment, both in the political and in the ecclesiastical sphere, and our Christian confession. Our Christian confession demands a return to Paradise, and thereby to the unity of the human race. Even the sacrifice of Christ on Golgotha loses its significance if the unity of our race, the unity in the origin of sin, and thus also the world-unity in the work of redemption, is not maintained. With the abandonment of this unity, our entire Christian confession falls away. But, even though we sang in the Te Deum: “You, eternal Father, exalt all creatures together!”, in reality the idea of unity had completely succumbed to the people’s idea in the political sphere, and in the ecclesiastical domain the People’s Church had taken it upon itself, unintentionally and unwittingly of course, to strike at the very root of our faith. The national type pushed aside the idea of the one human race, and so too the People’s Church pushed aside the idea of the one, universal, Christian world church. Article 27 of our Reformed Confession still confessed it so clearly: “We believe and confess one catholic or universal Church, which is a holy congregation of true Christian believers,” and at the end: “This holy Church is not confined, bound, or limited to a certain place or to certain persons, but is spread and dispersed over the whole world.” And no differently was it attested in the Heidelberg Catechism, in the answer to question 54: “That the Son of God, out of the whole human race, from the beginning to the end of the world, gathers, defends, and preserves for Himself, by His Spirit and Word, in the unity of the true faith, a Church chosen to everlasting life.” But all this has not prevented the idea of the World Church from increasingly giving way under the pressure of circumstances to the idea of the People’s Church; and that even now, otherwise very learned theologians cling to this false notion, without feeling how perversely they are thereby going against the core of our Christian confession. It is therefore constantly necessary that we return from our corrupted circle of thought to the circle of thought that was the starting point of our Christian confession.

—- Page 444 —-

CHURCH OF THE STATE. Previously it had reigned supreme in two ways: first, through the world-idea embodied in the Roman Empire, and second, through the adaptation of the entire appearance of our life and of all history to the cosmological idea. The national idea could triumph mightily in Israel. When Israel succumbed, it was precisely because it had not understood the sacred art of transforming its originally self-national life-thought more and more into the world-idea.
