Under polytheism through the binding of land and people to a national god, under Israel through the binding of the Kingdom to Jehovah, under Christian peoples through the binding of the State itself to the Creator of heaven and earth, under Islam through the binding of the civil state to Allah and his Koran. Never has the Magistrate professed atheism or even materialism. The sovereignty of absolute right is not self-government by and for the people. We are subjects of a ruling Majesty over us. We stand under callings and obligations, even as a whole people. And whoever now appears as an authority factor or as a Servant of the Almighty, the unchangeable fact is and remains that we are not free men but God’s creatures, and that we can only attain to freedom and self-development through the complete recognition and reverence of this authority that rules over us all.

—- Page 476 —-

460 STATE AND CHURCH.
§21. Power of God. Now if in this way the State and Government are bound to God, even though the Church of Christ was and is also outside her interference, the most natural, simplest and most desirable relationship between Church and State arises precisely here. It is not the Church that hands over her Scepter to the Government. In the possession of that Scepter, the Government was in all heathen lands, completely outside the Church. In the days when Christ was on earth, and soon after His Apostles and His Church founded, Christ Himself testified to Pontius Pilate that the power over which the Emperor of Rome and his provincial governor disposed was power given to him by God; and later Romans XIII: 1 ff. presented it in this way, that the then completely heathen power was a power laid by God on the Government. In Christ appears the Reconciler and Savior of souls, but this sphere of particular grace stands by itself, and entirely distinct from it is the sphere of governmental authority. Whether the people over whom the Government received the rule were heathen or Mohammedan or Christian, makes no difference to the magistracy. It was the same kind of authority over which the Government disposed in Babylon and Persia, in Baghdad or Cairo. It possesses in itself all the instruments it needs to take action. It is in itself complete. It will, ruling over a Christian people, be able to count on more submission and be able to call a happier state of the people into being and maintain it, but just as kitchen and laboratory remain one, regardless of whether the persons who have the leadership in them stand in or outside of Christ, so it is also here. Even under heathen princes there were excellent rulers, and many a Christian prince fell short in the fulfillment of his calling. The Government builds and maintains the State on its own terrain, and Common Grace offers it everything it needs for a proper administration. And all that can be demanded of the Government in this regard is that it openly and officially in the conduct of the State administration also makes the people feel that it does not come as a Ruler to maintain the people for its own benefit, but

—- Page 477 —-

POWER FROM GOD. The power ordained by God is to watch over the interests of the people and to honor God in the people. And if one now asks whether the Christian religion should also assert its influence on public life, the answer is: Undoubtedly, just as this was always the case in America; only, this influence must come to expression in the constitutional way. If the people over whom the government must be exercised are a baptized, a Christian people, then it must be asked in what way this people can assert its rights and influence on the government, and then it is the thus indicated way along which the influence of Christ’s church on law and right must work. The people under whose government a government acts may be a low-standing people. If so, then only a slight desired influence will go out from that people on the government. This can go so far that an increase in the people’s influence on the government would lead the whole country to ruin. In such a case, there is only salvation in cutting off the people’s influence and in transforming the people’s spirit through a monarchical or aristocratic government. On the other hand, if the people stand high, as was and is the case in our country, in Switzerland, in Scotland and in the United States, then the influence on the government will be a healthy one, and it is excellent if this influence increases. For a time this was the case in Athens. For more than two centuries this could be said of the Roman State. And after the Christianization of Europe, the Church of Christ succeeded in introducing better morals, raising the character of the people to a higher level, and also working for the good on the government. Under such a constellation, it is therefore highly desirable that the people themselves, as much as possible, come to the word and that the government owes part of its appearance to the support of the people. Where it is good, these two will then also always be seen to go together. If the people win in rich unfolding of their religious, ethical, intellectual and technical power, then almost always a great influence on the government will come to that people. There can of course be a counter-natural relationship, as was the case with us against Alva, but this does not last. Then tension arises. This tension leads to a breach. And as a rule, history then brought this happy outcome, that the tyrant had to bite the dust and the liberation of the people became the happy result.

—- Page 478 —-

462 CHURCH-STATE. The very desirable circumstance for the action of these Colonists was that it had arisen from a more serious religious movement of the 17th century. And that the Christian element among them had known how to assume a very high form, and that in general the influence that went out from the Christian Church in its various formations at the time on the thirteen original states, was as precious as it can be praised, proves history. From this it now followed of itself that the Christian influence constantly spread further in these States, the richer the significance was that the constitutional system assigned to the people’s representation. If the visible Church in any newly Christianized country consists of a people who for the most part still long for the fleshpots of Egypt, then the government of such a Church will do what is feasible to extend its power as a Church over such a crowd, and, in league with the government of the country, try to strengthen its guardianship over the population. This was indeed the actual situation when, at the advent of the Middle Ages, one single Church covered the entire country, the clergy in hard struggle sought to wean the people from their sins and abuses, and the government of the country pulled together with the Church on the same rope.
§22. Christian popular influence. Here in America, on the other hand, one was faced with a completely different situation. There was not one Church, but several Churches had organized themselves side by side with different confessions and forms of government; and in these Churches lived a people who on average stood very high in religious and moral respect. Here, therefore, there could be no talk of a bond between government and Church, for the Churches were not of one mind and purpose, and conversely, such a bond of state and church to keep the people under guardianship was also superfluous. For here the ecclesiastical population had long outgrown such guardianship. And so the most desirable situation then arose, that the greater the influence granted to the people themselves in matters of government, the more the spirit of

—- Page 479 —-

CHRISTIAN INFLUENCE ON THE PEOPLE. 463 Through this, Christ himself became a participant in the government of the people over the entire land. One need only think of the slave war. Never would a church have succeeded in bringing about the abolition of slavery through mere admonition to the authorities. The objections to this abolition were so manifold and far-reaching that no statesman would ever have dared to undertake such a bold venture, relying solely on ecclesiastical admonition. On the contrary, here the Christian spirit of the people gradually managed to inspire almost the entire nation, and a stream of higher purpose came into being, which carried along more and more. From the very bosom of the Christian people, there emanated such a powerful, driving influence on the President and Congress, that in the end they did not shrink back from this war with the South, but threw themselves into the fray. That not every excess was avoided here, is admitted, but throughout all of America, it is now recognized that in the slave war, a demand of Christian compassion and mercy has triumphed, and there is no one who would dare to plead even for a moment in favor of a return to the condition prior to 1863. Lincoln’s proclamation, which on January 1, 1863 freed all slaves, has been the triumph of the Christian religion in the United States. There is no country where the legislature holds a more powerful position than in America, but also there is no ecclesiastical life that asserts itself more decisively in the representative circles than in America. In all of Europe, there is not a single country where the relationship between State and Church is more blessed than in the United States. The government of the country gives God his honor, has nothing to do with ecclesiastical disputes, and moves in a completely free, practical direction, while conversely the Church of Christ, far from obstructing and hindering, on the contrary, in the richest variety, meets the needs of life, sees a place of honor assigned to it throughout the land, is financially independent of anyone, and so boldly influences public opinion and through public opinion influences the President and Congress, that no state church in Europe could even remotely compare with this powerful influence of America’s churches on the life of the people.

—- Page 480 —-

464 STATE AND CHURCH.
§23. Desired Condition. The churches here do not hinder the state in any way, and the state places itself on no point in the way of the church life. The independence and self-sufficiency of both is complete. The growth and flourishing of both corresponds to the most ardent wishes of the pious Americans. No limit is set to the mutual influence of the one on the other. In short, there is literally not a single link in the chain that one would want to file out. The churches in America do not complain about the state, and the state does not complain about the churches. The spirit of Christ can freely reign in the churches and through the churches over the people and through the representation of the people over the state. State and church do not stand in each other’s way at a single point. One hears nothing of strife or conflict. Even the Roman Catholic Church, which was originally drawn up on a completely different line, found opportunity to develop here. One also does not hear of any plan to reform this relationship. Of course, there is also complaint here. A perfect condition is never to be obtained on earth, due to the influence of sin. Abuse is to be condemned from all sides. But all this touches the details, touches the implementation and application of the system, and touches personal affairs. In the state, America also saw the party life emerge. In the group of Republicans and in the group of Democrats, the aftereffects of earlier traditions have continued, and new evils have come to the surface. Even among the Republicans, who were once so high and so pure in politics, an economic one-sidedness has arisen that has even led to their defeat. But all this applies to oscillations and changes in life, not the system itself. In the religious life, too, a slide towards modern errors has all too often been observed. Especially evolution has loosened much that was long established. But these too are just illnesses that for a moment disturb the normal state, and through it all, the high word of the confession of the Lord God remains. If one also applies the always valid rule here, that a sharp distinction must be made between what is normal and what is abnormal, then one must acknowledge that the relationship between State and Church in America is as blessed as can be desired on earth.

—- Page 481 —-

DESIRED STATE. The rule is required, and what appears abnormal may be confessed without a trace of hesitation, that no relationship between Church and State can be conceived that better answers the ideal set forth in the Gospels than what America presents to our view. Almost everywhere else, and especially in Europe, one feels so keenly, with what indignation the Christ, should He appear among us, would condemn the shameful condition of His Church, and one also wonders what terrain the Christ, were He to return to us, would find where His spirit could move freely and unhindered, that it is beyond any doubt, in our deepest conviction, that in no land on earth would the relationship between Church and State place less hindrance in the way of Christ than in the United States, not least because the irreconcilable bitternesses that so constantly disfigure the church life in our own country are almost unknown in America. If in all Christian lands the relationship of Church and State were to become what it is in fact in America, scarcely any wish would remain unfulfilled, and the Churches would have full power to exert their influence and blessing on the life of the people. Something from which we may immediately infer that what in the New Testament is presented to us as the image of Christ’s Church, with regard to the Government, is almost realized in America. To the national Church in Israel our argument will return of itself shortly, but let us set this subject aside for the time being, and ask ourselves how the emerging Christian Church presented itself in the New Testament, and then we find nothing else than local Churches, mostly already under the governance of elders, to whom deacons were added, and further led to a certain height by Apostles. Of an organization that unites the various local Churches into one and enables them to act jointly, hardly anything can be discerned. The only thing one can appeal to here is the assembly of which Acts XV informs us, and which is commonly called the Synod of Jerusalem. Yet in this assembly there is no mention of the delegates of all the Churches already founded being summoned to the Convention. Only the Church at Antioch had sent Paul and Barnabas to Jerusalem, to win the approval of the other Apostles.

—- Page 482 —-

These two Delegates then turned to the Congregation, to the Apostles, and to the Elders. We read that specifically James and Peter acted in an official capacity. The request that Paul and Barnabas made was solely to obtain a pronouncement on whether it was permissible to baptize and incorporate converts from the Gentiles without prior circumcision. This was then discussed, not in a Convention of Delegates, but in an assembly of the Church of Jerusalem. In that assembly there were 1) the apostles, 2) the elders, and 3) the entire Congregation. (See v. 22). In this assembly, the fundamental question was debated and decided in the spirit of Paul, and this not by a majority vote, but by unanimous consent. Of this decision, Judas and Silas then personally communicated to the Church in Antioch, and this in a letter that began with the words: “The apostles and the elders and the brothers, to the brothers who are of the Gentiles in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia, greetings,” and in which it was affirmed: “For it has seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay on you no greater burden.” Under the leadership of the apostles, therefore, a broadly constituted ecclesiastical assembly, but not in the spirit of what is called in America and Scotland the general assembly of all the Churches. Nor was it Paul, with his authority as founder and apostle, who cut the knot of Judaism, but the Church of Jerusalem alone that set about making a decision, and to this Antioch submitted. As an apostle, Paul continues to exercise a certain authority as the founder of Churches in the local Churches, but otherwise each local Church acts as an independent organization. In each Church, the offices are independently established and filled. Preaching takes place. Two sacraments are administered. Discipline is exercised. Collections are taken for impoverished Churches. But so little did these Churches then still seek healing through financial government support, that even the apostle Paul could write: “that no church entered into partnership with me in giving and receiving.” Philippians 4:15. Free-will offerings were placed in his hands or sent to him, but otherwise the apostle, sometimes by working until late at night as a tentmaker’s assistant, provided for his own support. Above all, he claimed no other authority for himself, even as an apostle, than over the Churches he himself had founded, and certainly not over the Churches in Jerusalem or Judea.

—- Page 483 —-

DESIRED STATE. 467 In general, the newly established Churches live in local independence and without a coercive bond, only in brotherly communion with other Churches. The Government has nothing to say over them. They honor the Government as the servant of God in civil and political life, but in the Churches, mention is made of the Government only in the prayers, which are also offered for the heathen Governments. It can then also be safely said that if one seeks an example of church life in our time that most resembles the church image sketched for us in the New Testament, one will not be able to observe it in Europe, but almost solely in America, where the apostolic authority that, according to the Acts and the Epistles of Paul, went forth personally at that time, is now represented in the writings of the New Testament, or if one wishes, in the entire Bible. Undoubtedly, in the New Testament all the local Churches together form the spiritual background of the one communion of the saints, which exists before God in organic unity as the body of Christ. Christ is the Head not of some local congregations, but of the Church as a whole. In Christ, this is the Church “without spot or wrinkle” (Eph. 5:27). The distinction between the local congregations, to which the hypocrites also belonged, and the “Church of the living God,” to which only God’s regenerated children are to be reckoned, remains, and it is this distinction that Calvin in particular sought to clarify by unraveling the visible and invisible Church. What lives and works on the spiritual background, however, is not to be judged in connection with the life of the State. We will therefore set this aside. If, on the other hand, one confines oneself to the Churches in their outward appearance, as they manifest themselves in the State, then there is no stronger resemblance to the New Testament church image, in whatever country, than what the church life in America would reflect. It is also to this New Testament church image that Calvin has strictly adhered in sketching the architectural plan for the new church building that he undertook. It is true that when he himself had to put it into practice, he did not succeed in realizing this image in its pure outlines. The ecclesiastical data he found did not allow him to do so.

—- Page 484 —-

468 CHURCH AND STATE.
not to. History also stood in the way of his ideal. But the outcome shows that when entirely new conditions arose in America, which were not weighed down by the highly questionable legacy of history, the fundamental idea that Calvin had theoretically sketched out—”the free Church in the free State”—naturally emerged, which could only take on its pure form in this new world. This now makes what the New Testament foreshadows, and what is found among the Presbyterians in America, speak of a surprising harmony, both between the churches themselves and as regards the relationship between Church and State.

§24. Actual Resistance.
If one now asks how it is that nevertheless in almost all the European Reformed Churches this original line has almost entirely deviated, then, as a result of the investigation undertaken, it now stands firm that two factors, which wonderfully fit together, have almost accidentally covered each other. These two factors are, in the first place, the then prevailing condition of the Churches when they went over to the Reformation, and in the second place the Old Testament character of many ecclesiastical conceptions. One did not stand free before the Government. The rule had long been ingrained that the Government had to support the Church. Hence, from the Roman Catholic side, the constant obligation was imposed on the Government to maintain the Roman Catholic organization against the emerging Reformation, if necessary with a strong arm. This led to the great decisions either having to fall on the battlefield or be enforced by the scaffold. Now the Reformation side could only take the position of victim or martyr in this, and the Anabaptists, as will become clear later, initially also considered themselves called to accept this completely passive position. The Calvinists, on the other hand, did not think so. However far they pushed passive resistance, they soon saw that they could not persist in this non-acting attitude, and so in Switzerland, as in France, and no less with us, than in Scotland, they soon came to the conviction that, in the face of the power of the Roman Catholic authorities, they had to set the power of the Reformed authorities as much as possible on their side. As soon as then a turning point was observed, and the chance stood open to convert the Government itself from the Roman Catholic to the Calvinist standpoint, they pressed for this or went over to it themselves. It was fixed for them that without the intervention of a Reformed Government on their side, the Roman Catholic powers would gradually crush the entire Reformation. If the Netherlands, England and the German Protestant princes had not also made their power felt on the battlefield, Austria, France and Spain would have carried out the cruel begun extermination and eradication of Protestantism. One therefore stood before the choice of either letting oneself be executed Anabaptist-style on the scaffold, or of setting another governmental power against the ruling governmental power, and this last option the Calvinist chose.

—- Page 485 —-

469 ISRAEL’S EXAMPLE.
§25. Israel’s Example. Meanwhile, the position to be taken everywhere also had to be theoretically justified, and this justification was thought to be able to be derived from the Old Testament, starting from the basic rule that the two Tables of the Law stood under the guardianship of the power that bore the sword, and this was now only to be defended, as one generalized the entire peculiar and special position of Israel as the covenant people and transferred it to the Church of Christ. Two considerations could lead to this. In the first place, the position had to be contested that the salvation of the elect only began with Golgotha. To assume that only the accomplished sacrifice of Christ had created the possibility for anyone to be saved, did not hold true. Enoch, Noah and Abraham, David, Hosea and Isaiah, Zerubbabel and Daniel were not to be excluded from the communion of the saints. Whatever perverse view one may have held about the Old Testament Eschatology of those days, it could still not be otherwise, or the Old Testament too had had its elect.

—- Page 486 —-

The Old Testament elect could not be lost forever. It had to stand firm, as we read in Hebrews 11:40, that the fathers of the Old Covenant could not be made perfect without us, so that only with the Ascension of Christ did the blessedness of the Father’s house begin. But this did not exclude the elect of the Old Covenant from that blessedness. Thus, the dispensation of the Old Covenant was taken up into the historical record as a chapter in the history of Christ’s Church. And in connection with this, what the Scripture spoke concerning the Israel of the Old Covenant was now also applied to the Israel of the New Covenant, that is, to the Christian Church. So the dispensation of the Law and the dispensation of the Gospel, taken up into the national church law of Israel, were transferred to the people’s church, and the calling of the Magistracy in Israel was placed on a par with the calling of the Christian Magistracy. This perverse representation was further nourished, in the second place, by the prophecy that had been given in the Old Covenant concerning the coming, position, and work of the Messiah. Naturally, everything that was prophesied concerning the Church of Christ proceeded from what existed as the Kahal in Israel, and the thought constantly shimmered through in that prophecy that what would first be revealed in the Messiah was already to be enjoyed spiritually and, as it were, in advance, in Israel. From the side of the world, there were differences and even partial contradictions. Under the Old Covenant, the entire action of grace was limited to one single people. Everything that was once to be realized was symbolically portrayed in the sacrificial service of this one people. A glorious Kingdom was held out in prospect, in which the Messiah would reign, but which, in the end, at the Consummation, would enter in. The spiritual seemed to have been pushed back, and the future glory was represented to us in the flourishing form of an earthly kingdom. Hence also the antedating of Christ’s appearance, even back into paradise, immediately after the Fall. Down to the smallest details, one thought one could point to this in the old fathers and in the fully developed confession of the Calvinists. There was a difference of dispensation, but the content of that dispensation applied from the promise in paradise. The material of the Old Testament intention was simply all spiritualized. And so one came imperceptibly to the final result, that the distinction

—- Page 487 —-

THE EXAMPLE OF ISRAEL. That the relationship between the Old and New Covenants was merely imaginary is well worth considering. The Cross of Golgotha cast its shadow not only forward, but also backward. The Church of Christ was one of the gates of Paradise from the beginning, and now, and until the Consummation. And understood in this way, it could not be otherwise, or the Government of the Christian land would also have been assigned the same calling as rested upon the Government under the Old Covenant. The King of Israel was the prototype to which the Christian king had to correspond. And so the notion arose, and has been further developed, which we also find in Art. XXXVI of our Confession, that the Government must not only act in civil matters, “to take charge of and watch over the public domain,” i.e., to provide for good order in the civic state, but that it is equally called upon “to protect the sacred ministry, to remove and destroy all idolatry and false worship, to overthrow the kingdom of the Antichrist, and to promote the kingdom of Jesus, and to cause the Word of the Gospel to be preached everywhere, that God may be honored and served by everyone, as He has commanded in His Word.” Now note well that it was not the Government that devised this. It merely allowed this formula to be included in our Confession. No, this demand, this postulate, was set before the Government by the Theologians and the Churches. After first having themselves suffered all the bitterness of governmental interference in religious matters at the hands of the Roman authorities, the Theologians and Churches judged that now the formerly persecuted were in turn to be allowed to enjoy equally powerful support and protection from the Government, and to be able to repay the abuse inflicted on them by their former persecutors. A position which we can hardly imagine ourselves into anymore, which is now taken seriously by almost no one, but which was then boldly pushed through and embraced as the only satisfying solution. And this is only to be explained by the double given, that one did not want to become an Anabaptist, and had to take up the struggle against the Roman Powers on the battlefield, but could not do this without the support of the Reformed Government, and therefore, in order to secure a peaceful future, had to apply the position of Israel under the royal shield of David to the Christianized peoples.

—- Page 488 —-

472 CHURCH-STATE. Now the truly surprising phenomenon presented itself here, that in doing so one did little else than imitate the Roman practice. In the Thirty Years’ War, the two religious powers, both backed by political power, stood opposed to each other. The distinction in principle was remarkable, the deeper one penetrated. In Rome, the ecclesiastical organization stood over against the Government. Rome was a Church that used the Government in its service as an independent spiritual power, while the Protestant Government found only submissive, docile Churches before it, which had to submit themselves. And on the other hand, no less significant a distinction was to be observed, that the Roman Church had taken over the priestly element from Israel into its own priestly organization, and thus ran less risk of falling back too deeply into Old Testament conditions and relationships. The result was, therefore, that on both sides, both from the side of the Roman Church and from the side of the Protestant Churches, the Old Testament image was kept high, and the Church was closely allied with the Government, but with this difference, that on Roman territory the mighty Church resisted the supremacy of the magistrate, while on the Protestant side the Church degenerated into the national folk church, and in that form of folk church had to bow under the mighty arm of the Government, surrendering its independence. The Church of Rome held its head high, our Churches have debased themselves and thrown away their honor, and it must, alas, be added that they are partly guilty of this self-abasement, because of the question of salaries. After 1619, when no more permission was given to hold national and international Synods, it was from that hour on the Government that tyrannized the Churches.

§26. WEAK APPEAL TO THE OLD TESTAMENT. Extremely weak, then, is also the appeal to the Old Testament, with which one has tried to defend the powers and duties of the Government to remove all false religion. See only Deut. 17:18—20. For there it states nothing else than that the King of Israel must have a copy of the law presented to him and govern according to it.

—- Page 489 —-

WEAK APPEAL TO THE OLD TESTAMENT. 473 It is true that we read daily: “He shall read in it all the days of his life, that he may learn to fear the Lord his God, to keep all the words of this law and these statutes, to do them.” But what can be deduced from this for our government? The law of Israel also included the ceremonial law. Shall we now claim that this too must apply to us? No one, surely, who gives it a thought. Only, what then follows: “That his heart may not be lifted up above his brethren,” would certainly also apply to our conditions, but what else does this contain than an admonition against the immoral abuse of governmental power? But even if we read in these words that the King of Israel is obliged, if necessary with the sword, to watch over the maintenance of the priestly service, what could then be deduced from this for our government, where there is no longer any service of that kind among us? Nor does it stand any better with the reference to Joshua 1:7, where it says: “Only be strong and very courageous, that you may observe to do according to all the law which Moses my servant commanded you; do not turn from it to the right hand or to the left.” This is indeed a special command given to Joshua, because he took the place that Moses had so long occupied among the people, to be at the same time the spiritual and political leader of the host. But how could it ever be deduced from this that this command given to Joshua is now also applicable to all princes and authorities? The very special character of this command to Joshua, given under such specific circumstances, cuts off all possibility of transferring it to all princes and authorities. The special may never be so generalized. Even less does the appeal to 2 Kings XI:11, 12 help. Here it is a matter of the coronation of Joash as king, and now it is said that the High Priest not only placed the crown on him as a symbol of government, but also gave him the Testimony in his hand. Now, even if we assume that this was the whole Torah, still nothing else follows than that a king of Israel was naturally bound to the law that applied in Israel. If it were still stated that he had to be given a copy of the Ten Commandments, one could then say that the law of the Two Tables is still valid and that therefore the government is also now bound to it.

—- Page 490 —-

The Church-State relationship must be maintained not only in its second, but also in its first table. But there is nothing about this. It only states that what was then, what the Bible is for us now, was given to him by the High Priest at his coronation. What follows from this for our Authorities? And this the less, since there is nothing that says that God commanded the High Priest to hand that book over to Joash, but only that he, as High Priest, did this. And one can go on with Psalm II:10–12 and with Psalm 72:10,11, and again and again one finds the same thing, partly specific provisions concerning the Authority in a particular country, which have nothing whatsoever to do with ours, and from which therefore nothing whatsoever can be deduced for our Authority, and partly statements of such general scope that they can never lead to a definite conclusion for our Authorities. It would therefore not be worth the trouble to elucidate each of these texts in more detail one after the other. The kings of Israel were in a position in which our Authorities are not, and the people of Israel over whom they ruled were excluded from all peoples and nations of the earth, in order to fulfill a completely separate service in the historical course of the Kingdom of God. Even if one were to assume that the King of Israel was obliged to carry out every provision of the Torah - which is not the case - yet from this nothing definite could be deduced for the Authorities of the nations, and thus also for our Authority. The theocratic character of the people’s state of Israel does not apply to our people’s state. Nor can anything be deduced from what one has further appealed to, namely the honorary titles given to the Authorities in the Old Testament. So, for example, if it is said to the people of Israel, and as one thinks, to the Church of Christ in this people of Israel: “Kings shall be your nursing fathers,” and from this it is deduced that even now the Authority must financially support the Church of Christ; while one then also refers to Isaiah LX:10, where it is testified: “Kings shall serve thee.” Even less can one deduce from what we read in Psalm 82:6, where it is said to the judges: “Ye are gods, and all of you are children of the Most High”; or from what is testified in Isaiah 44:28 concerning Cyrus: “That saith of Cyrus, He is my shepherd, and shall perform all my pleasure”; or finally from what, according to 1 Sam. 24:12, David as a young man said to Saul: “Thou art... ”

—- Page 491 —-

HISTORICAL DEVELOPMENT. From this, one might conclude that all government must provide fatherly care for every need of God’s Church. Statements gathered together, supposedly derived from Scripture, that would always impose the duty on the government to honor, support, and assist the churches and their ministers—but where it is repeatedly evident that more is read into the words than they actually contain, and above all, that one is always dealing with special and very particular relationships, from which no general rule can ever be drawn as to what the relationship between government and church, ordained by God, should be in every respect.

§27. Historical Development. Yet this current of thought after the accession of Constantine to Christianity could no longer be avoided. The crowning of the Emperor in Byzantium was in itself a surprising event, not to rejoice all who were Christian. Persecution now ceased. In place of persecution came elevation and honor. And as is commonly the case with such a change in circumstances, one now saw thousands and tens of thousands of heathens go over to the Christian Church, in the quality of that indifferent multitude that attaches itself as a matter of course to whatever is on the rise. Yet on the other hand, it is beyond doubt that the baptism of Emperor Constantine became the starting point for an inner corruption in the Church in its relationship to the government, the consequences of which still weigh heavily on the ecclesiastical well-being. For Constantine’s conversion not only ended the persecution, but also brought power and wealth to the leaders and heads of the churches. For the poverty with which they had thus far struggled, there now came abundance, and the episcopal word acquired undisputed authority even in matters of state. The temptation inherent in this, the then clergy, with very few exceptions, were unable to resist, and willingly granted the Emperor a supreme influence in all ecclesiastical affairs, provided only that the pagan philosophy and pagan worship were curbed and the Church no longer suffered any annoyance from that side. The less, therefore, did the Church feel the need to maintain its own independence and to resist the encroachments of the state.

—- Page 492 —-

476 CHURCH OF THE STATE. He resisted this, when Emperor Constantine in his famous address (see Eusebius, De vita Constantini Lib. IV c. 24) established as a rule that the Bishops held all authority over the internal affairs of the Church, but that he, Constantine, was himself appointed by God as the Bishop over the external affairs. It can then also be safely said that from 324 onwards, the authority in Christ’s Church has effectively passed into the hands of the Emperor throughout the entire East. Now, as the seat of imperial rule was increasingly shifted from the West to Byzantium, what has been called Byzantinism took firm root throughout the entire East. Even now, the so-called Orthodox, or what is called the Greek Church, is built upon this foundation of governmental authority. In Russia, the Czar still has similar power in ecclesiastical matters, as Emperor Constantine claimed in the fourth century. In Serbia, Bulgaria, etc., the King possesses similar power. Caesaropapism, once rooted in the East, has never been eradicated. And now, just as the political power in the East is visibly increasing in the last half-century, and the population in the East is growing faster than in the Western States, it will not do to point to Romanism and Protestantism alone for Europe, but Byzantinism or Caesaropapism must very certainly be added to those two as a third, very important factor for Christian life. It must be openly acknowledged that it was the Bishops of Rome who, when the complete subjugation of the Church to the State threatened, knew how to resist this, and in the struggle of the Church of Christ to free itself from the tyranny of the Government, the Calvinist still stands unchangeably on the side of Rome. That Rome in this fell into a somewhat questionable one-sidedness, and aimed not only to subject the Government to itself, but also to make the Churches of the whole world dependent on its Bishops, has never been lost sight of. The entire Reformation protested precisely against this one-sidedness and exaggeration, but insofar as it came down to maintaining the freedom and independence of the Church of Christ against the magistrate, the Romanists and Calvinists have always stood on the same line of principle.

—- Page 493 —-

477 HITHERTO. What has now, as the fruit of the Calvinistic principle, been achieved in the complete freedom of the Churches of Christ in America, sets the crown on the principle of ecclesiastical independence. The Bishop of Rome has, from the days of Constantine, made use of the transfer of the imperial throne from Rome to Byzantium, to inflate the ecclesiastical power of the West at the expense of worldly power. The no longer presence of the Emperor in the ancient capital worked to the advantage of the growth of material resources in the Church. And then, shortly after, when the Volkerwanderung covered all of Europe with younger peoples who were ready to receive Baptism, it was entirely understandable that the episcopal influence gradually encompassed all of life and developed a power that became dangerous to civil authority. Virtually all the dynasties of that time, even the English under Henry VIII, have bitterly experienced this pressure of the Church on the Government. Rome took up the cause of the freedom and independence of the Church, but at the cost of freedom within the Church itself. The unity of the world church was sought in outward organization, instead of in spiritual harmony. And ultimately the relationship between Church and Emperor became so strained that one of the two had to succumb. The consequence of this tension has also been that through the Reformation the bond of unity of the Church was broken, and the Churches fell apart, and then the Government in the West and in the center of Europe also managed to bring about a kind of Caesaropapism. In Germany, the ancient struggle between the Ghibellines and Guelphs, at least as far as northwest Germany is concerned, ended in a complete defeat of Papalism; in England a separate Church of England emerged and the Sovereign received the honorary title of Defender of the Faith; in the three Scandinavian Kingdoms Caesaropapism triumphed completely, and in our country the Remonstrant Politicians, with their “pietasordinum,” drove in the same direction.

§28. Hitherto. It must then also be established that at the beginning of the 18th century the

—- Page 494 —-

Caesaropapism gradually encroached upon the entire Protestant domain, and the fundamental change that had to come about here failed to materialize. The power of the Roman Catholic Church has been pushed back almost everywhere. Even in Spain, Bavaria, and Austria, nearly half the population is inclined to side with the civil authorities against the churches, and for the rest of Europe, whether in regions and countries that are Greek Orthodox or in those that are Protestant, the general rule is that the church is bound hand and foot, that it feels itself powerless to maintain its confession, and that it is mainly the material resources of church buildings and stipends that still try to hold it together. If those two material resources were to disappear, the Protestant church would split apart almost everywhere. In our small country, the anti-church individuals already numbered over 300,000, a figure that would already exceed three million for Germany. The Greek churches have also not been able to escape this fate. In Germany, they merged with the Lutheran state churches into the single Evangelical Church. In France, they have at the most unfortunate hour accepted the collegial system on a revolutionary basis. In England, the Church of England is increasingly inclining towards Puseyism. And indeed, in France, in England and Scotland, and also in our own country, free churches have emerged alongside the state churches, but the original free churches that took shape in the sixteenth century have, almost without exception, for the sake of the almighty dollar, merged into a salaried state church. In the Erastians, with their collegial system, this supremacy of the state over the church was even more starkly completed than in Russia or in Berlin. The Lutheran system began with the prince appearing in the church as a confessing member, and it was only by virtue of this confession, in connection with his high state position, that he was then honored as summus episcopus. With the advocates of the Pietatis Ordinum, on the other hand, the state was the true kingdom; in that kingdom of the state, the company of the church appeared alongside many other associations and corporations, and the state or the authorities stood under a divine calling to watch over the well-being of that church, just as over the well-being of the other constituent parts of the state’s life. Even our own Reformed theologians did not come to

—- Page 495 —-

CONCLUSION. As many have rightly pointed out, there was also in our land a Christian society, and this Christian society found its leadership in the Church of Christ. And on this terrain under the Church, the Government now also appeared—the civil authority—to watch over the external, civic affairs. The Remonstrant gentlemen, in this being essentially one with the Socinians, presented the State as primary, and if the State appeared, the Church, and consequently all other phenomena in society, were entrusted to the guidance of the Government. The Collegial system now further aggravated this, in that it misunderstood the high character of the Church. In the Caesaro-Papist systems, the Church still remains a holy appearance that can claim that the Government should treat it with reverence and maintain it with care and love, for Christ’s sake. But in the Collegial system, which arose with the Socinians and Erastians and has now also become the rule among us, this high view of the Church of Christ is entirely abandoned. Whether one has to do with a Christian Church or a Jewish Synagogue, or with a Buddhist convent, it is all the same. They are all collegia, i.e., associations, and the Government has to see to it that these associations do not violate their honor and do not enslave the freedom of individuals.

§29. Conclusion. The whole course of events thus comes down to this, that in the New Testament the Churches appear as entirely free, local Churches, connected only by the apostolic bond. Churches without funds and buildings, and who even allow the apostles to work for wages to provide for their livelihood. In this there is a great change with Constantine the Great. The Church must now be one, become rich, and have power, and for this purpose binds itself to the magisterial Government. Rome resists this and stands up for freedom, but only to later bind that very freedom all the more firmly to Papal ties, so that first the East and later also the Reformation falls away from Rome’s Church, and even in lands that remained entirely Roman Catholic, nearly half the population withdraws its allegiance. This breaking of the power of Rome would have been unthinkable if the Government had not supported the reforming elements with armed force. This support, however, has cost the Reformation Churches dearly, through almost complete loss of their freedom.

—- Page 496 —-

And so now the situation has arisen almost everywhere in Europe, that the Government has gained power over the Churches, placing them under its sway, holding them in its power through financial ties, thereby cutting off the maintenance of the confession, and thus seriously endangering the Christian character of the Churches.

§30. The Outcome in America. In contrast to this European misery in the ecclesiastical domain, the condition of the Churches in the United States stands out as a strong counterpoint. Not in all of America, for in South and Central America, Rome still sets the tone. But in the United States as a whole, the unique phenomenon has now been achieved, that there is no State Church at all, that the Government does not interfere in any way with the organization or church government of the Churches, and that the Churches of all denominations establish and move about with full freedom; that these Churches receive not a single dollar from the Government, but support themselves entirely; and finally, that these Churches, through the constitutional system, thanks to the co-election of their members for the State, exert an influence on the public and even on the life of the State, to which no legal limit is set. This entirely unique state of affairs owes the Church in the United States solely to Calvinism, and Calvinism owes the honor that its principles find here solely to the fact that it did not have to reckon with old conditions in America, but could itself create a new ecclesiastical life and a new state life that fully corresponded to the impulse of the principle. Government and Church, both entirely free within their own domain—the Church exerting no other influence than through its members, through its members on public opinion, and later in the representation of the country. So Calvinism wants it, where it can work freely. This could not be the case with us.

—- Page 497 —-

THE OUTCOME IN AMERICA - ANABAPTISM
not because Hugo Grotius and his followers put handcuffs on us. That is why the full blossoming of Calvinism first became possible in the United States.

§31. Anabaptism
Alongside this, completely separate from the Reformation movement, stood the Anabaptists, from whom the so-called Mennonites later emerged. These Anabaptists were idealists who refused to reckon with reality. According to their view, entirely in line with Holy Scripture, they made a principled distinction in politics between those inhabitants of a country who were considered reborn children of God and the rest, who still lived according to their old, sinful nature. All the latter the Anabaptists lumped together. These still lived under the influence of demonic powers and had nothing to do with Christ and thus the work of Redemption. Their sinful lives ultimately made a government necessary to keep them in check, but police, armies, and the like, as well as the oath, and ultimately even marriage, were to them nothing but unholy means to at least maintain some order in this demonic state. The reborn, the holy, on the other hand, had no need for any of this. It did not concern them. They did not get involved in it. All this was the poison and antidote of the unholy world, which was far removed from the holy standpoint of the Anabaptist crowd. Hence, even Nudists appeared in Amsterdam. This way of dressing was necessary in the sinful world, but did not exist in Paradise, and since these saints now spiritually lived again in the state of Paradise, clothing had no meaning for them anymore. Münster experienced the spectacles to which Jan of Leiden knew how to incite these deluded people. The marriage bond was abolished, causing the community of women to come to life again. And so they fell from one extreme to the other; and this ultimately so fearfully and bitterly that the government had to suppress and exterminate them by force of arms. This was all the more tragic because Anabaptism had actually emerged from very pious and sanctified people.

—- Page 498 —-

The sovereignty of each sphere has been taken up. The blood of the martyrs testifies to it, with what reverence-inspiring faith the Anabaptist martyrs went to their death, and even after the suppression of Anabaptism, interesting elements that had blossomed from the very beginning in the much smaller circle of the Mennonites or Baptists remained. Now, however, they have largely become estranged from the faith of their fathers, and Modernism reigns among them, but even in the middle of the 18th century, they often shone through their godliness. The world of their thoughts had baptism as its center, just as they always named themselves after baptism. They took baptism as the outward sign of the incorporation of their person into the body of Christ, and thus as the fruit of regeneration and conversion. Now, since there could be no talk of conversion and thus also no talk of regeneration in the case of a child or a person who has not yet come of age, they taught that no one should receive baptism unless he had previously professed to be a child of God, an elect, a regenerate, a converted, a believing one. Their purely Christian element had to be extracted from the life of the world, be separated from it, and every baptized person had to form a separate circle with the other regenerate ones. Not a Church. That served no purpose. For centuries it had been seen where the so-called Church of Rome and now also that of Calvin had led. They therefore did not call themselves a Church, but always a Society, as the Mennonites here in this country still call themselves the Baptist Society. One immediately feels the serious difficulty that these “believers” had to face. They were offended by the sight that the Sacraments, both Baptism and the Lord’s Supper, were generally administered to thousands and tens of thousands, in whom there was simply no question of a holy life, of a decisive faith, let alone of a powerful regeneration. All this evil had arisen from infant baptism. Infants, who knew nothing yet, who could not yet utter a word, had been recognized through baptism as members of the Lord’s Body. As they grew up, it then became clear, alas, how one had deceived oneself in them. And so an ecclesiastical condition had arisen in which believers and hypocrites, mixed together in large masses, were in the so-called Church of Christ.

—- Page 499 —-

THE ANABAPTISTS.483 lived together. But the Anabaptist broke with this. This could not be good before God. It did not come down to the Sacraments, but rather to the great question of life—whether a baptized person, before their baptism with water, had already been baptized with the Holy Spirit. This seemed to provide a firm foundation. The mixing would cease. Only the truly believing would commune, and realize the Body of Christ. And all the rest would fall back into the world. The Anabaptist would have no further dealings with this unholy world. They accepted no office in the magistracy, they swore no oaths, they bore no sword. All this belonged to the unholy worldly life, and from this the child of God withdrew, with the utmost decisiveness, through this entire way of being. But soon it became apparent that this all looked so beautiful and holy, but without there being any certainty that it corresponded to any actual result. Yes, if it could be determined whether the new birth had truly taken place, and it could be decisively proven that your claim that God had made you His child was a spiritual reality, then indeed, that would be the highest attainment possible on earth. The dividing line between the believing and the unbelieving would then be visible to everyone’s eyes. One could not be mistaken. Baptism would only be administered to God’s true children. And no Judas could sneak into the holy fellowship. But precisely this proved to be impossible. At first, they thought they had succeeded, and took it very seriously. The selection was then also made very strictly, and undoubtedly they initially succeeded in uniting only truly regenerated believers in the holy circle. But soon, deceived by this self-deception, the spiritual delusion of others became apparent. The realization that spiritual selection can always fail among men penetrated ever deeper. And when in the end the holy principle degenerated into carnal infamies, the delusion in which they had lived was finally seen in its nothingness, and the entire foundation on which the Anabaptist structure rested sank into ruin. But precisely herein lay the justification for what the Calvinists had put forward as the only good Church order. They had now seen the so-called world church of Rome, commending itself in that it maintained a worldly bond with the other Churches.

—- Page 500 —-

The Church-State. They did not for a moment let go of the authority of the government over the Church, but erred in their separation between clergy and laity, in their failure to recognize the local freedom of the Churches, and in their pretended Vicariate. Secondly, there were the entirely unfree Churches in Caesaropapism, in the Greek-Orthodox Churches, and in the Lutheran groups. There was also the collegial system, which denaturalized and degraded the Church of Christ to a pious association. There was the Anabaptist system, of those who thought they could arrogate to themselves the status of the elect by withdrawing from the world. And alongside and opposed to these five systems, the Calvinistic system took its place, which did not drive on the never outwardly to be established regeneration, but on holy Baptism; and among these baptized, by admonition and discipline, maintained the holy character.

§32. The Discipline of the Calvinists.
Yet the Calvinists just as decisively refused to accept a Church without purification through discipline. With discipline in name only, one would again get the intermingling of Church and world, and this must lead to the undoing of the true Church. Only with the inexorable demand for their own discipline does the Church maintain itself in America, and only thus does it begin to revive in our country as well. Yet among the Reformed it still often stumbles over what the past has bequeathed to us, and the question arises again and again as to what position one should take towards the buildings, funds, and stipends. To this the Free Churches always give the answer: If it is a matter of loyalty to Christ or to Mammon, then hesitate not for a moment. Your need will be provided for. This has also proved to be the case time and again, and so in Scotland and England, as well as here in Germany, the various Free Churches have still managed to save their lives. But one is mistaken if one thinks that the great masses can so easily be moved to accept this system. This depends on the degree of faith, and on one’s own temperament, and no less on the offense that unbelief gives. If faith is too weak in nature, it

—- Page 501 —-

THE CALVINIST DISCIPLINE. The temperament is melancholic, and the offense for which one stands is not gripping, then one turns to everything, withdraws into the mystical and arranges oneself. One sees well, therefore, not to impose the demand to which one oneself succumbed too quickly on others. It must naturally be borne in mind that the Erasmus natures in the 16th century almost without exception went back to Rome again, but then whoever, on the other hand, zealously advocates for Church purification, must also understand the holy art of patient waiting. Even in times of indifference, there is still often a power to transform the conditions that often surprises from behind. The main thing is just that we sharpen our own insight on the matter of the connection between State and Church and clarify the insight of the misguided. If it is between an ecclesiastical administration that the Government has imposed on us and the definite demand of God’s Word, then every believer must learn to not yield to the word of the most worldly Church and to obey God. If he sees on all sides how the funds and salaries are just the ones that corrupt the spiritual conditions, then he may not acquiesce that this unholy yoke still rests on the Church of Christ by the Constitution of the land, and he must seek ways to remove the bond between the spiritual Church and this material support. Never let it be a cause of offense that not everyone follows your example exactly. There are no fixed rules here that can be maintained with great precision with an open eye for all the different situations. The situations differ greatly and often the consequences of these situations differ even further. Do not be each other’s judges in this, but let our King be everyone’s judge in conscience. Only against one evil are we inexorable, and that evil is the silent acquiescence in what goes against God’s Word. To arrange oneself in what one’s conscience first condemned, in short, to doze off on the pillow of impassivity, is of the Evil One. State and Church must not just lie side by side, but live together. But the stimulus must come to you from both sides at the same time. On the one hand, the stimulus to keep your Church free from the State, but also on the other hand, to work all members of Christ’s Church on the State and on His Government. Calvinism is simultaneously a postulate for the Church and a postulate for the political life. Christian, in order to remain faithful to Christ, and democratic, in order to assert the Christian influence on political life. There is no system that can rival the Calvinism in the effectiveness of its operation for both Church and State.

—- Page 502 —-

CHAPTER XIII THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM
§1. Study of the party system. The party, a word derived from the Latin word: Pars, i.e. part, does not aim at a people as a whole, but at a part of it, and indeed at a part that, as a rule, stands opposite another part, or even several other parts. Formerly it seemed almost always as if only two parties could be tolerated in the State; but since the second half of the previous century, this has changed. In the German Reichstag, for example, there were no less than 14 political groups counted in 1907, in 1912 these had dwindled to 11, but even then there were still 17 unclassified deputies left over. The originally demanded duality of parties held out the longest in the United States of America, where the Republicans and Democrats disputed the terrain with each other; but here too, a further split had already occurred, partly on the side of the workers, and now also through Roosevelt. It was no different with us. Had we initially not had other than Liberals and Conservatives, since then the Anti-revolutionaries had appeared, later the R.-Catholic State party, then through a break with the Anti-revolutionary party the Christian-Historical, then the Radical, later still the Liberal-Democratic, which now stands next to the Liberal Union and the party of the Free Liberals; while the latter again has the Social Democrats to its left, who then, not in the Chamber, but still in the country, saw the men of the Tribune and the Anarchists go off from them.

—- Page 503 —-

STUDY OF PARTY POLITICS. 487 There was also formerly a certain duality among us, but now there is fragmentation. A reminder of this is now more necessary than ever, since it is so desirable to form a clear understanding of the right to exist of political parties. This has often been neglected among us, whereas in Germany the party division has repeatedly been the subject of scholarly investigation. Stahl began with it, shortly after the revolutionary turmoil of 1848, and in 1851, in his lectures, designated it as the doctrina de indole partium, quae nunc in republica et ecclesia exstant, and completed his lectures on this subject in 1857. During his lifetime he did not publish them himself. He died in 1861, and it was not until 1862 that the publication of his Party Doctrine appeared, which had a second edition in 1868. They are 29 college lectures, highly important, but which still left the party system itself more or less aside, and aimed more at an apology for the Anti-Revolutionary standpoint. Already in his first Lectures he began with the juxtaposition of the party of the Revolution and the party of Legitimacy. The whole work is almost 400 pages long and deals, as one should bear in mind, with the parties of State and Church as the two manifestations of the same differentiation in the bosom of the German people. From a more general point of view, the party system had already been studied in Switzerland in the first half of the previous century by Frederic Romer. His Lehre von den politischen Parteien was published in Zurich in 1844 by his brother Theodor Romer. Romer’s attempt is to explain the different political tendencies from “the fundamental relationships of the human soul,” especially with regard to the development of man in the different periods of his life years. He then studies the infantia, the pueritia, the adolescentia, the virilitas and the senectus, and believes that from this very different disposition of the human spirit in these different periods of life, Radicalism, Liberalism, Conservatism and Absolutism can be explained. Dr. J.C. Bluntschli has built further on this theme in his Politik als Wissenschaft, Stuttgart 1876, namely in the fifth chapter of this work, pages 497–637, and finally given a tabular overview of the varied ideas that are current in the four “state parties” of Radicals, Liberals, Conservatives and Absolutists, arranged according to man’s age. Constantin Frank discussed the party system in his Naturlehre der Staaten,

—- Page 504 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
Leipzig 1870. August Winter gave interesting observations in his work Über die Bildung der ersten Kammer in Deutschland, Tübingen 1870, especially on pages 78 ff. R. von Mohl in his Encyclopaedia of Political Science, second edition, Freiburg 1872, discusses these parties in the state on pages 648 ff. Among the writers of later years and from the present century, one should consult M. Ostrogorski La Démocratie et l’organisation des partis politiques, Paris, Calman-Lévy 1903, especially chapter VIII Le Bilan, pages 607 ff., in which he comes to the regrettable conclusion that the party system, at least in England, is committing suicide. See further Gustav Ratzenhofer, Wesen und Zweck der Politik, Leipzig 1893, Part I p. 189—220 ff. Further, Ludwig Gumplowicz, Geschichte der Staatstheorien, Innsbruck 1905, pages 462 ff., and in Die kämpfenden Parteien und ihre Interessen. Then especially the professor H. Rehm of Strasbourg, Deutschlands politische Parteien, ein Grundriss der Parteien-lehre, Jena 1912, who mainly discusses the electoral systems, but also examines the party system on pages 1 ff. and 36 ff. in more detail. He rejects the four-part party system of Römer and Bluntschli. Likewise that of Grotewold, who set aristocratic against democratic. (Die Parteien des Deutschen Reichstag, 1908); and in the main he aligns himself with the tripartite division that Jellinek proposed in his Allgemeine Staatslehre, second ed. 1905, pages 110 ff.; but more on this later. See also Treitschke in his Hist. polit. Aufsätze, 4th ed. 1871, the third essay, and further the reviews and political-legal-economic dictionaries. We also mention Harold W.V. Temperley, Senates and Upper-Chambers, London 1910, p. 1—25; as well as Sigmund Figdor, Parlamentswissenschaft, Berlin 1891, part II, Die Parteitaktik, which chose the conduct of parties in Parliament as the subject of study, but in connection with this also examines the party system itself in more detail. The third part of this work Die parlamentarische Technik is also rich in content, and at least the second part comes into consideration here.
§2. Scarce among us. Especially here the attention should be drawn to the literature on the subject, because among us little inclination arose to occupy oneself with it.

—- Page 505 —-

ON OUR POLITICAL POVERTY.489
The reason for this lies in our historical past. We lacked under our Republic a genuine parliamentary representation of the people. Both the governing power and the control over that power rested with a small number of qualified persons. They were considered the lords of the land, and the population had only to praise what the lords decreed. This untenable system was broken in 1798 and later in 1813, but even thereafter, the great majority of our people retained the old aversion to the rise of party life. It stood with the Press as it did with the people. One did not speak of the Press among us, but always of the Newspaper or the Gazette. And now that Gazette was good enough for family notices and the extensive reports of accidents, but what such a Gazette peddled in politics was, in the eyes of most, little more than the prattle of incompetents. The middle class, infected by the poisonous spirit from Paris, may have thought differently and already before 1848 placed the authority of the Gazette above the authority of the government, but the higher classes turned up their noses at it, and the lower population, both in the cities and in the countryside, continued to regard the political chatter of the Gazette as mere ballast. The Haarlem newspaper and a few city newspapers were exceptions, but in general one had to have nothing to do with the Gazette. Even its credibility was doubted from all sides. It only caused unrest. It was just “the Gazette,” and thus a completely superfluous literature. This had started with Van Effen’s Spectator. And furthermore, all that newspaper writing had never had any other effect than to make the citizenry discontented. In the politically unemancipated circles, nothing was understood of the Press as the Queen of the Earth. And as it stood with the Press, so it stood in the estimation of many with the parties as well. Groen van Prinsterer came out with his own Gazette, but his Nederlander found hardly any subscribers and had to be discontinued in the end. And when Groen van Prinsterer also tried to appear as the founder of his own political party, he was understood by only very few. Those who set the tone in politics could scarcely suppress their laughter; after all, there were Liberals and Conservatives, what need would there be for a third party? And although the men of the Revival showed him some sympathy, they still felt no urge for a solid party formation.

—- Page 506 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. It was also almost nothing that Beets and others opposed him, and many others supported him so purely nominally that he was called a “general without an army,” and soon, with his trio: Keuchenius, Kuyper and Van Otterloo, in 1869-’72, threw overboard the entire rest of his name-supporters and let the cry of “Regained independence” resound throughout the land. The number of votes cast for his candidates could then hardly be counted, but the false amalgamation was then nevertheless set limits, and however small it still was, the healthy shoot still sprouted from the root of the genuine stem. It is from then on that our Anti-Revolutionary press also dates.

§3. Tragic development.
In no country was the spirit of the people as a whole as opposed to party formation as in ours. It is true that in the second half of the eighteenth century, group had confronted group sharply in the arena, but what soon came upon us under French rule extinguished all party spirit, and in 1813–15 there was no other guiding thought than that now all division was forsworn and the one people revived. The temporary union had even promoted this unhealthy unity in the Northern Provinces, and when in 1830 it came to a breach, and one stood once more as one man against the South, it all melted again into like-minded political ideas. It was not until the period of 1839-’44 that one began to see the untenable nature of the almost moldy state. Then the connoisseurs could no longer be appeased, and through Thorbecke the for or against the wavering spirits was elicited. He founded a party as if with a magic wand, and of course those who did not join him then stood out as a party against him. This was, however, not otherwise understood than as a too high a leap with the sole purpose of temporary resistance. That it had to come to open, well-organized, programmatically-outlined party formation, back and forth, was felt by only a very few, Groen van Prinsterer foremost. In the general public, on the other hand, “the party,” just like “the Press,” remained in disgrace. One underwent the party action as a necessary evil that had to be tolerated temporarily like an emerging fever, and perhaps...

—- Page 507 —-

TRAGIC DEVELOPMENT. It was not yet seen or believed that purifying the wrong elements from the blood was necessary; but that party formation is a demand of political life in a developed people. In the countryside, one had one’s small village disputes and regional competitions, and in the great cities a press of some significance began to stir up young life; but taken as a whole, the people still hung in the mist that prevented any distinction of political color. There was not only no thirst for a properly organized party life, but in broad circles the party infection was still regarded as a dubious epidemic against which one had to be on one’s guard. Just as the newspaper, which thirsted for politicizing, was referred to the wastepaper basket, so party life was also considered a political corruption that had crept in, the suppression and eventual eradication of which was to be considered. The word “party” thereby acquired an evil sound in the Netherlands from the outset. A party member reminded one, as a matter of course, of the unjust concept of being partial. A party follower was someone who threw his independence to the winds. Party spirit was a mental illness. Party zeal was unpatriotic. Almost all compounds and derivations of the word “party” were under suspicion. A party, so it seemed, was like the leader of a guerrilla band. And insofar as one then, out of necessity, still set up electoral associations and even once sent out a leaflet, the idea that a proper party formation could be done remained almost completely lost, even long after 1850. Examining the state of affairs more closely, I found more than once that even deep into the seventies, the entire party action consisted in summoning about twenty people as the election approached, providing a small sum of money for the electoral action by interested gentlemen, placing a few advertisements in a local newspaper, and sending out a rather nice leaflet—not even to all voters, but only to those listed on a list. There was also sometimes a quiet action by landowners who had their administrators give a wink to their tenants, but there was neither public action nor party formation in a higher sense. A program, and then a very short and special one, mostly came only from the Kneuterdijk. And in full appreciation of the excellent forces that personally did this here and there, the whole thing still had the character of a private enterprise rather than a public party action.

—- Page 508 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. It would not be an exaggeration to say that party life in our country, as almost nowhere else, has languished for too long, and that, leaving aside the wild action of 1848, it was really only the Anti-Revolutionary party that was first concerned with better meeting the demands of the political situation.

§4. Reaction against further unfolding. Yet you would be mistaken in thinking that this blindness to the necessity of a vigorously flourishing party life came merely from political sentimentality. There was indeed the active force of a political conviction behind it. It was thought that after 1848 the cornerstone had been found once and for all in the political field. The Constitution was now the monumental interpretation of the ideal. As children of the same people, as sons of one fatherland, we ought to live together in concord and love. In short, the ecclesiastical idea was applied to the common national life. In the Church, that went without saying, we had a confession that had been handed down to us from our fathers and that was grounded in an appeal to God’s holy Word. With that Confession one had to persevere, unless a decisive error could be pointed out on the basis of God’s Word. If such an error became apparent, it had to be corrected, and after that correction the demand for a unified confession became just as strict as before. Now there did indeed—just think of the Voetians and Cocceians—arise rather loudly sounding differences, but this was regretted, even more, disapproved of. The rule remained what the holy Apostle had so earnestly laid upon the Christians at Philippi: “Fulfill my joy, that you may be of the same mind, having the same love, and above all: being of one heart and one feeling.” And this under the rebuking warning: “Do nothing through strife or vain glory, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem the other better than himself” (Phil. 2:2,3). The ecclesiastical rule that had arisen from this, that unity of feeling and confession must be an inviolable rule, and that division of conviction was to be condemned, was now also transferred to the political terrain. Civil life was merely the outer side with Christianity on the inner side. And by falsely clinging to the organic unity of these two manifestations of life, one now introduced the same demand that in Christ’s Church had been established as the rule, namely, that this same unity should also apply to the civil inheritance. In a heathen state like ancient Rome, Plebeians and Patricians might have stood opposed to each other, but in a Christian land this was unacceptable. One in Christ’s Church, one must also appear as one in the land, and whoever, like the Roman Catholic and the Remonstrant, did not agree with our Church, was then also considered as having fallen out of the mandate and could originally not even be appointed or elected to any office of significance. This connection between the ecclesiastical and the civil life continued even after 1848 in the estimation of many, but now naturally in reverse order. If 1848 broke the political mirror in which the nation had thus far glimpsed its image of unity, the breach of the same nature in the Reformed Church immediately followed. Whereas the earlier demand had been: one in the Church, and on the basis of this also one in the State, now it became party against party in the State, and thus also party against party in Christ’s Church. On our part, the slogan was then raised: unity of Confession in the Church of Christ, principle against principle in the national cause. And therefore, as long as the current in national politics received its inspiration from the Paris of 1789, we stood accordingly as a separate party, with all who shared our principle, as Anti-Revolutionary. §5. Church and State different.
Party formation, a transgression against the holy in the Church of Christ, is on the political terrain, as soon as a people comes to a higher development, a pressing demand. This demand is grounded in the multiform character of all natural manifestations of life. The riches of God’s Creation shine in the variation. Also among men, variation is the mark of a richer expression of life. A manufacturer delivers identical goods according to a fixed model. The artist, on the other hand, who paints the vase with the brush,

—- Page 509 —-

REACTION AGAINST FURTHER UNFOLDING. 493 the outer side with Christianity on the inner side. And by falsely clinging to the organic unity of these two manifestations of life, one now introduced the same demand that in Christ’s Church had been established as the rule, namely, that this same unity should also apply to the civil inheritance. In a heathen state like ancient Rome, Plebeians and Patricians might have stood opposed to each other, but in a Christian land this was unacceptable. One in Christ’s Church, one must also appear as one in the land, and whoever, like the Roman Catholic and the Remonstrant, did not agree with our Church, was then also considered as having fallen out of the mandate and could originally not even be appointed or elected to any office of significance. This connection between the ecclesiastical and the civil life continued even after 1848 in the estimation of many, but now naturally in reverse order.
