If 1848 broke the political mirror in which the nation had thus far glimpsed its image of unity, the breach of the same nature in the Reformed Church immediately followed. Whereas the earlier demand had been: one in the Church, and on the basis of this also one in the State, now it became party against party in the State, and thus also party against party in Christ’s Church. On our part, the slogan was then raised: unity of Confession in the Church of Christ, principle against principle in the national cause. And therefore, as long as the current in national politics received its inspiration from the Paris of 1789, we stood accordingly as a separate party, with all who shared our principle, as Anti-Revolutionary.

§5. Church and State different.
Party formation, a transgression against the holy in the Church of Christ, is on the political terrain, as soon as a people comes to a higher development, a pressing demand. This demand is grounded in the multiform character of all natural manifestations of life. The riches of God’s Creation shine in the variation. Also among men, variation is the mark of a richer expression of life. A manufacturer delivers identical goods according to a fixed model. The artist, on the other hand, who paints the vase with the brush,

—- Page 510 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
Each vase depicts a distinct image or scene. And this character of variation, which is already a mark of the higher, reigns throughout God’s creation, in all the realms of nature, and most strongly in the world of human beings. One has even wrongly sought to banish all variation from the angelic world. Even though the angelic host remains hidden from us behind an impenetrable veil, what we read of angels and archangels, of Seraphim and Cherubim, still points to rich diversity. But above all among the children of men, natural variation is rich and sharply delineated, and not only in the duality of man and woman, in the differences of age, race and nationality, and vocation, but also in the individual person. When one enters a small, quite ordinary family in a fishing village, and observes the man, woman, and children, one is at first inclined to think that all life here is identical, but prolonged and more frequent interaction with these 5 or 6 persons brings an ever richer light to bear on the differences in character, disposition, and inclination that increasingly manifest themselves between these 5 or 6 persons. “No two are alike,” it is said, even of the leaves on a tree, and this certainly characterizes all human life. The first superficial impression, which so easily leads one to the delusion that the four, five children of one family are merely repetitions of the same type, is deceptive. Uniformity does not exist in God’s creation, much less monotony. Humanity works in a factory-like manner toward a hollow model, and only as an artist in the rich diversity, but in what God creates, the endless variation is the divine fundamental rule. It is the infinity of God that expresses itself in this endless otherness. Here, however, the fixed rule applies that in us humans this wealth of variation remains hidden at first, but then must also come to light through further development and unfolding. One who enters the nursery of a children’s home notices well that even these little ones do not all look the same, but in their expressions of life they are almost all the same. Grown up to be children, in the playroom they already show differences in character. This they show in their choice of toys, in what attracts and fascinates them, in their play and speech. Soon, as boys and girls taking to the streets, they drift further apart. When they have become young men and maidens, then

—- Page 511 —-

CHURCH AND STATE DIFFER—EVER-RICHER VARIATION.
495. It turns left, what attracts right, and already everyone begins to become something of their own. In adulthood, these endless personal differences then become fixed. And even among the elderly, one seeks in vain for identity. John is not Peter, and Peter is so entirely different from Henry. Each has their own name, and this must be so, for they are not the same. Now, this variation governs the position in the Church of Christ, not because a natural element entered here, but because a supernatural element intervened. The ray of light from Above determines the very colors in which it breaks. In all natural life, on the other hand, the action comes from below, and hence the endless variations. Now, if one consults the condition of the still undeveloped, just developed, more developed, and highly developed peoples, as they live side by side in the different parts of the world; and likewise in those peoples the different classes according to the degree of their power development; and thirdly, the individuals according to age and upbringing—then the firm conclusion is that the lower the development still stands, the less significant the variations are, and conversely, the further the development progresses, the variations in insight, views and convictions increase, and thereby sympathies and antipathies are set in motion. Sympathies that then seek union with like-minded people, and antipathies that lead to an increasingly decisive position against those who think differently.

§6. Ever-richer variation.
This then divides the one nation into its partes, and thereby the parties announce themselves. According to this rule, one also observes geographically and historically that hordes and still wandering groups are mostly one in their own bosom; that in groups that have chosen a fixed abode, domestic, agricultural and family differences arise, but without parties forming; that, the higher the development goes, schools and groups begin to appear within the people itself; and that, if a people succeeds in raising itself to a higher political level, the State parties present themselves of their own accord. This comes out little, as long as the fellow-citizens themselves

—- Page 512 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
Not until the people themselves take a greater part in public affairs, and the absolute power of the Government regulates the common interests, do differences of opinion and differentiation of conviction begin to manifest themselves, almost without exception. And it is the group-wise penetration of these opposing convictions that creates and must create the party life.
Tyrannical coercion may indeed attempt to hold this back, but almost always it ends either with the triumph of the people over the authority that seeks to repress all development, or—for history shows us striking examples of this as well—with the downfall of the people. On this ground, therefore, it must be laid down as a rule that, with a developed national life, the formation of parties, or a splitting up into parties, is an unavoidable requirement of a healthy political life, and that the people injures its own future if it prefers to let this open party struggle rest or tries to prevent it, and, if it does break out, tries to suppress it.
The emphasis on this must be all the stronger, because we Dutch are in this respect behind almost all other, more highly developed peoples. Our disappointing history from 1648 to 1848 is the cause of this, and the heavy pressure on our national life of an overly strict ecclesiastical stamp also contributed to preventing the blessing and fruit of a healthy and vigorous party life in the political sphere from being awakened and appreciated. To suppress party struggle is to kill the national life. Whoever loves a pulsing life of inner power in his people must, in principle, abhor all scoffing at party life as proof of historical folly. Stahl rightly called out to his hearers in the Berlin lecture hall: “Partylessness is not my virtue!” (p.1) and Dr. J.C. Bluntschli has said so truthfully in his Politik als Wissenschaft, Stuttgart 1876: “The richer and freer the political life takes shape, the more decisively do the political parties come to the fore. The most politically gifted peoples show the most developed party formation... Only the struggle and friction of party opposites brings forth the highest state creations. Thereby the political necessity and usefulness of party formation is proven.”
Not without reason, then, does Bluntschli also say: “The political parties are therefore not, as many narrow and anxious minds understand them, a worrying evil, a sickness of state life, but on the contrary a condition and a sign of healthy

—- Page 513 —-

NOT TOO POLITICAL IN CHARACTER OF THE PARTIES. 497 “political life of the people” (p. 449). It is no honor, but a disgrace, to belong to no party, and a statesman who boasts of standing above the parties, glories in his lack of principles and inconsistency. “These parties,” as Bluntschli concludes, “are the natural necessary appearance and expression of the powerful inner impulses that move the political life of the nation.” The weak, oppressed party life, as it has crept up with us from around 1630 to 1790, shows how the land and the people were actually delivered into the hands of small coteries, and the people as a people did not participate politically in its various ranks. The resulting state of mind still lives all too much in more than one conservative circle. And even among us Anti-revolutionaries, it still happens that one recognizes the necessity of acting as a party, but systematically withdraws from the care for the flourishing of his party, leaves the care for the lesser gods to others, lets its organization go blue-blue, has its meetings visited by others, and makes do with a meager annual contribution financially.

§7. Not Too Political Character of the Parties. In larger states, one encounters this less, since in such states world problems come up again and again. It is more in the states of the second and third rank that political life sometimes breathes so faintly. And although it cannot be said in general of our country that we are insensitive to this, it must be confessed that in the Netherlands from of old a broad group was encountered that had no eye for the political struggle in the life of the state. Certainly our polder life has also nourished this evil. One then hardly looks over his dike to the polders that enclose his own polder, and for what lies further away, the heart has no interest. Now, if one asks the question in what contrasts, in what organizations and in what alliances the parties have to act, then a sharp distinction must be made between the parties in the proper sense political and non-political. For the action of political parties immediately aims at the course of affairs in the whole state. Party formation that confines itself to the course of events in a Explanation 32

—- Page 514 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. Not even a single village, region, or city is included here. Even political movements that determine an entire province cannot be counted as part of the political party life. Family feuds that sometimes last for centuries and dominate the state of affairs in a village or small town are also excluded. The same can be said of the fading remnants of parties that no longer count in political life. In Hanover, in the old Tuscany, in southern Italy, in Portugal, and so many other places, one still finds small groups that honor the traditions of a fallen princely house. But gradually these traditions are fading away so nearly without a trace that even the Bourbons and Bonapartists in France scarcely count anymore for the general politics of the country. The same applies to the partisans of Don Miguel in Portugal, and to the royalists in Brazil. Until recently, the same could be said of the Orangists in Ulster, whose activity once stirred all of Europe for a moment. It can hardly be denied that such fading groups have a political character, but they cannot be honored as political parties. To a certain extent, the same can be said of groups that appear to defend special interests, as repeatedly happened with the Agrarians. Yet it would not do to leave such groups out of the discussion entirely. For all such groups extend too far across the entire country and are too numerous in interested parties for them to be able to determine the outcome at the ballot box. Ostrogorsky even goes so far as to claim that in England the truly political character of the parties is becoming increasingly weakened, and that the entire organization under the so-called Caucus, in its pursuit of interests, is committing suicide (op. cit. p.). He even speaks of a real divorce taking place between the political parties and political principles. “One sees the divorce of politics from principles” (p. 585). Treitschke had already come to the conclusion forty years ago—it was in 1871—that a tenable division of the parties could no longer be given. Van Romer and Bluntschli had already seen how they tried to explain the party grouping from the difference in age: students radical, young men liberal, older men conservative, the aged absolutist; a division for which there is now no one.

—- Page 515 —-

ORGANIZATION OF PRINCIPLES.499 Yet the one thing that is common to all is that most people become somewhat discolored politically over the years—a general party division based on the difference in age is directly contradicted by the facts, not to mention dwelling on it for a moment. Stahl, Kaufman, and Paulsen tend to see the parties as little more than advocates for special interests. Jellinek boldly proposed dividing the parties into three groups. The first would be the party that had been in power but lost, and now led a reactionary opposition; the second would be the party that was in power at the moment and sought to maintain it, and therefore was conservative; and the third would be the party that was striving to seize power, and therefore tried to attract the masses by espousing ideals, the radical. A classification of which Rehm (p.6) rightly observes that characterizing the various parties by their power position and tactics, while remaining disconnected from the goal that the party is pursuing, is not to be accepted.

§8. Organization of Principles. The embarrassment in which most writers on this subject apparently find themselves is to be explained by the fact that, especially in England and America, where party life has developed most vigorously, what concerns organization has become so firmly established that the original antithesis of the two great parties has faded more and more. Hence the parties ultimately found their lasting strength only in their organization, which was carried on and on by the family groups in the people. In America, one would belong to the Republicans from childhood, because one’s father also belonged to them. It was therefore self-evident that the flourishing of the Republican party promised the young man a personal future, while the rise of the Democrats threatened him with the loss of position and fortune. So the emigrants from the Netherlands in Mississippi, Iowa, Illinois had also joined the Republicans. The very name of Democrats repelled them. But then they also went to the polls with the Republicans at every election, without hardly giving any account of whether the platform of the Republicans at the next election rested on principles that they could endorse.

—- Page 516 —-

500 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. The citizens died, the party remained, and only in its organization did one find the embodiment of a politics in which one was, as it were, born and raised and which one did not think of abandoning. But precisely for that reason, political action increasingly lost its principled character. To attract, both parties pushed more and more to the fore what could warm the masses, and the soul went out of party life.

§9. Gradual Difference. Let us for a moment set aside the non-principled motives for party formation, then it is self-evident that in a people who live from the same fundamental principle, the difference of opinion in the political parties can be nothing other than a difference in degree. If all compatriots stand on the same principled basis, then only the question can arise whether the condition of the government and the entire state organization develops in good order according to the jointly professed principles. Now a difference of insight can arise, with one finding more to criticize in the existing state of affairs than the other, and also that one represents what should come in place of the existing in a more or less divergent way from the existing. Then all begin by accepting the existing state of affairs, and they all also judge that the existing has to develop further; only, they differ in their appreciation of the existing and in the pace of further development. One finds what is not good and therefore wants to improve it recklessly; the other is more moderate in his judgment of the existing and does not want to move forward except with a calm step; while the third admits that there are stones here and there that cry out for improvement, but nevertheless comes forward for the existing as historically sanctioned and legitimate.

—- Page 517 —-

GRADUAL DIFFERENCE - RESPECT FOR DIVINE AUTHORITY It stands quite differently, however, when a change occurs in the general state of affairs that causes these three parties to gradually drift away from the common state foundation on which they had thus far built together. This already threatened at the end of the Middle Ages, this continued to progress as the Naturalists came to power, and reached its culmination at the end of the 19th century. In the Middle Ages, the general conviction was that in the political realm, one must first and foremost reckon with God as the Source of all power and authority, and that in the construction of the state, man served only as an organic instrument under God. In this lay the principle and starting point for all state rights and politics. Between the Guelfs and Ghibellines, the struggle raged over the manner in which the state would regulate its relationship to the Almighty; whether the statesman would reckon with God’s power and authority on his own authority, or whether the relationship between the Almighty God and the earthly governing power would be mediated by the bishops and the Pope. The relationship of the individual peoples to the Emperor as the Roman Caesar, who felt himself to be the Christianized, but still the legitimate successor of the Caesars, might also raise disputes, but there was no difference over the very foundation on which the state edifice rested. Even when more emphasis was placed on natural law, one initially still regarded that in man and in the connection of man with man as speaking of nature as a product of God’s will, and even in a radical thinker like Althusius, however faintly, the authority of God ultimately carried the day. There were some who doubted, and it did not lack for a single atheist, and there were even incorrigible know-it-alls who, before Rousseau, were more extreme than Rousseau, but in general public opinion, among the masses, and among the rulers and scholars, it was still considered the firm rule that one had to reckon with God in all things, and thus also in the political realm.

§10. Respect for Divine Authority
The universality of this conviction was often connected with the unity of the church formation and the adherence of all inhabitants

—- Page 518 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM
to that formation; a factual motive that cannot be emphasized enough. The Holy Scripture itself gives us, even in the old days of the people of Israel, almost as good as never the impression of a religiously homogeneous multitude. Formally yes, but in essence no. Whoever weighs and values the Psalms and the Prophets historically, comes, at one historical moment after another, almost invariably to the conclusion that the true worshippers formed only a relatively small group in both Judah and Israel, and that the great masses more lent their ear to those who mockingly cried: “There is no God!” (Psalm XIV:1), and in Psalm X:2—4 it says: “The wicked hotly persecutes the afflicted. As he puffs up his nose, he does not seek. He blasphemes the Lord, and all his thoughts are: ‘There is no God.’” From both David and Isaiah one constantly receives the impression that those who, out of conviction, rested everything they built on the one foundation in God, were only a part of the people. This began already shortly after the exodus from Egypt. With a few great historical moments excepted, this continued. And although after the return from the exile a more uniform confessional life had set in, Golgotha shows us what stamp this uniform adherence to God bore. And the Revelation of John, far from foreshadowing a future in which this relationship would be completely reversed, rather points to an end result in which the blessed will be outnumbered by the unbelievers, the demonic horde. In the Apostolic writings, too, we find no other representation than that the Christians form a small number, and the masses in Greece and Rome stand opposed to the cross. Martyrdom sets in and continues until the conversion of Constantine. With the Council of Nicaea the turnaround comes. From that moment on, ever-wider ranks of the people are brought under baptism, and the representation gains ground that Christianity is a religion that must gradually be accepted by the entire people, until finally all of Europe in all its expressions of life and institutions, and thus also in its state organization, displays a Christian character in which almost without distinction all join together. Thus the entire representation that the Old Covenant and the Apostolic literature give us was undone. There was no longer a “flock of the Lord” remaining in constant danger in a world that had sunk into unbelief, but everything was now called Christian.

—- Page 519 —-

THE BREACH HEREIN. 503 Not individual, not essential, not internal, but generally occurring in human society. In the form, for the external, in the appearance a unique and glorious state. So flat the counterpart of Golgotha.

§11. The Breach Herein.
But however beautiful the outward appearance, the inner reality did not correspond to it. So the breach could not fail to occur, and the Reformation sought to triumph over the untruthful appearance with the inner truth. In the early years of the Reformation, the situation returned to what was known from the Psalms and Prophets, from the Gospel and the Apocalypse. A situation re-entered that made the Apostle of Tarsus write: “You see your calling, brothers, that not many of you are wise, not many mighty, not many noble, but God has chosen the foolish things of the world to shame the strong.” Hence the martyrdom also returned, and the apostasy from God increased in worldly circles. The image of his Church, drawn by Jesus himself, seemed to be embodied anew. Place that image from Matthew VII: 13, 14 before you, and know the history of the Reformed in their period of martyrdom. “For the gate is wide and the way is broad that leads to destruction, and many are those who enter through it; but the gate is narrow and the way is constricted that leads to life, and few are those who find it.” These were the days they then experienced of bitter persecution, yet of holy refreshment. One found the image that Jesus had designed echoing loudly in their own lands. Only, that renewed and rejuvenated state did not last. Calvin himself, the great confessor of the little flock, set himself to the task of nevertheless regaining a Church in Geneva that would encompass the entire population. He saw the breach in France, in Scotland and in our own country, but his ideal remained to unite all the true, the only saving Church; and with this, all that had characterized the life of the Middle Ages pressed in anew on the community.

—- Page 520 —-

504 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. Not only in Geneva, but also in Scotland and in our own country, it went this way. The consequence of which had to be, and was, that the churches had to renounce discipline; that the confession sank; and that in the 19th century a state was born in which in Christ’s Church the believers were made to huddle together as a small remnant, and the Old National Church was abandoned to Modernism. This has now become the cause that not only with us, but in almost all Reformed countries, the divine foundation has gradually slipped out from under the state structure, until finally, under the adopted honorary title of Modern State, a conception of political community has penetrated, of which this is the all-dominating characteristic: “Our political life, apart from God, entirely emanating from man, and piety nothing but a private hobby for those who find pleasure in it.”

§12. The Authority from God or from Man.
This necessarily led to the political-minded and politically involved subjects gradually dividing into two parties that stood diametrically opposed to each other in their starting point for political life. The first of these two parties held fast to the conception that had thus far always prevailed, that political life must be governed by the authority of God, while the newly arisen party consciously contested this position and derived the authority that would hold in the State from the will and insight of man. Yet this principled opposition did not emerge everywhere equally conscious and decisive. It did so in France, in Germany and with us, but almost not in the United States and in Great Britain. Although Jefferson in his struggle with Hamilton had consciously chosen the irreligious position, it cannot be said that the Democrats in America are pursuing a political theory of unbelief. Even now they often make a point of appealing to the Holy Scriptures in their election campaigns. In England it remained customary for both Whigs and Tories to hold the sacred in honor. And although it gradually became the rule that in the studies on constitutional law and political science the modern starting point was pushed to the fore, yet in their public appearances before the people, one continued to pay homage to God. Even in Germany, liberal state scholars such as Bluntschli still reckoned with the majesty of God.

—- Page 521 —-

THE AUTHORITY FROM GOD OR FROM MAN. If in a political sense he had been a liberal, for him the cherished proposition was, “Christ Himself, however, would have found it difficult to come to an understanding with modern Liberalism.” Politics as Science, p. 603. On the other hand, that Christ came to establish a new world kingdom consecrated to God, is in his view nothing but a Jewish delusion, of which he does not hesitate to say: “Jesus Himself has rejected this opinion on every occasion.” Perfectly correct, if one applies this to a world dominion of the Jewish State, but the very opposite of Jesus’ announcement, as soon as the question arises of what is yet to come. In order not to alienate the crowd from sight, one began everywhere to give a religious gloss to the idea of the modern State; while on the other side the Conservative groups, who wished to perpetuate the ancient condition, still clung to a certain religious confession, not so much to explain the authority of the State, as to strengthen their own position. The antithesis: the authority from God or from man, fermented indeed in all lands without distinction in the political struggle between the past that was passing away and the modern State system that was being invoked, but did not at once establish the party division on this antithesis. Even from the Roman Catholic side, one has only after years seen the necessity of allying with the conservative or the liberal groups, to appear as a separate party formation. The Center became in Germany the embodiment of this better insight; in Belgium it remained hidden under the liberal Constitution of 1830, and only announced itself practically under the party name of Clericals, as recently in the School Struggle. What Stahl, Von Kleist Retzow and Von Gerlach aimed at, has not in Germany led to a principled party division. In Switzerland the principled antithesis revealed itself only in a few cantons. And in fact it can be established that only in our country the advocates for the Divine authority in the State law, as far as this had to come to the fore among Protestants, have appeared as a separate political party on Groen’s initiative. Everywhere else the believing Protestants remained under the leadership of the Conservatives, while younger spirits attached themselves with a certain predilection to the Democrats, sometimes even to the Socialists. But alas, as a result of this impurity, the party formation, which

—- Page 522 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. The fundamental opposition between the authority of God and the authority of man still has not been clearly enough broken through, yet it increasingly manifests itself more distinctly in the study of constitutional law. Both Protestants and Roman Catholics see the inevitable necessity of this, each in their own way. Already in more than one country, this has led to cooperation at the ballot box between these two groups. And it can be safely said that the further one proceeds leftward in unwinding the authority of the state from the bonds of divine authority, the more powerfully the sense of duty to stand up for this scorned divine authority awakens among the confessors of Christ. The excesses into which Radicalism, Socialism, Syndicalism, Anarchism, and Nihilism have run have begun to frighten people. The Doctrinaires may still cling convulsively to their theory of absolute human authority, but the irreligious character of the almost materialistic spirit of the age has long since ceased to satisfy. Rather, it begins to provoke disgust, and instead of sliding further down this precarious path, one seeks refuge in what Theosophy offers us in an Indian style. Even during the anxious war that broke out in 1914, the religious diapason rose again in politics. However hesitantly one may proceed in excluding the intermediate links between the two opposing sides, the logic itself presses the peoples forward. Whether the authority to which we must bow comes from God or from man is once and for all the most absolute and comprehensive political opposition, and its radiation makes itself felt in every domain.

§13. Gradations. By no means, however, does it follow from this that in the various countries all those who oppose the absolute divine or the absolute human authority would therefore have to join together on both sides in one and the same organized party; much less still that the two thus organized parties in the various countries would have to unite internationally in a league of defenders of divine and of human state authority. Precisely because the opposition between the theory of divine

—- Page 523 —-

VARIATIONS.507 The fundamental opposition that human authority forms in all political action allows for variations in its development. Variations arising from the fact that the nature and tendencies among children of men differ so greatly that the historical past of all peoples, far from being one, rather strongly differs, that difference in age tends to produce difference in the manner of conduct, that many interests mingle in the political struggle, which react to the loyalty to the professed principles, that the numerical distinction between the two parties brings about, that in one country a party almost naturally acquires the majority, which in the other country remains poor in number, and that in one period the one, and in the following period the other party may boast of being in possession of powerful, influential personalities. One need only think of what the name of Thorbecke on the one hand, and the name of Groen van Prinsterer on the other hand, have been able to accomplish among us. All these factors working on party life, which have applied in all times and in all regions, and have therefore almost everywhere resulted in gradations making their way upward in political development; gradations which then in essence bring out three sharper steps, depending on whether they aim at the preservation of what came down to us from the past, at the full working out of what has been accepted as necessary, and at progress to take a step forward. These three steps, these three gradations, these three variations have increasingly presented themselves in a firm, lasting form in the 19th century, and one has become accustomed to presenting oneself as Conservative, Liberal and Radical in all countries as much as possible. Not, of course, as if these three natural variations had only arisen in the 19th century. Rather, they are as old as wherever a freer state life has developed. But the three types of variations that have become current under those names date only from the beginning of the 19th century. We have already pointed out that the word Liberal in particular as a party name first came into vogue in 1814 in Spain, as a contrast to the Serviles. Earlier, the parties used to be more distinguished by names derived from a historical fact, a temporary constellation, a dynastic conflict or a dominant personality. One need only think of the Schieringers and Vetkopers, the Hoek

—- Page 524 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
Since after 1789 a radical revolution took place in the political mindset, and all attention was focused on the opposition between the Revolution and the Gospel, names have been given such as Whigs and Tories, Roundheads, Gueux, Jacobites, Welfs, Ghibellines, the Red and White Rose, or also Carlists, Marinists and Jacobins, and the like, which have receded into the background, and no longer denote persons and facts, but are borrowed from the general variations that occur everywhere, a party division. The old nationally closed character of the great state parties could no longer be maintained. The community among the peoples increased to such an extent that one was increasingly convinced that it was the same principles and the same interests that governed the political struggle in all the nations. Even though the struggle of principles and interests in all countries retained a somewhat modified character, the scientific study of constitutional law, the publicity provided by the press, the community of scholars in conferences without end, made one feel that it was the same current of life that was murmuring everywhere, and hence that the threefold basic idea of Conservative, Liberal and Radical found acceptance in all countries.
§14. A different division. Of course, in itself this would not have encountered the slightest objection, if one had applied this threefold gradation both to the party that stood up for the divine authority, and to the party that chose for the absolutist character of human authority. For most of the factors that bring about these threefold variations of Conservative, Liberal and Radical present themselves as much among the Theocrats as among the Anthropocrats. Even in our small country, everyone feels how in the one Anti-revolutionary party both men of Preservation, and men of the Continuation of the professed principles, and men of Progress present themselves. Among our Roman Catholic compatriots, one

—- Page 525 —-

LEGAL REARRANGEMENT.509 The same is true here. There are conservative, there are liberal, there are radical Roman Catholic politicians who nevertheless cling firmly and inexorably to the theocratic starting point that they find in their Church. Since this was also the case among us Anti-Revolutionaries, at least in the days of Groen van Prinsterer, it should not surprise us that more than once the Conservatives of Left and Right drew the same line, and so too the Radicals in both principled parties supported each other. Insofar as in any issue the fundamental principles came less to the fore, and the gradations on both sides more dominated the action, this avoidance of differences was entirely natural. How often did Groen van Prinsterer not gratefully praise the reverence for conscience that expressed itself in Allard Pierson’s advocacy for the free school. Also in 1894, the desire to escape the census suffrage proved to be so strongly felt on both sides that Minister Tak van Poortvliet received support from the Anti-Revolutionary side. Yet precisely then it became clear each time how extremely limited the influence is that such formal questions exert on the state of people’s minds. For with the figures it could soon be shown that the Radicals had almost everywhere given preference to the men of the Left over the Anti-Revolutionary Democrats. However desirable a more precise party division might be, into two main parties, one advocating the divine and the other the human origin of Authority, and each having to point to the three sub-parties of Conservatives, Liberals and Radicals, such a pure division cannot be achieved. What would be theoretically perfectly correct and even a demand of pure division, does not work in practice. Life is too multicolored, too composite and too complicated for political life to move along such pure lines and gradations. The three names Conservative, Liberal and Radical are therefore, increasingly, almost exclusively applied to the groups on the Left, and the Right have increasingly clearly adopted the three quite different names of Anti-Revolutionary, Roman Catholic and Christian-Historical. These three names, however, do not indicate three gradations. Rather, they stand as the one Roman Catholic and the two Protestant parties, each with its own starting point, side by side as two main groups, and one finds under

—- Page 526 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. The two main groups have three variations in degree. Among the Roman Catholics, there are three shades, and the same on the Protestant side. The Christian-Historicals stand furthest to the right, and within the Anti-Revolutionary party, the Calvinists, historical and current, distinguish themselves from the others by their democratic spirit. In essence, this means that among the Christian politicians, one first looks at the difference that arose in the 16th century through the Reformation, and then at the three variations that manifest themselves in the political movement, which then run parallel to the Conservative, Liberal, and Radical. In Germany, they have tried to combine these three gradations from the Protestant and Roman Catholic sides. In fact, some of Stahl’s followers had also gone over to the Center. Stöcker, however, felt that this would not work, and then threw himself more on the economic side of the political struggle, which did gain him some support, but politically weakened him. With us, the only good way has been taken, which leads to allowing the traditions from the 16th century to come into their own, with Protestant and Roman Catholic politics each appearing with their own organization on their own program, and further as two independent parties supporting each other.
§15. Cooperation.
This line of thought has also gradually led in the 20th century to a well-regulated cooperation between the three parties on the Right; a combination that was also tried with good results in 1909, but then failed in 1913, but which never intended to merge the three parties into an artificial unity. Abandoning one’s own principles is always paid for in the long run with loss of influence and ultimately with powerlessness. Protestant and Roman Catholic politicians cannot merge, because both place the origin of political authority in God, but diverge in how God makes this authority valid. Then, on the Roman Catholic side, one is referred primarily to the Vicar of Christ, while the Protestant politicians refer to the authority of the Holy Scriptures and the common...

—- Page 527 —-

COOPERATION.511Grace. The requirement of this mutual position is therefore that we leave our Roman Catholic allies and their position here undisputed, and that we seek our cooperation only in the sharply formulated solutions of such issues that govern the political situation and in which we on both sides, each from our own premises, come to a concordant conclusion. The merging of those who since 1894 chose “Christian-Historical” with the party of Groen van Prinsterer, which lives on in the Anti-Revolutionaries, would of course be unthinkable, because the authority of Holy Scripture is recognized as the highest authority on both sides, and Calvinism and the Revival are merely two expressions of the same endeavor; but there is certainly an advantage if these two parties also maintain their own household. There are still far too many ecclesiastical, social and personal differentiations here, which would only weaken the Anti-Revolutionary party if it were to join in its organization and action. Our conclusion therefore remains that the two main parties have split according to the opposing convictions regarding the source of Authority, and that furthermore the subdivisions in the organized parties among the Left can only be distinguished according to the three gradations in the Conservative, Liberal and Radical elements, while on the Right the historical distinction of Protestant and Roman Catholic position is primarily dominant, although variations occur in both of these main parties that partly have an ecclesiastical and partly a gradual character, and do not allow for any merger. Socialism finds no place in this framework, because it is a party not of principle, but of class interest, and has only fantasized its political system in order to be able to serve this class interest under the guise of a higher idea. When the war broke out in 1914, one saw how even the idea of internationalism melted away. With the principled parties, one could perhaps still include Anarchism and Nihilism as dead-end offshoots of Radicalism, but insofar as these factions proceed from the absolute denial of all authority, they cannot logically be classified under the group that seeks the source of authority in man. Rather, it must be said that they can and do not seek a place among the political parties.

—- Page 528 —-

POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. As for the parties of principled political character, we must therefore confine ourselves to the two main groups, each with its threefold variation. Both on the other side and on our side, the affinity always reasserts itself in these variations. This came out less strongly in the period when a single variation managed to gain a majority in Parliament, but it stood out more sharply as soon as this ceased, and a majority in Parliament could no longer be formed by the mere combination of variations. From that moment on, an alliance of parties has been visible on both sides of Parliament—not only with us, but also elsewhere, presented as Right and Left; a designation that, without artifice, expressed their mutual affiliation, serving the purpose of combining three groups on both sides.

§16. Personal Groups. The repeatedly attempted effort to discredit the opposing party as a mere personal following and to degrade it to a personal clique has sometimes succeeded for a moment, but has always shipwrecked in the long run. Thus, one knows how the Old Conservative group, which set the tone from 1815 to 1830, after 1848 constantly tried to speak of Thorbeckianism, in an attempt to create the impression that the newly emerged Liberalism, far from being a principled political party, was to be regarded merely as a clique of personal admirers of Thorbecke; and the Liberals who appeared after 1848 tried in the same way to underestimate the following of Groen van Prinsterer. In their eyes, the Anti-Revolutionaries did not form a principled political party, but merely a group strung together by reverence for Groen’s talents, which would unravel of itself after his death. This pretense found all the more acceptance because such personality-bound cliques have indeed sometimes appeared. One need only think of the Boulangists in France. But the outcome itself spares us all uncertainty. Boulangism fizzled out as soon as Boulanger’s clique was overthrown, and he died. Here, on the other hand, Liberalism has only become more firmly established after Thorbecke’s, and the Anti-Revolutionary party has become more firmly established after Groen van Prinsterer’s death.

—- Page 529 —-

PERSONAL GROUPS. SPLINTERING. Since Groen van Prinsterer, before his death, transferred the commander’s staff to me, the political significance of our party has steadily increased. From the other side, repeated attempts have been made, first to speak of Groenians, and then, when Groen’s name could no longer be of service, to speak of Kuyperianism, in order, if possible, to damage the credit of our party once again. But this attempt too must be regarded as a failure. Since Groen van Prinsterer, three cabinets have already taken the field from the Right, and the political significance of our party has steadily increased. The ignoble political trick has thus failed in all three of the cases mentioned. Yet it cannot be denied that every leader, in whatever country, suffers from personal peculiarities, which may indeed benefit the party in some ways, but which may also sometimes bring it into disrepute. But it does not last long before both the supporters and the detractors of such statesmen very well perceive the real political power that constantly emanates from them, despite the personal often hindering factors. This result is then recorded by history, and thereafter the party in the country itself resumes its natural course, entirely apart from its deceased leader.

§17. Splintering. Another very serious problem of kindred nature is, unfortunately, all too native to us, and consists in the splintering that is wont to occur in every politically principled party, through the standing on one’s own feet of individualists, with small groups behind them, who, while still belonging in principle to the party from which they have separated, nevertheless set out to create a small, kindred side-party, at the expense of the unity of the party. It is often wrongly attributed solely to personal ambition that such faction leaders engage in this. We do not deny that such a sinful tendency often mingles with such an appearance. Whoever was closely acquainted with such faction leaders was all too soon convinced of this. But one is mistaken who thinks that all such splitting up is to be explained solely by ambition or self-conceit. The party life itself, by its very nature, presses towards faction formation.

—- Page 530 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. Its foundation. This lies in the public character that is inseparable from every political party. Every party has its twelve or more periodicals in which it expresses itself, every party holds meetings, summons assemblies, and pleads in Parliament for its principles or its interests. In this public work in the press, in meetings, and in Parliament, the party members naturally come to the fore more than others. This leads to the result that each of these prominent party members makes his personal opinion known. This coming forward for his convictions often provokes contradiction, criticism, and disapproval. And it has happened more than once that one sees but too often that he who is elected by a majority of votes still gets a small dissenting group on his hands. In that group, then, consolation is sought after the defeat. And if in that smaller circle there is a lack of insight and statesmanship, then but too easily the idea arises to “not lay down the head,” as the saying goes, and it has happened more than once that it then depends on mere chance whether the separation will come or not. One has seen this on the Left with Kuyper v.d. Copello, before him in the colonial field already in Franssen v.d. Putten. Wintgens and V. Nierstrasz both called themselves Conservative, but always turned the rudder of the ship to a different side; on the Roman Catholic side one was by no means satisfied in all corners with Schaepman’s actions; among ourselves we know how first a small group of Bronsveldianen, and later a small group of Buytendijkianen, separated themselves; and also in recent times an action came forth from our cooperation with Rome, which was soon followed by what Keuchenius’ son committed. Always something on their own. One of our ardent advocates for proportional representation really thought he had to place even his own political banner against the common political opponent. C.E. van Koetsveld was his name. Now we do not doubt for a moment that all these Einspänner were of excellent race and spoke out of sincere conviction; only, individualism was too strong for them. If one applies their individualistic self-sufficiency in every family, in every church, among every teacher, in every army under the commanders, in every fleet under the commanding officers, then we get a whole mess on earth, and thus all regulated operation of our vital forces would be forfeited.

—- Page 531 —-

PEELING AWAY.515 It is the same with the members in our body. Whoever allows one-sided prominence of certain members, and overly privileges or misuses certain organs, breaks the great power of the organism that normally works together in all its members and organs. And so it is here as well. Merely going along with the crowd, without individual personal development, brings harm to the whole; but then again, an underestimation of the mutual connection, in order to make the individual shine above the rest, inflicts an equally deadly wound upon the organic community. In constitutional England, they have long understood this, and the most zealous politicians in Great Britain know exceedingly well how, without a virtuous organizational connection, any army falls apart and is defeated, and conversely, how only the virtuous connection of the parts can lead the whole to victory. England owes this to its past, and likewise to the abundance of its Parliament; where parties of +300 members stand against each other in the same Parliament, no one dares to lightly attempt separation. It does occur, as one saw in the swing under Chamberlain, but in England such a Sonderbündler is still a very rare exception; and when it does occur, it is undertaken on a grand scale, not with a little clique, but with a massive group. Our country, on the other hand, has only 100 members in Parliament; half of them are 50; and when such a half, like ours, further divides into three gradations, the independent, principled parties cannot but be small in number. The Union-Liberal and the Roman Catholic clubs are now the most numerous; but on the one issue, the Romans only went along once, and then barely enough to make a difference. The binding bond is therefore easily slack among us all. And when you add the polder spirit, which from of old has channeled our politics into all kinds of little groups and subgroups, the outcome is to be foreseen, and one must always be on the lookout for the emergence of new factions. History shows well that they do not take root, and are entirely forgotten after a few years, but what is in the nature of a people cannot be turned, and in this case even less so, since the political opponents naturally serenade under the window in the dead of night like violins. It has been so from of old. Prince William suffered under it. Our entire past

—- Page 532 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM
Has been shattered. Even the plague that befell us under Napoleon has not eradicated this evil. Thorbecke had to yield to his recalcitrants. Groen van Prinsterer stood at last like a general without an army. And even after his death, the Einspännerei (one-man show) in politics has repeatedly celebrated its heyday. The poppy heads must still be cut off. Without equality, no freedom, and brotherhood becomes the victim. For this tragic aspect of our political drama, let us not be too quick to lower the curtain. It is an evil that has been ingrained in us for centuries, against which we cannot arm ourselves seriously enough. And this all the more so, since, as could not be otherwise, there is also all kinds of provincial variation, ecclesiastical distinction, class difference, and class distinction added, in order to unravel the already so tangled embroidery even further. The so important significance of organization in community must come more to its right among us, or we will hand it over to the opposing party. A fear, therefore, all the more to be dreaded, because neither Right nor Left can find a single party in our country that has not in turn been lost to this Einspännerei. Defeat, oppression, violation, and injury of our rights have all too often proved to be the only means of being able to harness our four-footed team again and drive them out together. There is a strength in the fact that our citizens, even in our larger cities, still mostly live in their own homes. The custom abroad, to house four, five families together, each on one floor, has something that does not appeal to the Dutch heart. A patriotic team you will never get, as long as this whip does not pass over, with your hodgepodge of Einspännerei.

§17. Alongside the principles, the interests. That alongside the political principles, common interests can also lead to party formation, is shown in particular by the agricultural group in some countries, and, since Marx pushed the working class to the fore, Socialism in its various shades. The representation given by Rehmen and others, as if, on closer inspection, almost all political parties ultimately owe their origin to...

—- Page 533 —-

INTERESTS ALONGSIDE PRINCIPLES.
I cannot concede that conflicting interests are the sole basis for political parties. I do not wish to idealize, and I acknowledge without reservation that there has scarcely ever been a political party that, once its power increased, did not owe its continued existence and growing influence in part to the satisfaction of all manner of interests. Quite certainly, opposition parties that have never yet held the reins of government are the purest in pursuing a principled goal. Without hesitation, I recognize that even our own party stood the purest when Kuyper threw down the gauntlet in the School Struggle. Then a unity of prayer arose among us, betraying a holy spiritual fervor. Scarcely a word was spoken of subsidies. That one of our own would be entrusted with a ministerial portfolio did not occur to anyone. Not even for a promising office did anyone compete. It was then about the cause, and the cause alone. The youth of the fatherland must not be spiritually ennobled on the “modern sect school,” as Groen van Prinsterer had painted it. Whoever then, as I myself was, had the opportunity to closely observe the outcome of the action then set in motion, would sometimes have considered it impossible that an “interest politics,” as the Germans call it, could ever emasculate us. And yet, since we gained a foothold in 1891, this clinging to interests has indeed come to the fore among us as well. To deny this would be to contradict the facts. With an ever-increasing number, the desire emerged to be placed in an honorable post, preferably with a generous annual income. In the school question, one could repeatedly observe in the expressions one heard how various calculations had, if not displaced, then at least weakened the former unwavering commitment to principle. Subsidy became the watchword for not a few. For raises in salaries and pensions, we sang along with the Left. To emerge socially as the fruit of political zeal and devotion also smiled on us from all sides. I will certainly refrain from exaggerating here. For I know very well how it has been and still is in all lands and among all parties. I even concede that the pursuit of one’s own interests does not usually let go of the struggle for holy principles. In the days of martyrdom, one suffers for one’s principle; but once the door of triumph has been opened, restraint is always imposed and the principle is gradually accommodated to the interests.

—- Page 534 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. The longing for martyrdom has replaced the desire for gain in all true awareness. Even more so than in our Christian parties, in all the other parties, already for the obvious reason that the “Little People” among us form the vast majority, and that we are so limited in our numbers under the pretext of the higher office. I also gratefully acknowledge that, alongside the longing to promote one’s own interests, the inspiring zeal for the principle not only remained a spark, but continually rekindled into a flame. What I have observed, however, is intended only to avoid the appearance, as if we on our part were blind to the interference of the interest that ultimately comes to threaten the struggle for the principle. That this danger is not imaginary, I acknowledge, and I do not even conceal that the interference of interest, as soon as we become a winning party, could not fail to occur. Even in the Christian parties, one has to deal not with angels, but with men. Principles and interests stand in relation to each other as soul and body. The principle is the unseen, the interest the sensual driving force. Now that the opposition is almost entirely dependent on the unseen, spiritual driving force, and the party in the majority also strives for the visible, it is perfectly understandable that the party in opposition as a rule stands morally higher than the party that has control over both the invisible and the visible element. It is a distortion of the position, if the party that has been brought into the minority, and is now thus again in opposition, reproaches the party that has decapitated it, as if it were allowing the interests to have a say, as if this were its own alone, and as if one had not sinned oneself in this regard. This amounts to playing the hypocrite.
§19. Appointment of Mayors. Before I became a minister, I had asked for a list to be provided to me of the political coloring of the mayors in the various provinces, in order to learn the state of affairs. The nomination with the appointment of places of mayors was handed to me. The result turned out to be that of the +180 municipalities in South Holland at that time, only one was Antirevolutionary, three were Roman Catholic, three were Christian-Historical, and all the rest were Liberal. This state of affairs, which I found in all the other provinces as well, could not but strike me as highly unsatisfactory. For the appointment of mayors is one of the most important means by which the government can influence the political orientation of the municipalities. And if this appointment is made in such a way that the political complexion of the municipalities is almost completely determined by the Liberal element, then the government, in my opinion, fails in its duty to maintain the balance between the different political tendencies in the country.

—- Page 535 —-

APPOINTMENTS OF MAYORS. In the remaining sixty-three municipalities, a Liberal was appointed as mayor. In Friesland, the situation was as follows: out of 43 municipalities, 36 had Liberal mayors, 4 had Anti-Revolutionaries, and 3 had Christian-Historical mayors. In North Holland, with 144 municipalities, 122 had Liberal mayors, 3 had Anti-Revolutionaries, 3 had Christian-Historicals, and 6 had Roman Catholic gentlemen. In Overijssel, out of 61 municipalities, 56 had Liberal mayors, 4 had Roman Catholic ones, 1 had a Christian-Historical, and not a single Anti-Revolutionary. In Drenthe, the record was set with 32 Liberals out of 33 municipalities; Groningen had the same, with all its 52 municipalities occupied by Liberals, with the sole exception of Nieuw-Pekela. Only in Zeeland was the situation somewhat less partisan. Zeeland had 103 municipalities; 79 of these had Liberal mayors, and 24 had men from the opposition, divided as follows: 12 Roman Catholic, 11 Anti-Revolutionaries, and 1 Christian-Historical. North Brabant and Limburg were not investigated at the time, partly because I did not know any like-minded people there who could provide information, and partly because these two provinces, being almost exclusively populated by Roman Catholics, have never been on the same wavelength as the provinces north of the Moerdijk. North of the Moerdijk, there were therefore 539 Liberal mayors, and only 67 from the three circles of the opposition combined. If one now adds that the Mackay Cabinet had appointed thirty-nine mayors from the Right, so that only about fourteen non-Liberals were appointed from the Liberal side, one is still amazed at the nonchalance with which the Liberal ministers of the Interior had advised the Crown for the vacant positions. Yet, if one compares these mayoral figures with the voting figures in the elections preceding the year 1900 (the year of my investigation), the outcome shows that the three parties in opposition, taken together as a whole, at least outnumbered the various liberal groups. Duty therefore demanded that this glaring party inequality, provided the quality of the candidates did not object to it, be evened out. In 1905, when the ballot box was opened again, it therefore cost little effort to show that my Cabinet had proposed more mayors from the Right than from the Left, and I could have no objection to the fact that a list was made of this. For that list proved nothing else.

—- Page 536 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM. But the extreme moderation with which I had pressed for the restoration of injustice. On the other hand, the fact that they distilled an act of accusation against me from this list was the ne plus ultra of shameless partisanship. The all-decisive circumstance, however, that the previous permanent spirit of exclusion of the Liberal Cabinets had undermined the policy of the Cabinet, was entirely suppressed, and they assumed the holy appearance as if the Liberal Cabinets had never forsaken the plumb line of equity and as if with my appearance partisan spirit had come to the fore for the first time. As a sample, I thought it not unimportant to insert this calculation here, since the interest to be placed in office can endanger the principled character of the political parties.

§20. Other appointments. Only to refute the objection that it was indeed the office of Mayor that had led to such Liberal party extravagances, but that otherwise from the Liberal side it had always been aimed at the pursuit of rights and equity, I add here subsidiarily how it stood in 1900 with the Inspection of the Elementary Schools. There were then in function 20 School Inspectors for the Districts and 104 for the Arrondissements; together 124. Of the 20 District School Inspectors, now 15 were Liberal and only 5 from the Right, and of the 104 Arrondissement School Inspectors, 79 belonged to the Liberal, and only 25 to the non-Liberal groups. Of the 124, it therefore stood at 94 Liberals and only 30 on our side, and here too applies what I remarked above, that the Mackay Cabinet had already made some improvement, so that it stood even more stark before the appearance of this Cabinet. If one now looks at the ratio between Right and Left in the Governor-Generalship of the East, the Governorship in the West, in the Council of State, in the Supreme Court, in the Court of Audit, the other judicial colleges, the public Universities, the Gymnasiums and the Secondary schools, or any other institution for which the right of appointment rests with the Government, then one comes to an even more deplorable result

—- Page 537 —-

OTHER APPOINTMENTS. 521 results. For most of these institutions, it was actually as if there were no other parties in the Netherlands besides the Liberal parties. Where I would admit that among us Anti-Revolutionaries after 1890, 1904, and 1913—that is, after the years in which three Right-wing cabinets under Mackay, Kuyper, and Heemskerk took office—the interest in devotion to the Anti-Revolutionary party began to compete all too noticeably with the love for our principle, the exclusion that the Liberal monopoly had caused us to experience has never been forgotten. It is a fact that where, both on the Right and on the Left, the interest was asserted alongside the principle, we too by no means go forth as saints, but that, undoubtedly, if one compares the Right with the Left on this point, under our cabinets there has never been any talk of the application of the exclusivist monopoly in which the Left always sought to exert its power. But although this difference must be established, and I do not exaggerate in the least in calculating it to our advantage, it must still be admitted that a party in power, or with a chance of coming to power, is always in danger of having the interest, especially in the matter of offices and positions, break the holy impulse of the principle. In the end, one is not even ashamed to come out publicly for this. The principle therefore remains the main guide, but the soul of the party suffers at the expense of the body. Honesty even demands that it be admitted that this shadow side of party life must have fallen much darker on the Liberals, or even in general on the Left-wing groups, than on us. The longer one is in power, the wider the circle becomes that is feeding on positions and salaries. This in turn brings about that the party is also regarded by more neutral spirits as the fixed distributor of honors and positions. Thus, the entire phalanx of opinion-less hangers-on gradually swings over to the ruling party. There arises a sense as if this ruling party is really the only one that counts. Whatever lies outside it is considered to be of less importance. And it is not long before the opinion gains ground that it alone has the country of office-holders in its possession. It was indeed this long duration of rule that almost inevitably brought about that shameless partisanship in appointments of which I spoke.

—- Page 538 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
The fact that among us the preponderance still lies so much more on the side of the principle, I therefore constantly place on the other scale the question of how it would have gone with us if we too had been granted an uninterrupted administration as from 1848—’90, that is, of nearly half a century. In degree, the disease symptom has not yet progressed so far as on the other side. In every electoral setback lies for this disease case still the medicine. And so too can one in the press and at the meetings insist enough that one should never ascribe a mightier influence in his personal conviction to the interest, in proportion to the principle, than the reality of life imperatively demands. To ideal and reality the just proportion must also here remain assigned. Official positions are by no means only marks of honor, with advantage of position, but they are also the members of the body politic that must serve the principle, and in whose influence the principle cannot be missed, or it will be the penance for its active operation on the body politic, and thereby also on the life of the people. Not only, however, that up to 1890 Liberalism excluded us from the national family circle, but the Anti-revolutionaries too did not cease to feed this illusion, shy as they were, at least up to Kappeynes “Sharp Resolution,” in their tents. This was dereliction of duty, especially after a man like Groen van Prinsterer had been given to us, to throw open the shutters and let the full light come in again, and it is therefore not too much to blame Liberalism that it has made use of this insensitivity of the believers to appropriate the profit thereof for itself. It is above all the long duration in the administration of the parties that here causes the luster in the political life-blood to fade. Already in England and America one can observe this, and with the eye at least on these two countries, there is truth in Rehm’s remark that ultimately the interest of the party comrades pushes the principle, which once united their fathers, more and more into the background. From father and grandfather the descendant finally feels no other, or they formed a separate caste in the country, which always stands up for the common caste position, regardless of which side the political action may go out. At a meeting held in Holland, Michigan, I allowed myself, above all, to urge the Republicans

—- Page 539 —-

THE MONEY QUESTION.
This weakening of Hamilton’s starting point also led me to point out to my Republican fellow-emigrants, to whose group most Dutch emigrants also belonged, and already the next morning I could read in the Democratic newspapers that I had urged my fellow-countrymen to join the Democratic party. I had already pointed out how the same thing was happening in England. Therefore, it cannot be emphasized enough that among us, too, against this calcification, as I call it, of the life-artery in our political life, we must be warned. To set this aside is not the interest in party life, but a party that began with the principle in its banner, and then degenerates into a hunt for positions and salaries, forfeits the advantage of its initial lead by itself.

§21. The Money Question.
Of a completely different character is a second kind of interests, which can influence the emergence and striving of parties, and which arises from the necessity for the Government of the country to provide the necessary financial resources. The tax system in its full extent weighs heavily on the population. It can press on them with fairness, and it can press on them in a very unfair manner. Everyone who comes into consideration to contribute is therefore self-interested here, and, with the exception of a few zealots and zealots, it can safely be said that in the question of how a government will come to the money it needs, all parties without distinction have a serious word to say. The group and class interest necessarily makes itself felt more and more strongly here, the higher the total amount of the budget climbs, and this climb is indeed overwhelming not to be overlooked. Of course, in connection with this, the population figure and further the interest and redemption of the national debt must always be taken into account. In 1849 the amount granted for the twelve main chapters of our State Budget was not more than ƒ69,646,709. Even in 1866, seventeen years later, this for the main resources was still approximately at this amount and amounted to ƒ69,217,000. But in 1900 this had already risen to a total of ƒ137,744,000, and fully to ƒ153,354,000, and for 1914 even a total expenditure of ƒ195,355,000 was granted, for the main resources alone. We now stand

—- Page 540 —-

THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
The total revenue had already risen to around 230,000,000 guilders, while the population in 1830 was estimated at 2,613,487, in 1909 at 5,858,175 inhabitants, and has since grown to 6,350,000. So since 1848 there has been an increase of 108%, about a doubling, while the expenditures have nearly quadrupled. Given this state of affairs, it could not be otherwise—the political parties had to include the financial question in their party program as well; and since every party program must naturally be aimed at attracting as large a number of voters as possible, it could not fail to create a strong tendency to let the private interests of the various groups of voters weigh in when determining its financial program. This affected the relationship between direct and indirect taxes, as the former always repel the most, but also, on the other hand, the proportionality between incomes and the amounts to be paid in taxes. Certainly, in an earlier period, too little attention was paid to the greater or lesser ease with which something could be contributed from the smaller or larger income for the benefit of the Kingdom, the Province, and the Municipality. Proportionately, the more affluent paid too little; the less affluent, and especially the middle class, too much. Now, of course, the improvement to be made in this respect had to be connected with the economic rules governing the social condition, but as the right to vote was gradually extended and the various political parties gained an ever-broader class of taxpayers, it could not be otherwise than that among the voters the desire had to arise from all sides to pay less, or at least not to be taxed disproportionately higher. And since this was a very essential interest for the parties to curry favor with the broader groups of voters, one party did this more shamelessly than the other, but none went scot-free. I have constantly urged that the more affluent should bear a larger share of the required amount and that the unfortunate who belong to the lowest class of wealth should be completely exempted. An element that could not be neglected and could not be left out of the game as long as it was still the paying or not paying of taxes that co-decided the right to vote or not. Undoubtedly, this interest in paying too little or too much has also influenced the party program of the Anti-Revolutionaries.

—- Page 541 —-

THE ECONOMIC STRUGGLE.525
Their influence must be felt. A party that has long harbored the “common people,” and has since been reinforced by little more than a narrow fringe of Capitalists, must keep a sharp eye on the tax burden. Naturally, this then gives rise to differences of opinion within the party. There are also among us who see an advantage for their own finances in shifting the burdens even more away from the common man, and transferring them in an increased percentage to the more affluent; while conversely the middle-class and the more well-to-do, from whom even more would be demanded, resist this reversal in the financial domain.

§22. The Economic Struggle.
In the third place, the principle is threatened to be pushed back in the economic struggle between the various social enterprises of agriculture, industry, trade, shipping, fisheries, and all kinds of crafts. Continual attempts are made on this account to unite the stakeholders in agriculture into a firm organization, and to have them act in concert for whatever can promote agriculture. In Germany, the so-called Agrarian Party has already come forward so completely independently for this purpose, that it not only sought to exert influence on the major political parties, but especially in the eastern part of Germany, preferred to appear as an entirely independent party alongside the political parties, and through the dependence in which more than one district thereby came to be in regard to its resistance or support, came to control the outcome of the ballot box. As a consequence of this, the land question has also been pushed more and more to the forefront, both in England and America, as well as in Germany and Austria; and when Henry George appeared with his entire peculiar system, it was soon able to boast of broad sympathy. More and more, the conviction broke through that, with the steady increase of the population, the ownership of the land must not become a plaything in the hands of accidental power-holders. The soil must serve the population, and conditions such as emerged in England and Scotland, where a few private individuals came to possess a much too remarkable part of the national territory, and withdrew this from the nourishment of the people, increasingly presented themselves as untenable. Thus, it has not yet come so far in our country, since such abuse of the soil has hardly manifested itself among us until now. On the other hand, Big Capital has also made itself master of the soil to a greater extent as an investment medium. This weakened the position of the peasant class, and against this evil, flowing both from this and from other phenomena, the great Association then arose, which, originating in the South under the name of the Farmers’ Union, has now already found ramifications in the greater part of our country. So far, the establishment of this Union has not yet led to any significant political intervention, but it is nevertheless evident that this can be expected at any moment, and that this could weaken the political character of our State Parties, by introducing an impure admixture into the ballot box.

—- Page 542 —-

526 THE POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEM.
§23. The Tariff Question.
The struggle over the Tariff, which played such a strong role in the 1913 election, moved along the same line. Here too, the appeal was almost exclusively to the interests of the stakeholders. Undoubtedly, the Tariff question was also very amenable to scientific treatment, and the principled question of whether the coexistence of peoples should lead to free trade or to protection is an extremely important problem in economic terms, which is also very closely connected with the struggle between nationalism and cosmopolitanism. However, the scientific study of this issue can never lead to a general conclusion that would indicate the choice to be made for each particular country at any given moment. If we had to deal with a world government that could bring the choice to a decision for all peoples at once, then an absolute choice, provided it was for all peoples at the same time, would be very conceivable. Now, on the contrary, since such a world government neither is nor can be expected, and the government of each country separately has to decide its own course, the choice is by no means determined solely by principled consideration, but must even be very closely observed in terms of the choice

—- Page 543 —-

THE TARIFF QUESTION.527 The commercial usages that had gained currency in other lands. Something that applied even more to our country, where—outside of England—not a single other country on the face of the earth had introduced the Free Trade system, and where the yield from the Tariff was still steadily rising. Matters standing thus, the dispute over Free Trade and Protection has indeed been treated on principle in the lecture halls and in the handbooks; but at the ballot box, it has been almost exclusively with an eye to the interests of the various groups within the citizenry. The opponents of Tariff increases have in every way frightened the inhabitants with the price increases that the goods in general, and certain much-used articles (one thinks only of clogs), would have to undergo. This has driven a number of voters to fear for their purses, and the district figures show that this question of interests almost entirely dominated the outcome of the political elections in June and July 1913. The foreign trade, this goes without saying, would have preferred to abolish all Tariffs; but through the very extensive transshipment of what came into Rotterdam and Amsterdam to Germany, the domestic trade and shipping were also apprehensive of losing out, should the Tariff be increased. Our industry, with an eye to our low Tariff, had in part sought salvation in a very special form of production, and this part of our industry spoke up with authority for Free Trade, while the other part, which had precisely not been able to come up because of the low Tariff, now also could not appear as a power at the Ballot Box. And so in the end, in June/July 1913, not only was this economic question entirely dominated at the Ballot Box by the interests of the economic groups, but one also saw the chance to completely dominate the entire political struggle on the political inheritance.
