He remained steadfast in the Christian character of the English State, but the freedom of the Churches was fully recognized. In §36 it says: “That to the public profession, held forth, none shall be compelled by penalties or otherwise”; and in §37: “that such as profess faith in God by Jesus Christ, though differing in judgment from the doctrine, worship or discipline publicly held forth, shall not be restrained from, but shall be protected in the profession of faith.” Cromwell as a man of action could not follow these “Saints” and let them have their way. The breach with them finally gave him the European position of Protector of Protestantism, and it was his triumph as Moderator of European Protestantism, which at the same time dealt the death blow to the all-spiritualizing enthusiasm that for a moment had the fate of England in its hands. This driving of the spiritual enthusiasm that the Puritans and Saints had produced, also gradually lost its driving force, and the moment approached when the political element had to separate itself from this movement. Both had still been most closely united in the original Independentism, but now they went their separate ways, leading to the establishment of a quiet ecclesiastical Independentism and the rise of an independent, democratically tinged politics, until finally George Fox in Quakerism once again gave voice to the element of enthusiastic dualism, to prepare the way in which this whole spiritual movement would transplant itself to America, leaving in England itself, next to Presbyterian Scotland, nothing but the moderate Episcopal Church with the Non-Conformists.

§55. Consolidation in 1689. It is here that the entire religious action in England culminated.

—- Page 717 —-

CONSOLIDATION IN 1689. What was consolidated in 1689 by our Stadtholder was the result of a process that had been going on since 1701. The so-called Toleration Act gave—and this was the main point—the principle the victory, that the State and the Church were inseparably one. In place of this, a state of affairs now arose which, while regarding the Episcopal Church as the National Church, nevertheless granted full freedom of movement to the Dissenters or Non-Conformists as religious groups, and excluded all spiritual domination. The Quakers were relieved of the oath and could now suffice with a declaration of obedience. And without partiality, it may be stated that in the more than hundred-year struggle, which William III as a peacemaker might bring to an end, not the Episcopal, but the Non-Conformist principle of the Puritans had gained the victory. Politics and Religion, which Henry VIII had fused into absolute unity, now went completely their separate ways. Although the Non-Conformists, both Presbyterians and Puritans, Independents and Quakers, had also initially taken up their own political position, this political action of theirs had more of a negative than a positive character. From the outset, it turned against all politics that made the Government mistress of the spiritual life, and pleaded for freedom. But when this plea had finally been won, and the religious peace had been consolidated by William of Orange, the Dissenters soon detached themselves from all politics and withdrew into their ecclesiastical or spiritual domain. The political elements that had joined them not for Christ’s sake, but out of political calculation, or at least out of political sympathies, separated from them. The Whigs came to stand opposed to the Tories, and even a still more radical element, which could not be acquitted of all revolutionary intentions, emerged from the Dissenter camp. Just as a weak remnant of the Anabaptists lived on among us and in Germany in the Mennonites or Baptists, so in England too the Quaker element continued in Baptism, but only on a small scale and without political significance. The spiritual stream that for more than a century had caused the religious life in England to surge higher than in any other country, steadily declined in strength, and the

—- Page 718 —-

The Calvinist civic and social life consolidated itself both politically and ecclesiastically in a well-established form—politically as a constitutional and religiously as the dominant state church with high privileges, yet with full freedom of movement for all dissenting currents alongside it.

§56. Calvin’s and Languet’s Ecclesiastical Unity. To properly appreciate this outcome, one must of course go back to the starting point of this whole action, just as it had first announced itself in Calvin, and then was systematized by Languet in the French religious wars: Not the Church and its own sphere in the State, but the State as a civic household as an element in the Church, and that Church encompassing the entire people, with only the Jews as an exception. Applying the Old Testament model of the people’s state of Israel, Calvin, and his followers, however self-conscious they were in trying to place the authority, both ecclesiastical and political, in the hands of the “best,” i.e., the spiritually and intellectually highest-ranking, could not escape the fact that, by virtue of the standpoint of faith, and especially in connection with the confession of election, co-determination accrued to all members, just as had been the case in the Kahal of Israel. Calvin might now express this through the consensus populi, and Languet more as a sovereignty granted by God to the people, but in any case the position that accrued to everyone in the political sphere was governed by their position in the spiritual realm. Whoever was baptized and had longed for access to the Holy Communion was fully entitled in the Church of the Lord, and as such also fully entitled in civil society. For him who stood outside the spiritual community of the Church, on the other hand, there was no place in the thus Christianized civil society. And it had to be so—there could only be one, the only valid Church, that would rule, so that the coexistence of various, mutually exclusive Churches in confession would be an absurdity. Rome’s confession that the Truth was only one, that there could only be one Church, and that this Church had to be supported and maintained by all authorities, under the absolute exclusion and suppression of all sects and dissenters, was on this standpoint the only logical and consistent, and found its seal in the alleged guardianship of Christ. Even freedom of conscience could not be respected on this standpoint. When one broke with Rome, one therefore faced the choice of either realizing the goal that Rome was aiming for in a different way, or choosing a different goal. If one chose the former, it came down to shaking off the papal yoke from the entire Church of Christ in all lands and reforming it according to the confession of Protestantism. There could then be no Roman Catholic Church left alongside the Reformed Church. The Church of Christ remained one. It only had to shed the impure garment that the Hierarchy had wrapped around it, and then it would be Reformed, but still one and the same universal Church of Christ and remain so. It was therefore only a matter of providing a better unity bond for the hierarchy, which now fell away, in its place. And just as previously the task had rested on the authorities to support and protect the Church of the hierarchy, and to defend against all sects and apostasy, so now the same task would remain with the authorities, but to support the Churches in synodal bond. The authorities would no longer have to determine what would apply in the Church of Christ. This would organize its entire household completely independently. But just as the Church constituted itself entirely independently of the authorities, so the authorities would uphold and protect it. Freedom of conscience could be better respected. Some indulgences could be allowed. Especially in other parts of the world, much could be overlooked. The Churches of the East, which had never submitted to Rome, could, at least temporarily, be spared. But in the Christian land, only he who was a member of the one true Church of Christ could claim the right to co-determination. It was and remained the one, true Church, which, going out over the whole world, embraced all peoples, and the respective authorities in the various lands could do no other than resist what opposed this Church, and bind, yes, kill with the sword, whatever threatened the life of the Church.

—- Page 719 —-

Page 720—-
704 CALVINISTIC.
§57. The establishment of the new Church bond failed. The question now was whether the Reformed would succeed in finding a Church bond that could replace the bond of the Hierarchy. When this completely failed, and it soon became clear that there was no prospect of ever being able to gather a complete Church of Christ in all lands represented in a synodical assembly or council, so that even the Synod of 1618 in Dordrecht ultimately lost its international character and was rather announced as an official and narrower National Synod, it became clear that Calvin had allowed himself to be misled in his expectations; that what he had hoped for failed to materialize; and that one soon found oneself facing a factual state of division, which mocked every institutional unity, even though the organic unity of the Church as an organism remained intact. Even before the 16th century drew to a close, it became crystal clear that the Hierarchy had certainly received a formidable blow, but nevertheless maintained itself with vigor and even went towards a new evolution of strength. From the entire South of Europe, there was soon no more hope for the Reformation. It was equally clear that in the East of our part of the world, the old Byzantine Church continued its caesaropapism unremittingly. And it soon became clear how in the German States that heeded Luther’s call, the caesaropapistic evil crept in, which had already proceeded to the persecution of the Reformed. Something to which, to the utmost calamity, was to be added that while in the Netherlands and in Scotland Calvinism was making progress, in France it had to experience the Bartholomew’s Night as early as 1572, in England it saw itself displaced by the Independents and Quakers, and from Belgium it was completely banished, yes, even in the Netherlands it did not win the Generality Lands. This led in the 17th century to an outcome that made the adherence to the unity of the ecclesiastical institute absolutely impossible. Calvin did not experience this. But his followers soon found themselves faced with the insoluble difficulty that the unity of the Church as an institute became mere appearance; that Rome maintained its ecclesiastical institute with unbroken power; and that the Reformation itself, in spite of and against its will, did not belong to one, but to a number of ecclesiastical institutes, which in confession, in divine service, in church government and in public appearance increasingly diverged from each other. What had been aimed at proved unattainable. The means employed did not hit the mark, and when now, with the exception of a part of Germany, Switzerland and the Netherlands, the entire European Continent again came under the Roman institute, the Greek institute or the Lutheran institute, it was settled that the appeal to the people of Israel could no longer be maintained, and that Calvinism now had to know how to bring forth a completely different institutionalization of ecclesiastical life from its own principles, or, if it could not do so, it would die out. It was not just a matter of a principle of ecclesiastical, but of political action. What had been tried in the second period of Calvinism in France, and had been systematized by Languet, resulted in the fragmentation of the country into its parts, depending on whether one adhered to Rome or followed Calvin. This could therefore never become a rule and standard for the Church of Christ in the various countries.

—- Page 721 —-

705 THE THIRD STAGE IN ENGLAND AND AMERICA.
§58. But when now on the Continent the outcome was disappointing and the action could not go any further, it has come to such a powerful seizure of the spirits in England that in fact not on the Continent, but first in England (apart from Scotland and Ireland) the political question, insofar as it was connected with the Reformation principles, has come to a decision. This did not take place in Germany, because the Government there also held the reins in the Reformed States. Nor in Belgium, which again became entirely Roman Catholic. And not in France, which, with the exception of the Cévennes, saw the power of Calvinism wither away as early as August 23, 1572. It has already been pointed out how even the Synod of Dordrecht suffered under the government’s bond, and how thereafter every national synod was even prevented by the government. Our country did not even dare to take the initiative for a world synod. But this was the least evil. Much worse was that here at home the entire people was also drawn into the one Explanation.

—- Page 722 —-

The Church sought to take control, so that soon the vast majority of the members of that Church could not be said to agree with the confession of the Church. Discipline, except in the case of flagrant moral transgressions, completely collapsed. Everything was called Reformed, but it was far from it; and then, when state coercion was finally abandoned at the end of the 18th century, the 19th century quickly showed what a lie all these years had been, as applied to this entire ecclesiastical institution. The cause of this lay in nothing other than the unnatural connection between the ecclesiastical and the political life, and it followed from this naturally that neither the ecclesiastical nor the political institution could come into its own, until one had overcome the primordial fault of this contra-natural connection between State and Church. This, however, was unthinkable without a mighty upheaval. It had to lead to a powerful revolution. And in England, where the separation of these two institutions was finally, at least in part, accomplished, the principled struggle between the Absolutism of the Stuarts and the freer life-consciousness of the Puritans then ignited, until finally the liberation of both institutions was achieved. Only then did the State and the Church each acquire the fundamental position due to them. In Israel of old, the foundation of the structure of the State could be no other than the foundation of the spiritual House. But this applied exclusively to Israel, because it occupied a completely exceptional position among the nations as the people of the Special Revelation. This exception, however, existed for no other people outside of Israel, and the almost incredible mistake that has been made since 325 consisted in this, that this completely exceptional position of Israel was then applied to all Christian peoples. For this was grasping at the impossible. Whoever was not a member of the State, of the national people, of the popular community, could not be excluded either by Baptism or by any confession concerning invisible things. And conversely, the question of who were not members of an ecclesiastical institution could never be answered otherwise than by personal profession. Baptism could serve as an introductory rite, and with this introduction into the ecclesiastical life one could be content until the age of majority; but at a more advanced age, the ecclesiastical institution always had to demand...

—- Page 723 —-

DUTCH CHARACTER. The confession of faith can never give the decision as to belonging to the nation. The solidarity of the children of one nation with the political people as a whole is based on birth or naturalization; that of the children of the Father who is in heaven cannot be derived from national affiliation, but must come from the confession of faith. Now, from the confusion of these two completely distinct spheres, all previous confusion had arisen. These two had to be disentangled again. Political life sprang from a completely different root than ecclesiastical life. And therefore a bond could and had to be laid between the two, but that bond had to do full justice to the independence and the otherness of the peculiar existence of both. This, however, did not take place among us, nor in Germany. It was only in the third period of Calvinism that this came about, and the English people, thanks to their strong individualistic national character, have been the means in God’s hand to bring out, though through a terrible struggle, at least for a part of the people, the beautiful fruit that Calvinism had always promised, but which had not yet come to full bloom.

§59. Dutch Character. Especially Dr. Weingarten has devoted his attention in his excellent historical overview to the two-fold form in which the various Puritan groups successively appeared, first ecclesiastically and politically together, and then, with the abandonment of the political element, only religiously. He gives this separately for the Anabaptists in his VI chapter, p. 157, for the Independents in the VII chapter, p. 159 ff., and for the Quakers in his X chapter, p. 243 ff.; I have already dealt with the Presbyterians. It was entirely natural for all these groups that they successively began by taking a position of their own, both in the ecclesiastical and political field. Hence a boundless confusion for a time, but it could not last long, or the untenable nature of such an approach would become evident, and then group after group withdrew to the religious terrain, and from the consciousness of their own movement the political striving has arisen to completely separate Church and State.

—- Page 724 —-

Not only did they seek to Calvinize, but they also sought to establish a system in the political sphere that would give full autonomy to all groups. However, this could not lead to the desired conclusion in England itself. The Episcopal Church here continued to maintain its overwhelming influence, and the Monarchy, after the Cromwell period, raised its head again with renewed vigor. Thus, there can be no question that even in England’s state life, Calvinism carried out its fundamental principles. While the Episcopal Church was Calvinistic in its confessional sense, this Confession was embedded in a worship service that did not correspond to it, and which has now even led to Puseyism and High Church. And while among the Non-Conformists there were democratic sympathies, but after the appearance of William of Orange, they almost all rallied to the Whigs. As trendsetters, they could not be prominent in the political arena. And this all the less because in England the local and provincial administrations had not developed. The local and provincial administrations were entirely subordinate to the predominant and all-controlling power concentrated in Parliament. Many of the Non-Conformists joined in this, and gradually lost their enthusiasm for their original ideas, and the more resolute Puritans no longer felt at home in England, and went, partly over the Netherlands, to America, to found there the free Colonies out of which, before the end of the 18th century, the mighty republic of the United States would be born. Now they organized their community life not according to the English, but according to the Old Dutch model. The Netherlands had known the great importance of the free, autonomous urban life since the 13th century. Parliamentary power had never developed among us to this extent. These forms of political association fitted entirely with the fundamental idea of Presbyterianism and Puritanism in general. The so-called Pilgrim Fathers therefore initially found refuge in our cities, and it was from Holland that they crossed over to the New World in 1620. Douglas Campbell, in his Puritans in England, Holland and America, London 1892, two volumes, has conclusively demonstrated that the Pilgrim Fathers and other Dissenters who later settled in America brought with them ideas from our lands that they incorporated into their American state...

—- Page 725 —-

THE REVIVAL OF THE PROCESS IN AMERICA.709 life would try to embody. Especially in the first part, pp. 418–467, he has explained the political affinity between the Dutch and the American colonies.

§60. The Revival of the Process in America. So what had come about here in the 16th century, was then in the 17th century brought over to America in a more developed form. And it was only under Washington in the 18th century that it could continue in its own form. Here, the political impact of Calvinism had been halted since 1627. Of a more democratic development of the people, there was soon no longer any question among us. The Regent rule began to take root; and when the Sans-culottes brought the French Revolutionary Democracy here, it was the ideas of Jean-Jacques Rousseau that tried to crush Calvinism here. Among us, Calvinism only resumed its course in the days of Groen van Prinsterer. In America, on the other hand, a religious movement has taken control of the course of events since 1620, which soon brought the democratic principle of Calvinism to full bloom. Undoubtedly, there was initially a degree of difference between the arrived Puritans, who were mostly Presbyterians, and the Pilgrims, but as Campbell as well as Ezra Hoyt Byington, in his The Puritan in England and New-England, London 1896 p. 41, point out, the democratic basic tendency of both striving ultimately brought them to unity, and this democratic intent was derived by both from Calvinism. Campbell had argued in Part II pp. 10–12 that Calvinism is democracy in its teaching. Byington joins him in this and expresses himself in this way: “Whether we agree with this or not, it is historically certain that the Calvinists have always and everywhere stood up for the rights of the people against the arbitrary power of the government.” Froude, Bancroft, Tiskee and others unconditionally join in this, and Mr. J.R. Green, History of the English people 1873 (III 45) not unjustly expresses himself in this way: “The simple man of the land, as soon as he felt called by God, was raised to the dignity of a king and priest.”

—- Page 726 —-

The Calvinists were aware of a power that was stronger than the might of a King. In the mighty elevation of the great multitude, which was the outflow and fruit of the Calvinistic Confession of grace and election, lay the germ from which the later conviction about the equality of humanity has arisen, “p. 41. Rev. Drysdale has only briefly touched upon the further course of Presbyterianism in America in his: History of the Presbyterians in England, but he too is convinced that the democratic tendency of Calvinism is beyond doubt (p. 242). The so-called Lex Regia, which, incorporated by Ulpian into Roman law, asserted the proposition that the people originally had control over all political power, but had then once and for all transferred authority to the Monarchy—a proposition even accepted by Thomas Aquinas—has been consistently rejected in principle by the Puritans, Presbyterians, and Pilgrims in America. In the Church of Christ, all were brothers, and since their Church had been the foundation for their state organization, they could not but refrain from the mutual equality of citizens. To be sure, the Church, in its manifold appearance, was now completely detached from the State, all Churches stood on an equal footing, and each Church lived from its own means, so that not only in the realm of conscience, but also in the ecclesiastical domain, the most complete freedom was respected; but the religious fundamental tone still prevailed throughout the entire American state life; and not from Paris, but from Geneva and from Amsterdam, the free people’s life has emerged, which now still flourishes in America. It was the immigrant settlers who founded all the States in America. These immigrants belonged almost entirely to the respectable citizenry. There was no talk of a lower class among them. So it could not be otherwise, or they all felt themselves as equal peers. The aristocratic idea of a richer or ennobled class that would dominate the others could not arise. And the same was true of the monarchical idea. Until the War of Independence, the King of England remained their natural head, but under his scepter they organized themselves in America on a completely free footing. And when it succeeded for Washington and his men to conquer the complete independence of America in the War of Independence, every reason for a monarch in America disappeared.

—- Page 727 —-

The Agreement. To establish a new Monarchy. There was no foreign enemy to be feared, there was no wild undercurrent of the population that had to be held down by force, and no princely house could assert any rights or claims. For this reason, the Republic became the self-evident form of government, and when Hamilton and Jefferson debated whether this form of government would be grounded in the religious ideas of Calvin or the revolutionary principles of Rousseau, by far the majority of the States gained the victory for Hamilton’s system.

§61. The Agreement. This principle had already come to a well-defined expression in England itself in the so-called Agreement of the People, which was presented to Parliament on January 20, 1648 by the Levellers, under the leadership of John Lilburne. The thrust of this was to give effect to the conviction that there should be a pactum among the sons of the same people, by which they bound themselves to set limits to the absolute power of the government. This could not, it was argued, be left to Parliament, since the law could diminish the powers of Parliament. It had therefore to be laid down in a pactum, which remained independent of Parliament and also governed Parliament. Here too, there was a Sphere Sovereignty for the people. They went so far that the pactum already strongly resembled what we now call the Constitution, and this Constitution had to regulate the structure of Parliament, with one House; the structure of the executive power; to limit the duration of the parliamentary sessions to only 2 years; equal, proportional distribution of the electoral districts; universal suffrage for all adult men, except for domestic servants and state pensioners; freedom of religion; complete separation of Church and State; setting limits to the legislative power through a kind of referendum; in short, this Agreement contained a complete program for the establishment of the Rights of the People, more than a century before the Jacobins came to power in Paris. “But precisely among the Puritans and Pilgrims, this democratic striving had a much more religious character.” Already on the Mayflower, which sailed in 1620, the Pilgrims established their constitutional order, which began as follows: “In the name of God. Amen! We, the undersigned, subjects of His Majesty, our Sovereign Lord King James, by the grace of God King of Great Britain, etc., have undertaken this journey for the glory of God, the spread of the Christian faith and the honor of King and Country, and now unite, mutually and reciprocally, by this present contract, before God and in our own presence, to establish good order and to achieve the stated objectives. By virtue of this our covenant, we shall now enact and decree all such good and fair laws, and all related ordinances, institutions and offices, as shall appear necessary for the well-being of the Colony, and we promise to submit to and obey them.”

—- Page 728 —-

This document was signed by all on “11 November Anno Domini 1620” (see B.P. Poore, The Federal and State Constitutions of the United States I p. 931).

§62. Washington versus Paris. The distinct groups thus formed then united in Hartford in 1839, on January 11, into a league and also there proceeded from the premise that the people had the decision over their own destiny. Already then it was what later became more officially formulated: “We therefore unite and bind ourselves to be as one State or Commonwealth, and enter into this covenant and confederation, for ourselves and our successors, and for all who may later join us as emigrants” (see Poore I p. 249). About what in the old world had been built on a different basis, one did not express oneself, but in the new world, where one was lord and master over the organization that would be given to the political community, it could not be conceived otherwise than that the Colonists were fully empowered to proceed according to their own discretion. And following this, it could not be otherwise than that they were led by their Calvinist basic thought in the religious sphere to choose the democratic form of government. God the Sovereign, the people the sovereign in their own sphere - this was the principle that triumphed.

—- Page 729 —-

WASHINGTON VERSUS PARIS.
But it was God who granted the people the authority to choose their own government and to organize their national life according to their own discretion. In nothing, as far as popular freedoms and popular rights are concerned, did the state life established by these Calvinist emigrants in America fall short of the strictest Jacobin proclamations of Paris. Rather, the structure that the Calvinists in America undertook had a much more solid character; the consequence of which was that, while in Paris Jacobinism was followed by the domination of Napoleon, and after Napoleon the Bourbon dynasty once again imposed its scepter on France, in North America the development of the state life on a Christian-democratic foundation has proceeded uninterrupted and is still in full bloom. What hindered Calvinism in the first period of its development was the then still widely prevalent belief that only one form of church could be valid for all Christendom, and that the government was called upon to establish, protect, and defend this one Church in all lands, if necessary by the sword. Hence, even in our own country, such untenable concepts continued to prevail in official life until 1795. Yet this did not prevent the impulse from Calvin from leading, by logical consequence, to the separation of Church and State and to a state order grounded in Common Grace. The germ for this development lay, first, in his emphasis on the doctrine of election; secondly, in his grounding of all of life on Holy Scripture, in connection with the testimonium Spiritus Sancti, and the resulting universal priesthood of believers; and finally, sealed by the declaration that every believer was called to refuse obedience whenever the government commanded something against God’s commandment or honor, and that the inferior magistrates were even obliged to resist by force a government that violated the people’s conscience and, if necessary, not to submit to its authority. From this Calvinist conviction, the rejection of the Spanish yoke and the abjuration of Philip II in our country originated, while at the same time the resistance of the Huguenots in France, which threatened to fragment the country, marked the second period of Calvinism, which found its political thinker in Hubert Languet. Now, when this resistance in France was broken after 1572, and could no longer manifest itself in a political form, it sought to express itself in the realm of popular morality, custom, and propriety.

—- Page 730 —-

The final development of Calvinism in this period, when it was partly stifled in its political development here by excessive spiritual individualism and partly by the overwhelming power of the Episcopal element of the people, so that it could no longer come to independent development, has finally, through emigration, attained in America both ecclesiastically and politically that full unfolding which has realized, on a Christian-democratic basis, the ideal of the free Church in the free State, as far as this could be achieved in this dispensation of sin and misery.

§63. To place this outcome on a par with what the French Revolution of 1789 intended in its bloody course is historical falsification. I have already appealed, in confirmation of this judgment, to Hamilton, who joined in a principled struggle with Jefferson precisely on this point. Von Holtz, in his Verfassung und Demokratie der Vereinigten Staaten von America, Dusseldorf 1873, p. 96, rightly wrote: “It would be folly to say that the writings of Rousseau had any influence on the development in America.” What Hamilton himself declared proves the correctness of this opinion. For he expressed himself in a way that could not be stronger when he testified: “The French Revolution is no more like the American Revolution than a forgotten woman in a French novel is like a Puritan matron in New England.” Even more extensively, he came out for this in a letter that Henry Cabot Lodge included in his Alexander Hamilton, Boston 1892 p. 256, and in which we read: “When I openly profess the doctrine of Atheism in the Paris Convention, and hear it greeted with loud applause; when I see the sword of fanaticism drawn to impose a political creed on the people, while those same citizens are invited to submit to the French armies, which yet present themselves as the saviors of Liberty; and when I see the rapacious hand stretched out to overthrow and despoil the monuments of religious worship, then I feel that I can be happy in believing that there is no real resemblance between the problem that was decided in France, and the problem that was decided in America.”

—- Page 731 —-

FURTHER ELABORATION OF THE CONTRAST. Just think of the notorious fact that in America, at a meeting of the Convention, Franklin, when things were not going well, proposed to the assembly to stand up and ask for the wise help of God in prayer, and compare that with a Jacobin Convention in Paris, and any equation of the two aspirations becomes absurd. It may also be added that Edmund Burke did not judge otherwise when he compared the French Revolution of 1789 with the “Glorious Revolution” under William of Orange in 1688, and thus concluded: “Our Revolution and the French are just the reverse of each other, are absolutely the reverse of each other, so much so that they stand right opposite to each other in every particular and in the whole spirit that animated them both” (Works, Mc. Lean, London, Vol. III p. 25). May this brief indication suffice, while I further refer to my Calvinism, Six Stone-lectures, Edinburgh, T. Clarck 1899, pp. 109–115. With Dr. Julius Hatschek, Allgemeines Staatsrecht, Leipzig 1909, Vol. II p. 1–12, the contrast is taken less sharply, since the writer himself stands on Rousseau’s side, but even he acknowledges, in any case, that the true popular freedoms did not first come to us from Paris, but had already been granted to us by Calvinism, partly in England, partly in America, long before the French Revolution. Ezra Hoyt Byington, Douglas Campbell, Otto Gierke, in his Joh. Althusius, Weingarten, in his Die Revolutionskirchen in England, Drysdale in his History of the Presbyterians, Dr. Troeltsch whom I quoted extensively, and with Ranke almost every historian of significance, judged no differently. There is no more eloquent plea for the freedoms and rights of the people, as honored as a gift of God, in the history, than that of Calvinism in the succession of its three periods; and it is mainly the sad fact that in our country since 1627 Calvinism had to give way politically, and the Remonstrant politics triumphed, that has become more than anything else the cause of the suppression of the principles of political freedom in our old Republic.

—- Page 732 —-

716 CALVINISTIC.
§64. Monarchy and Republic. The Calvinist struggle was never for or against the monarchy, the struggle was for Christian democracy, not in the current sense as if the lower classes should set the tone, nor as if the demos should have the government in hand, but in the only correct meaning, that the entire demos, the whole people in all its parts, should be able to assert their rights in the government of the country. This could be done in a monarchy, provided it was constitutionally organized; it could be done in a republic, provided the nobles and the wealthy did not come to sole dominion, as happened with us under the Regents; and the only condition was that it should be a Polyarchy, so that the various members of the organic people’s whole, each in their own way, would ensure the good progress of the life of the State. Now, under our Queen, we have come closer to this highest political fundamental idea of Calvinism than we were once under the Regents, even though the form of government was then a Commonwealth. With Thomas Aquinas, it cannot be denied that he favored the lex Regia, with Kleist-Retzow, Von Gerlach and Stahl, yes even with Haller, we are in the most decisive sense Antirevolutionary, but insofar as the Antirevolutionary standpoint in a general sense, even a rather rigid and steep, yes almost absolute Monarchism allows, two currents must be admitted in the Antirevolutionary standpoint: the one absolutely Monarchical, the other Christian-democratic in nature; and where these two currents diverge, our history calls upon us to follow in the footsteps of Groen van Prinsterer, to seek the guarantee of our constitutional freedoms not in Paris, but in Calvinism, and in that sense I also plead for Christian Democracy.
§65. Conclusion. The conclusion of this § can therefore be brief, and it is that the impulse from Calvin has led, through a development of three periods, with logical consequence, to Christian Democracy. That Calvin remained captive in the idea of an instituted world church was a legacy of the Middle Ages, which at the time still dominated almost all minds. That he still considered the government called upon to defend and support the Church of Christ with the sword, and thus came to the stake of Michael Servetus, logically followed from the same tradition. For if the Church of Christ is not only a world organization, but also a Cosmic institution, then the national government is framed in the universal Church government. These two propositions were not from Calvin, but Calvin took them over. On the contrary, Calvin’s own confession was the position of the elect believers in Christ’s Church, the Testimonium Spiritus Sancti that sealed them, and therefore the rejection of all hierarchy in the Church, both the papal and the episcopal, as well as the dominion of the notables. As long as Church and State remained on this standpoint, this naturally led to the honoring of the people’s freedoms and rights in the political sphere. Hence the constant calls for the consensus populi for the government, his pleas for the election of high officials by the people, and his preference for the republican form of government. All authority flowed from the Sovereignty of God, but this Sovereignty of God descended among men in three forms: in the form of government on the authorities in the form of rights and freedoms on the people, and in spiritual form on the believers in the Church. In this way, Calvin gave a peremptory impulse to a movement that had to result in the separation of Church and State, the democratization of the form of government, and the resting of the government on Common Grace.

—- Page 733 —-

MONARCHY AND REPUBLIC. - CONCLUSION. 717 In the second period, which first entered with the French religious wars, after Calvin had already died (in 1564), the unity of Church and State was driven to the extreme and the country was torn into fragments, in order to maintain that unity by allowing the political to be completely dominated by the spiritual motive. Hubert Languet, with Hottomann, gave for this second period the system that, by laying the starting point for all power in the believers, was to prepare the dominion of the Saints in England. The two elements of Church and State, which history was already busy tearing apart, therefore remained clamped together with Languet and his kindred spirits, and hence his fault that he sought the basis of authority in the people, instead of in the Sovereignty of God over the people.

—- Page 734 —-

718 This error had to lead the third period of Calvinism to swing over to the other side, and this is what happened in the English civil wars. Yet this latter process could not be brought to a final decision in England. The godless spirit that at the time prevailed among the English notables, and was then covered up, if not by Episcopalianism, then at least winked at, gave the Puritanism an outlet that has indeed worked through, but did not fundamentally transform the English state life. The Puritans, both Presbyterians and Independents and Quakers, ultimately got the short end of the stick in London, and although they made a tolerance with William of Orange, and in connection with this the freedom of public worship, yet the civil disabilities remained in force. Calvinistic in the letter of the Confession, the Church of England ceased to be. Calvinistic in its intentions and aspirations. Calvinism maintained itself in Presbyterian form in Scotland and in Independent form in England, but the government of the country was no longer dominated by the Calvinistic idea from then on. This ultimately led to emigration to America, and only when in this new part of the world new states were founded in colonial form, was it in these newly built states that Calvinism, or rather Neo-Calvinism, came to its full unfolding. All politics was now, in conformity with Common Grace, bound to Almighty God, so that the government still proclaims days of prayer, the separation of Church and State is completed, and the Church lives in all its institutional variations absolutely free, provided it lives from its own means and maintains itself through spiritual excellence. Likewise, the popular rights and popular freedoms in a political respect were respected and maintained by the spiritual children of Hamilton, in the widest sense, but in such a way that the ochlocratic impropriety was tempered and excluded by the co-decision of the Senate, which is elected by the colleges of the States; and furthermore by a ministry that is quite different from that in Europe, leaving the responsibility to the President and not even appearing before Parliament. Of course, this outcome has deviated far from the spiritual father after whom it calls itself; but to judge whether this fruit has nevertheless come forth from him, or has been grafted in from outside as a foreign stock, you only have to ask what the elements of Calvin’s system are that he took over from the past, and which were the others that he introduced as new in his time. And since Christian democracy was completely foreign to this inherited past, and on the other hand has logically followed from what Calvin wove new into the old, it remains, as Dr. Troeltsch has so rightly recognized, to the honor of Calvinism that it has brought us the idea of the free citizen state, with in that citizen state the free Church of Christ.

—- Page 735 —-

CONCLUSION. 719 What our fathers after 1627 neglected and the Regents here in the country have corrupted, has only come to new life among us in the 19th century. Groen van Prinsterer called for it, thanks to his studies in the Archives of the House of Orange. His saying: “I am a Calvinist” has found resonance, and the revival of the Reformed confession in the ecclesiastical sphere has naturally brought us a revival of Calvinism in the state idea as well. Whether this renewal will hold its ground will depend on our faithfulness!

—- Page 737 —-

REGISTER
